issue_title: url concatenation with a prefix doesn't account for the prefix in the right hand side
issue_body:
If, for example, a gabbi-run is done with a prefix in the url:

```
gabbi-run http://example.com/prefix
```

against a service which produces output that has links which are absolute urls:

```json
{"links": [{"rel": "self", "href": "/prefix/somewhere"}]}
```

following that link:

```yaml
# assume a prior request
tests:
  name: get self
  GET: $RESPONSE['$.links[?rel = "self'].href']
```

will result in a 404 because the generated url will be GET `http://example.com/prefix/prefix/somewhere`.

This can be worked around in existing tests by using the prefix in the tests, not in the host info given to gabbi.

This is not, however, a good fix because the point of the prefix is to allow testing of services that might be mounted in different "sub directories".

issue_title: API: from_randomness should support ULID/Randomness objects
issue_body:
The `from_randomness` function in `ulid/api.py` supports creating ULID instances with a randomness value from a given value. In addition to the currently supports types, it should also support  `Randomness` and `ULID` types as well.

* When the given value is a `Randomness`, a straight copy of all bytes should suffice.
* When the given value is a `ULID`, a straight copy of the last 10 bytes should suffice.

issue_body:
_From [crh0...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/108345305523799306459/) on November 09, 2010 15:00:05_

Is anyone else having this problem?

\>>> t = TCP(seq=2857195823, ack=1820174829, flags=16, dport=80, sport=42191)
\>>> i = IP(src='\xc0\xa8\x01d', dst='\xd0Rd\xaa', len=40, p=6, data=t)
\>>> e = Ethernet( dst = '\x00\x22\x6b\x5f\x71\xbd', src = '\x00\x1f\x5b\xc5\x01\xc6', data = i )

issue_title: Usage for System Admins
issue_body:
I'm trying to figure out a sane way to allow sys admins to install just the library dependencies of an executable into the build tree, link to the libs statically and then install just the executable into a system path. In this scenario the install prefix for the dependencies is different than the install prefix for the executable.  The following does the trick but is a little verbose. 
```
mkdir build
cd build
cget install --file ../requirements.txt --prefix .
cmake -DCMAKE_PREFIX_PATH=`pwd` ..
make
sudo make install # Installs executable to /usr/local
```
Is there a better way of doing this or are there any ideas on how cget could support this in the future?

Also, is setting CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH to the cget prefix path the recommended way of using cget with IDEs like CLion?

issue_title: Add an option to control recursive search & parsing of source files.
issue_body:
As we started to discuss in issue #61 , FoBiS.py could be made more robust by enhancing user control (without losing the automated features that make FoBiS.py attractive in the first place). An easy step would be to include a `directories=` parameter. For the _cumbersome_ example of the [README](https://github.com/szaghi/FoBiS/blob/4463152649d604da1290f933cd4ee98b068e45ee/README.md#ok-what-can-fobispy-do-we-are-poor-fortran-people-we-do-not-understand-you), it would look like:

    directories = ["src/", "src/nested-1", "src/nested-1/nested-2"] 

(this is for the fobos config file, but there would be an equivalent command-line parameter `--directories src src/nested-1 src/nested-1/nested-2`). This parameter would indicate FoBiS.py which directories to search for source files, in a __non-recursive__ way.

@szaghi suggested that, if the only effect of a `directories=` parameter would be to limit the recursive search to certain directories, the parameter could be named `only=`. I think however this would be ambiguous, and an __independent__ specification of `directories=` and `recursive=` is preferable. For example, `recursive=True` and `directories=["src1", "src2"]` is a perfectly valid use case, useful when only src1 and src2 sub-directories of the current location should be searched for and parsed (out of the possibly many directories present here (e.g. "obj", "src1-old", "other-project"), but still in a recursive way. On the other hand, the above _cumbersome_ example with `directories=` specification would be associated with `recursive=False`.

I would make the further _strong_ suggestion that FoBiS.py becomes __non-recursive by default__, unless an explicit `-r` or `--recursive` flag is passed (`recursive=True` in fobos), **[like most unix commands](http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/82485/when-wouldnt-one-want-cp-to-be-recursive)** that actually process file contents (e.g. grep, cp, ctags). Adding a `-r` is really a minor additional burden for the user, and is much more compatible with the unix command-line philosophy.

As a summary:

- the current behavior would be obtained by passing a `-r` or `--recursive` flag (equivalently, recursive = True in fobos file, by default, False)
- if none of `--recursive` or `--directories` is specified, only files in the current folder are searched for or parsed (i.e. default is `recursive=False` and `directories=["./"]`).
- the preferred behavior in a `fobos` config file would be to specify `directories=`.

issue_title: Vector valued functions
issue_body:
In order to allow vector valued functions as models, a nice way to define such functions is needed. Unless I am mistaken, sympy does not have a standard way of doing this (yet?).

What would be the best way of doing this? I propose the following:
- list of functions
```python
a = [x**2, y**2, 2 * x * y]
```
Or a tuple could be used if it makes more sense for it to be immutable.
- vector object
```python
a = vector(x**2, y**2, 2 * x * y)
```
- overload an otherwise unused operator, e.g.
```python
a = x**2 & y**2 & 2 * x * y
```
Other operators that could be used are ```|``` and ```^``` but ```and``` makes more sense to me.

issue_title: Fitting an ODEModel raises 'ODEModel' has no attribute 'numerical_jacobian'
issue_body:
Hi,

If I try to fit this ODEModel to my data it raises `AttributeError: 'ODEModel' object has no attribute 'numerical_jacobian'` on symfit versions 0.3.5 and 0.3.6.dev93; python version 3.5.2.
@tBuLi I think this a high priority issue, and should be fixed asap, immediately followed by the release of 0.3.6. Let me know if I can help.

Here's a complete code example (excluding imports) to reproduce the error.

```python
data = np.array([[  0.,  92.,   0.,   0.],
       [  1.,  57.,   2.,  38.],
       [  2.,  24.,   4.,  72.],
       [  3.,  13.,   5.,  82.],
       [  4.,  11.,   5.,  84.],
       [  7.,   7.,   7.,  86.]])
t_data, food_data, hex_data, oct_data = data.T

def sigmoid(t, t0=0, k=1, N=1):
    return N/(1+symfit.exp(-k*(t-t0)))

t = symfit.Variable()
Cfood, Chex, Coct = symfit.variables('Cfood, Chex, Coct')
t0hex, t0oct = symfit.parameters('t0hex, t0oct')
t0hex.min = np.min(t_data)
t0oct.min = np.min(t_data)
t0hex.max = np.max(t_data)
t0oct.max = np.max(t_data)
khex, koct = symfit.parameters('khex, koct')
khex.min = 0
koct.min = 0
khex.max = 150e-5
koct.max = 150e-5

dhex = sigmoid(t, t0hex, 100, 1) * khex * Chex * Cfood**2
doct = sigmoid(t, t0oct, 100, 1) * koct * Coct * Cfood**2

model = {
        symfit.D(Chex, t): dhex,
        symfit.D(Coct, t): doct,
        symfit.D(Cfood, t): - dhex - doct,
}
model = symfit.ODEModel(model, initial={Chex: 0.1, Coct: 0.1, Cfood: 99.8, t: 0})

fit = symfit.Fit(model, t=t_data, Cfood=food_data, Chex=hex_data, Coct=oct_data)
result = fit.execute()
print(result)
```

issue_title: Consider using something other than `zenpy` for the client in the examples
issue_body:
Currently the example in the README is something like:

```python
from zenpy import Zenpy
zenpy = Zenpy(**credentials)
# ... later down
zenpy.users.update(...)
```

However I'm using like this:

```python
import zenpy
client = zenpy.Zenpy(**credentials)
```

So when I've read `zenpy.users.update`, I just assumed that the `zenpy` module had a `users` field on it (or perhaps there was a `zenpy.users` module). To make it more clear, perhaps change `zenpy = Zenpy(**credentials)` into `client = Zenpy(**credentials)` (or `zenpy_client`).

issue_title: Snap package for howdoi
issue_body:
I found howdoi useful and I used it to write an article on how to create a snap package,
https://blog.simos.info/how-to-create-a-snap-with-snapcraft-for-howdoi-cli-utilility-for-stackoverflow/

I created a package for howdoi and I uploaded to the Ubuntu Store. 
It can be installed in Ubuntu and other distributions that can run snaps, by running:

snap install howdoi

You may want to add the 'snapcraft.yaml' file (https://gist.github.com/simos/4d498bf773ab23827901c7c9a8015517) in the source directory of howdoi, so that users can also build the snap themselves.

I used the name 'howdoi' for the snap (instead of howdoi.simosx); if you would like to maintain the snap yourself instead, please tell me to transfer ownership of the snap.

issue_title: Since 97569e1, setUpClass is called once per test
issue_body:
Since 97569e1 `setUpClass`, `tearDownClass` `setUpModule` and `tearDownModule` get called once per unit test. This is due to running each suite's component individually, as the subprocess mode does.

According to the [unittest documentation](https://docs.python.org/2/library/unittest.html#class-and-module-fixtures) the order and number of times `setUpClass` and `tearDownClass` are called isn't strictly-speaking defined. It just so happens that by convention on unittest's own single-threaded mode that they only get called once, since the tests run in order. No guarantee is made about what happens when tests are run in a randomised order or in parallel.

Personally, I use those methods to do "expensive" set up and tear down where I know I can maintain the invariant between tests in `setUp` and `tearDown`. As such, the current behaviour makes my tests run a lot slower.

I would submit that the correct behaviour is to detect where tests are setting those methods and only divide up suites to the level where any of those methods are defined. So, for instance, if `setUpClass` was defined on a `TestCase`, then the entire `TestCase` should run in serial, but could be parallelized amongst other tests.

Here's some output to show the problem and a testcase:

from testtools import TestCase

    import sys


    class TestSetUpClass(TestCase):

        """Test set up class."""

        @classmethod
        def setUpClass(cls):
            sys.stderr.write("setUpClass\n")

        def test_one(self):
            """One"""
            sys.stderr.write("method one on two called\n")
            pass

        def test_two(self):
            """Two"""
            sys.stderr.write("method two on two called\n")
            pass

        def test_three(self):
            """Three"""
            sys.stderr.write("method three on two called\n")
            pass


    Green 1.11.0, Coverage 3.7.1, Python 2.7.9

    test.test_two.test_set_up_class
      TestSetUpClass
        OnesetUpClass
    method one on two called
    setUpClass
    method three on two called
    .   One
    .   Three
        TwosetUpClass
    method two on two called
    .   Two

    Ran 3 tests in 0.108s

    OK (passes=3)

issue_title: Speed up unit tests for subdivision checks
issue_body:
Running `py.test` with durations shows the culprits

```
$ git log -1 --pretty=%H
8ec6fdfd48d8a046ebc5b9390f9c000b577ea453
$ tox -e py27 -- --durations=7
...
1.53s call     tests/test_surface.py::TestSurface::test_subdivide_on_the_fly
0.97s call     tests/test_surface.py::TestSurface::test_subdivide_quartic_check_evaluate
0.28s call     tests/test_surface.py::TestSurface::test_subdivide_cubic_check_evaluate
0.26s call     tests/test_surface.py::TestSurface::test_subdivide_quadratic_check_evaluate
0.26s call     tests/test_surface.py::TestSurface::test_subdivide_line_check_evaluate
0.18s call     tests/test__base.py::TestBase::test___eq__
0.01s call     tests/test_surface.py::TestSurface::test__compute_valid_invalid_linear
...
```

## Main Culprit

`Surface.evaluate_multi()` is slow, in particular, when we use 561 points within the reference triangle

----

NOTE/Post-Script; The sixth one is a "lie", it reflects the start-up cost of importing `mock` for the first time:

```
$ python2.7 -c 'from time import time; s = time(); import base64; print(time() - s)'
0.00239682197571
$ python2.7 -c 'from time import time; s = time(); import mock; print(time() - s)'
0.33260512352
$ python3.5 -c 'from time import time; s = time(); import base64; print(time() - s)'
0.0032052993774414062
$ python3.5 -c 'from time import time; s = time(); import mock; print(time() - s)'
0.2292490005493164
```

Why does `test___eq__` bear this cost? Because it is the first in alphabetical order of all the tests and because all the tests import `mock` at runtime rather than at import time.

issue_title: handling wrong type tag values
issue_body:
###About

if one wants to edit an existing exif, where some tag values are written in the wrong type and those tags are not edited, piexif will crash when the edited dictionary is attempted to be dumped back into bytes, to be written back to the images, since some tag values are in the wrong type(when packing to struct in _dump)

###Notes
piexif is great to edit or create new exif in an image, but we have issues where we often get images that have valuable information in exif tags we don't edit, but the information is stored in the wrong type, in cases like this, just loading exif from image and trying to _dump back will fail.

Please give us a suggestion on how to solve this rather than looping through all the tags and either removing the tags which have the value in the wrong type or trying to force the value in the right type.

I agree that the exif standard should be enforced and the type of each tag value is well specified, but still many cameras or people processing images will write the tag values in the wrong type and your _dump script can not seem to handle that, did you happen to experience similar issues yourself?

issue_title: JSGString incorrectly matches None
issue_body:
`JSGStringMeta` does a pattern match against a the string representation of the target instance.  If the target instance is None, the match is applied to the string "None".  None should either map to the empty string or not map at all

```python
from pyjsg.jsglib.jsg import JSGString, JSGPattern
class S(JSGString):
    pattern = JSGPattern(r'[a-zA-Z]+')
    
isinstance('abc', S)
   True
isinstance('abc1', S)
   False
isinstance('', S)
  False
isinstance(None, S)
   True   # <--- Wrong
```

issue_title: underlying error if raised while setting password in keyring is not raised.
issue_body:
In the below code if OSError is raised then actually reason for the error is never known.

keyring/backend.py:
    
    class _ExtensionKeyring(KeyringBackend):
        def set_password(self, service, username, password):
            """Overide the set_password() in KeyringBackend.
            """
        try:
            self.keyring_impl.password_set(service, username, password)
        except OSError:
            raise PasswordSetError()
    
So i think we should change   this to.
    
        try:
            self.keyring_impl.password_set(service, username, password)
        except OSError, e:
            raise PasswordSetError(e)
    
so that we will know whats the actually error is.


regards,

Moqtar

issue_title: converting a TCP packet with SAckOK option to a byte sequence fails
issue_body:
Here is a simple example that demonstrates this problem:

```
from scapy3k.all import *

# NOP option
packet = Ether()/IP()/TCP(options=[('NOP',None)])
print(packet[TCP].options) #this works
print(bytes(packet)) #this works

# SAckOK option
packet = Ether()/IP()/TCP(options=[('SAckOK',None)])
print(packet[TCP].options) #this works

```

This probably has something to do with the fact that the selective ACK has a length field of 2, but no data.

QMessageBox stops the asyncio event loop
issue_body:
Here is a simple test file : 

```python
import sys
import unittest
import os
import asyncio
import quamash
import logging
from PyQt5.QtWidgets import QDialog, QMessageBox
from PyQt5.QtCore import QLocale, Qt
from PyQt5.QtTest import QTest


class Dialog(QDialog):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()

    @asyncio.coroutine
    def message_box(self):
        result = QMessageBox.question(self, "Test", "Do I bug ?")
        return result


class TestBug(unittest.TestCase):
    def setUp(self):
        self.qapplication = quamash.QApplication(sys.argv)
        QLocale.setDefault(QLocale("en_GB"))
        self.lp = quamash.QEventLoop(self.qapplication)
        asyncio.set_event_loop(self.lp)

    def tearDown(self):
        try:
            self.lp.close()
        finally:
            asyncio.set_event_loop(None)

    def test_bug(self):
        dialog = Dialog()
        @asyncio.coroutine
        def exec_test():
            asyncio.async(dialog.message_box())
            yield from asyncio.sleep(3)
            for widget in quamash.QApplication.topLevelWidgets():
                if isinstance(widget, QMessageBox):
                    QTest.mouseClick(widget.button(QMessageBox.Yes), Qt.LeftButton)
            yield from asyncio.sleep(1)

        self.lp.run_until_complete(asyncio.wait_for(exec_test(), timeout=10))

if __name__ == '__main__':
    logging.basicConfig( stream=sys.stderr )
    logging.getLogger().setLevel( logging.DEBUG )
    unittest.main()
```

This outputs : 
```
/usr/bin/python3.4 /home/inso/bin/pycharm-4.5.4/helpers/pycharm/utrunner.py /home/inso/code/ucoin/cutecoin/src/cutecoin/tests/process_cfg_community/test_bug.py true
Testing started at 16:44 ...
Exception in callback Task._step()
handle: <Handle Task._step()>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/asyncio/events.py", line 120, in _run
    self._callback(*self._args)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/asyncio/tasks.py", line 282, in _step
    self.__class__._current_tasks.pop(self._loop)
KeyError: <QEventLoop running=True closed=False debug=False>

Error
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/inso/code/ucoin/cutecoin/src/cutecoin/tests/process_cfg_community/test_bug.py", line 47, in test_bug
    self.lp.run_until_complete(asyncio.wait_for(exec_test(), timeout=10))
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/quamash/__init__.py", line 291, in run_until_complete
    raise RuntimeError('Event loop stopped before Future completed.')
RuntimeError: Event loop stopped before Future completed.

Task was destroyed but it is pending!
task: <Task pending coro=<wait_for() done, defined at /usr/lib/python3.4/asyncio/tasks.py:340> wait_for=<Future pending cb=[Task._wakeup()]>>
Task was destroyed but it is pending!
task: <Task pending coro=<exec_test() running at /home/inso/code/ucoin/cutecoin/src/cutecoin/tests/process_cfg_community/test_bug.py:45> wait_for=<Future pending cb=[Task._wakeup()]> cb=[_release_waiter(<Future pendi...sk._wakeup()]>)() at /usr/lib/python3.4/asyncio/tasks.py:335]>

I think it's due to this behaviour of QMessageBoxes :

Currently it's possible to set parameters to be filtered of the request body using [`filter_post_data_parameters`](http://vcrpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/advanced.html#advanced-use-of-filter-headers-filter-query-parameters-and-filter-post-data-parameters).

It would be nice to support the same for PUT requests as well.

issue_title: Mellanox Switch.
issue_body:
Hi,

Can you please add Mellanox Switch to snmp_autodetect?

If you use  1.3.6.1.2.1.1.*1*.0 for Mellanox switch, you will receive
.1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0
= STRING: "*Onyx,MSN2410*,SWv3.6.6102"
But for other models you can get 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0 = STRING: "*Mellanox *
MSN2700,MLNX-OS,SWv3.6.4112".
you can do regex to find  *Onyx,MSN2410* or  *Mellanox *and use
.1.3.6.1.2.1.1.*1*.0
Alternatively , you can use  1.3.6.1.2.1.1.*2*.0 and find  .*33049 * at the
result to identify Mellanox switches.
It's up to you.

Thanks.

issue_title: Delay needed in check_enable_mode() for Aruba Wireless
issue_body:
I've been working on adding support for Aruba controllers.  I had an issue getting into enable mode.  All would work fine while debugging and stepping through the process by hand, however, when running the code, it always throws a "Failed to enter enable mode." exception.  After a little digging, it looks as though the check_enable_mode() would send '\n' and check for the prompt before the Aruba controller has time to actually return to the prompt.  The end result is that the previously mentioned exception is thrown.

Adding a sleep between sending the command and checking for the prompt(shown below) fixes everything but I don't know if this is the preferred way to solve this issue.  I've also attached a screenshot of the logon process for reference.  I'm more than happy to give any other info that may be helpful.  Any thoughts?

```
def check_enable_mode(self, check_string=''):
    """Check if in enable mode. Return boolean."""
    self.remote_conn.sendall('\n')
    time.sleep(self.global_delay_factor) 
    output = self.read_until_prompt()
    return check_string in output
```

![arubacontroller_login](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4991000/18397832/8641c07a-768f-11e6-8e62-8bf46dd41428.png)

issue_title: setpassword() fails incorrectly
issue_body:
We use the `zf.testrar()` to see if the user put the correct password for a job during downloading.
But for some archives the `zf.setpassword()` will fail with:
```
zf.setpassword(password)
*** BadRarFile: Bad count to header decrypt - wrong password?
```
Even though it's the correct password.

I can share a test RAR if you like?

issue_body:
nicely if server reports a newer version is available, or forcingly if server reports the client version is no longer accepted. See https://github.com/ornicar/lila/issues/1735

Needed to prevent 1.0.7 clients from sending borked analysis.

issue_title: macos package
`find_library` won't search `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` on Linux (need workaround)
issue_body: This is a problem that arises with this line ( https://github.com/pearu/pylibtiff/blob/master/libtiff/libtiff_ctypes.py#L41 ). On Mac, one can set `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH` and this has the expected behavior when using `find_library`. However, this is not the case on Linux with `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`. See this discussion about the issue in Python. ( https://bugs.python.org/issue2936 ) However, if `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` is set, this line ( https://github.com/pearu/pylibtiff/blob/master/libtiff/libtiff_ctypes.py#L43 ) will import the library given the right name (e.g. `libtiff.so` or `libtiff.dylib`) even though `find_library` fails.

issue_title: Advance Status Support
issue_body:
Currently it's very hard to get visibility into what the Forked Process is doing.
The only status available is from the service looking at the PID file via the **is_running()** function.

Imagine you need a service that has multiple threads, I want to expose how many threads are running by issuing the status call to main service status. 
Or that I want to expose 1min, 5min, 60min counter values for the service itself. 

Is there currently a way to do this? Am I missing something?

If not, is there a way to make the service listen on a socket for status reporting purposes.
Something like this: https://searchcode.com/codesearch/view/574564/

issue_title: [Feature request] Live capture to file only
issue_body:
Current LiveCapture interface allows capturing packets to file, but only in addition to capturing them through stdout. That might (or might not) affect performance.
As an alternative, users utilize subprocess to call dumpcap or equivalent by hand. That's tedious and not user friendly, as it requires creating a cmd line string, launching process and waiting for it to complete.
Hence it'd be welcome to provide the functionality through pyshark, which is AFAIK the closest Python package to wireshark.
The functionality would allow specifying that the capture should go straight to file, bypassing stdout altogether. It'd possibly be limited (no packets count, no processing), but will give a convenient way of launching dumpcap (or equivalent).

In short, the motivation for this change would be twofold:
- avoid potential performance issues when using stdout for passing captured packets
- provide convenient abstraction over dumpcap execution

Best regards,
Robert

issue_title: Ability to use wal -f without specifying a wallpaper
issue_body:
I'm using this wallpaper I made, based off of the Solarized Dark colorscheme:

![solarized_dark](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17169802/37805134-4180de32-2e06-11e8-8e27-0d235561094d.png)

`wal -i` doesn't produce very good output, so instead I've taken to manually inputting the Solarized Dark colors into my own .json file, like so: https://pastebin.com/SQLXmkmS

I would like to be able to use wal to set up my wallpaper and colors without having wal extract them from the wallpaper itself. Basically, this is my desired workflow:

1. Create a colorscheme .json file
2. Do something like `wal -f ~/mycolorscheme.json -i ~/mywallpaper.png` to set the colors and wallpaper
3. `wal -R` on reboot to reload that colorscheme and wallpaper

Currently however:

1. `wal -f` and `wal -i` are mutually exclusive
2. Colorscheme .json files must have a "wallpaper" field, or else wal crashes
3. `wal -R` only applies the colorscheme that wal extracted from the image, it ignores any file passed in with `-f`.

issue_title: PEP-0527 - no longer provide .exe files
issue_body:
For Windows we currently upload and provide `.exe` and `.wheel` files on each release. PEP-527 deprecates `.exe` files (aka: `python setup.py bdist_wininst`) and AFAIU the new PYPI infrastructure will even prevent uploading / hosting them:
https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0527/
This is good news as we'll have to upload 10 files (+1, the tar.gz) per release instead of 20.

issue_title: Argument to on_callback_query() is not a Message
issue_body:
While debugging a problem I had with the way I handled the callback query in my bot I discovered that the actual argument passed to the `on_callback_quey(self, msg)` named msg was in fact... not a Telegram Message 😱.

Two clues led me to this conclusion. First when trying to convert to a namedtuple `Message` I get a warning:
```python
>>> Message(msg)
/usr/lib/python3.5/site-packages/telepot/namedtuple.py:48: UserWarning: Unexpected fields: data, id, inline_message_id, chat_instance
Bot API seems to have added new fields to the returned data. This version of namedtuple is not able to capture them.

Please upgrade telepot by:
  sudo pip install telepot --upgrade

If you still see this message after upgrade, that means I am still working to bring the code up-to-date. Please try upgrade again a few days later. In the meantime, you can access the new fields the old-fashioned way, through the raw dictionary.
  warnings.warn(s, UserWarning)
```

Ok at first I did not paid too much attention to the error since Telegram has been updated yesterday with some new types related to games.

Second clue: for debugging purpose I always log a pprint of each message my bot receive. This one was no exception:
```python
>>> pprint(msg)
{'chat_instance': '-6597979017591450366',
 'data': '1 0',
 'from': {'first_name': 'James',
          'id': 1007,
          'last_name': 'Bond',
          'username': 'jamesbond007'},
 'id': '123456789012345',
 'inline_message_id': 'BAAAAJorAAAz4B6OldTqM5uGeY8'}
```
(ok, to protect Great Britain finest agent privacy I edited some values)

Searching through the Telegram API doc it seems the actual object received is a [CallbackQuery](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#callbackquery) dict.

If this is actually true, the `msg` name of the param is really misleading... it appears in the [source code](https://telepot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_modules/telepot/helper.html#DefaultRouterMixin) and in the [examples](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/blob/master/examples/callback/lover.py). I know that technically a argument name is not a bug... but since we mostly infer the role and type of an argument through its name I really think this could be misleading.

issue_title: ipdb: routes with same prefix and different priority not handled properly
issue_body:
The kernel allows multiple routes for the same prefix with different priorities:
```
# ip route add 10.0.0.0/24 via 10.1.1.1 table 2
# ip route add 10.0.0.0/24 via 10.1.1.1 table 2 metric 10
# ip route add 10.0.0.0/24 via 10.1.1.1 table 2 metric 11
# ip route show table 2
10.0.0.0/24 via 10.1.1.1 dev eth0 
10.0.0.0/24 via 10.1.1.1 dev eth0 metric 10 

pyroute2 doesn't handle this situation properly:
```
>>> from pyroute2 import IPDB
>>> ipdb = IPDB()
>>> ipdb.routes.tables[2]
[{'metrics': {}, 'oif': 3, 'dst_len': 24, 'family': 2, 'proto': 3, 'tos': 0, 'dst': '10.0.0.0/24', 'flags': 0, 'ipdb_priority': 0, 'priority': 10, 'scope': 0, 'encap': {}, 'src_len': 0, 'table': 2, 'multipath': (), 'type': 1, 'gateway': '10.1.1.1', 'ipdb_scope': 'system'}]

(IPDB only sees one of the two routes.)  It looks like the issue is that the 'priority' field in 'RouteKey' is not marked as 'required' ... In other words, I think 'RouteKey._required' in ipdb.route.py should be 4 instead of 3.

The reason this is a problem for me is that I need to change the route priority with zero downtime.  If I change the priority of a route in pyroute2, this is handled around line 410 of ipdb/route.py, which checks whether RouteKey will change, and if so it deletes the old route first then adds the new route.  Unfortunately, this means there is a brief period of time where the route doesn't exist, and that is a problem for me.

My first thought was "maybe there is a way to modify pyroute2 so that it will actually change the existing route rather than deleting and re-adding it".  However, it turns out that the kernel itself doesn't support that: http://lkml.iu.edu/hypermail/linux/net/0504.3/0017.html

So, my second thought was "maybe I can manually add then remove the route", but this doesn't work either due to the bug described above:
```
>>> ipdb.routes.add(table=2, dst='10.0.0.0/24', gateway='10.1.1.1', priority=10)
>>> ipdb.commit()
>>> ipdb.routes.add(table=2, dst='10.0.0.0/24', gateway='10.1.1.1', priority=11)
>>> ipdb.commit()


  Is there any good way to fix this so that the new route will be added before the old route is deleted?  I know there are other cases where adding the new route first will cause a conflict in the kernel and therefore fail, so I think you would either have to detect whether the new route will cause a conflict in the kernel, or simply try adding the new route first and if it fails then delete the old route and retry adding the new route in order to implement this?

  The kernel doesn't actually support changing the priority of an existing route (See http://lkml.iu.edu/hypermail/linux/net/0504.3/0017.html ), so you have to add a new route and delete the old one.  If I change the priority of a route in pyroute2, this is handled around line 410 of ipdb/route.py, which checks whether RouteKey will change, and if so it deletes the old route first then adds the new route.  Unfortunately, this means there is a brief period of time where the route doesn't exist, and that is a problem for me.

issue_title: [announce] IPDB global transactions
issue_body:
An important IPDB update is coming with 0.3.10: global transactions in IPDB.

Previously there was per-interface transactions only::
```
    eth0 = ipdb.interfaves['eth0']
    eth0.address = …
    eth0.ifname = …
    eth0.add_ip()
    eth0.commit()
```
With 0.3.10 will be possible to run commit() call in IPDB instance also:
```
    eth0 = ipdb.interfaves['eth0']
    eth0.attr = … 
    …
    eth1 = ipdb.interfaves['eth1'] 
    eth1.attr = …
    …
    ipdb.commit()
```
The `commit` collects all the running transactions and commit them. In the case of an exception, all the transactions will be rolled back. So it is possible e.g., to change an interface settings only if some other interface is changed successfully.

By default transactions are not sorted and executed in some random order. If you need some exact sequential order of the transactions execution, you can set `tx_priority` (with 0.3.10 the name can be changed not to be confused with tx/rx queues, but now it is so) — the higher the number, the earlier the transaction will be executed in the common queue. Transactions w/o `tx_priority` set will go unsorted in the end by default.

Another change is that it is possible now to use «setter» functions on interface objects (in some future on routes too):
```
    # one way
    ip.interfaces.eth0.ifname = …

    # other way
    ip.interfaces.eth0.set_ifname(…)
```
The latter returns the interface instance, thus making call chaining possible here as well.

To review the coming changes, one can check out the `ipdb_global_transactions` branch (no docs yet, see diffs)

Right now only interface transactions are covered. Route transactions will be covered soon. With 0.3.11 I hope to deliver some sort of traffic controls integration in IPDB.

Any questions, proposals etc. are welcome. And stay tuned!

issue_title: add rinohtype support to nbconvert
issue_body:
This would allow exporting a Jupyter Notebook to PDF using rinohtype.

nbconvert relies on pandoc to convert from MarkDown to reStructuredText and LaTeX. Appareantly, 
Jupyter notebooks support MarkDown with HTML, (at least some of) which pandoc converts to the other formats. So it's not possible to parse the MarkDown source using rinohtype's CommonMark parser. The MarkDown format supported by Jupyter is probably different from the CommonMark spec anyway.

It is possible to use pandoc to convert to reStructuredText and feed this to rinohtype. Here's the start of a rinohtype-based PDF exporter:

```python
"""rinohtype PDF Exporter class"""

# Copyright (c) Jupyter Development Team.
# Distributed under the terms of the Modified BSD License.

from traitlets import default
from traitlets.config import Config
from testpath.tempdir import TemporaryWorkingDirectory

from .rst import RSTExporter


class RinohPDFExporter(RSTExporter):
    """
    Renders PDF using rinohtype documents.
    """

    @default('file_extension')
    def _file_extension_default(self):
        return '.pdf'

    output_mimetype = 'application/pdf'

    def from_notebook_node(self, nb, resources=None, **kw):
        rst, resources = super(RinohPDFExporter, self).from_notebook_node(nb, resources, **kw)

       # rst can be stored in a StringIO and passed to rinohtype

        with TemporaryWorkingDirectory():      # copied from the LaTeX PDF exporter
            notebook_name = 'notebook'
            rst_file = self.writer.write(rst, resources, notebook_name=notebook_name)
            self.log.info("Building PDF")
            ...

        return pdf_data, resources

```

issue_title: Disable certificate validation during test
issue_body:
When doing `urlopen(wellknown_url)` ([line 117](https://github.com/diafygi/acme-tiny/blob/master/acme_tiny.py#L117)), would it be possible to disable the certificate validation?

I had a server that didn't renew it's certificate in time, and because I've got the server redirecting all HTTP to HTTPS URLs, the check failed, and it didn't continue with the process of issuing a new certificate.

As a quick solution, I simply commented out theses lines, renewed the certificate, then un-commented them.

I should add that LetsEncrypt disables their certificate checking as well, presumably for this reason (plus, they are happy to do verification over insecure-HTTP, so websites can get their first certificate).

---

As to why the certificate expired in my case - the server had lost its DNS resolver address (from DHCP), so it stopped resolving domain names (including "acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org"). This meant that the connection to LetsEncrypt didn't work, and the error reports weren't being sent to me... where I should update my logging system to notice a lack of reports.

issue_title: Producer does not flush its buffer at exit
issue_body:
Hi, 
it's not actually an issue but rather a feature request. I have the following code:
```
#!/usr/local/bin/python3
from kafka import KafkaProducer

producer = KafkaProducer(bootstrap_servers=["10.20.30.40:5060"])
for i in range(1000):
    producer.send('topic', key=i.to_bytes(), value=i.to_bytes())
# that's all
```
When consumer reads the topic, it gets no messages. I've made an investigation and found that the reason is producer does not flush its message buffer before the script terminates. Appending `producer.flush()` to the script solves my problem, but not in all cases. For example, what if I've got an unhandled exception and flushing does not happen? Please consider handling `KafkaProducer.__del__()` and/or using `KafkaProducer` via the context (`with` statement).

issue_title: improve support for finding various offsets
issue_body:
I came across this issue while reproducing the exploits in [1], which require knowing the offsets of dynamic tags, relocations, and other objects within their respective sections.

Currently, this can be done in client code using something like the following:
```
def get_tag_offset(dynamic_section, n):
    return dynamic_section.header['sh_offset'] + n * dynamic_section._tagsize
```
Tagsize is marked for internal use, but can be gotten portably using `some_elffile.structs.Elf_Dyn.sizeof()`. However, the function above is not passed an ELFFile object. I think it's reasonable for client code to want to calculate offsets in this way without having been passed an ELFFile and without abusing internal attributes. At the very least, we might as well provide this functionality without forcing users to repeatedly implement functions like the one above.

The obvious solution to me is to add virtual address and offset attributes and `get_offset` methods to `Section`, `Relocation`, etc, so that an object's offset can be easily found without having access to the section or segment that contains it. However, this requires adding stuff in a number of places, so I want to see what people think before I submit a patch which touches most of the high-level API.

[1] [How the ELF Ruined Christmas](https://www.usenix.org/conference/usenixsecurity15/technical-sessions/presentation/di-frederico)

issue_title: Smarter handling of TemporaryDirectory disk space
issue_body:
When converting disks between formats, COT may need to use one or more temporary directories to store files. COT uses the [`tempfile`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/tempfile.html) module for this purpose. which by default creates temporary directories in, by preference:

   1. The directory named by the TMPDIR environment variable.
   2. The directory named by the TEMP environment variable.
   3. The directory named by the TMP environment variable.
   4. A platform-specific location:
        * On RiscOS, the directory named by the Wimp$ScrapDir environment variable.
        * On Windows, the directories C:\TEMP, C:\TMP, \TEMP, and \TMP, in that order.
        * On all other platforms, the directories /tmp, /var/tmp, and /usr/tmp, in that order.
    5. As a last resort, the current working directory.

When working with large files (such as RAW disk images), `/tmp` is often not large enough to contain these files, and COT fails ungracefully in this case.

COT should check the available disk space in the created temporary directory against the expected size of any file(s) it will store there and warn the user.

If an out of space error occurs, COT should recognize this and advise the user how to set their environment variables to select a different location.

issue_title: The Examples documentation for Test Docker Images has a flaws
issue_body:
Hello,

I was playing with the 'Test docker Images' here: https://testinfra.readthedocs.io/en/latest/examples.html#test-docker-images

I had implemented a single parameterized test using the `docker_images` decorator as suggested and noticed that the behavior was unexpected and caused me to troubleshoot why my tests where using the wrong OS for more than a few hours.  

Here's an example that will reproduce this.

VERSION INFO
===========
- testinfra version: 1.6.5
- docker version: 17.04
- python version: 2.7.13
- pytest version: 3.2.1

# Step 1
- Configure your `tests/conftest.py` as recommended in the link above.

# Step 2
- Implement some test
```
# tests/test_flaw.py

@pytest.mark.docker_images('centos:6')
def test_user_exists_on_centos6(host):
    root_user = host.user('root')

    assert root_user.exists
    assert root_user.shell == '/bin/base'
    assert root_user.gecos == 'root'
    assert root_user.home == '/root'
    assert root_user.group == 'root'

@pytest.mark.docker_images('centos:7')
def test_user_exists_on_centos7(host):

    # This will catch the flawed behavior.  On my system the system under test was CentOS 6.x!
    assert host.system_info.release == '7'

    root_user = host.user('root')

    assert root_user.exists
    assert root_user.shell == '/bin/base'
    assert root_user.gecos == 'root'
    assert root_user.home == '/root'
    assert root_user.group == 'root'
```
- Behavior of the above is not what you expect.  Yes, I can combine the test into a single test and just use the decorator `@pytest.mark.docker_images('centos:6', 'centos:7')` but the flawed behavior is the same. 

- We expect the two tests to run under two different docker containers one for `centos:6` and one for `centos:7`.  However, what I noticed was that I was executing two tests under `centos:6` even when the second test should have only executed on `centos:7`.  The assertion in the second test should expose the unexpected behavior that I'm noticing.  

I'll provide the example failure later.

issue_title: Disable alpha numeric sorting on suggestions
issue_body:

```
suggestions = fuzzyfinder('baa', ['zbaa', 'dbaa', 'abaa'])

issue_title: Watchtower + Multiprocessing
issue_body:
The logger is not successfully writing to CloudWatch when using multiprocessing. I tested to see whether this was my configuration by dropping a watchtower handler and using a file handler. This logged perfectly, however, when switching back to the watchtower handler only messages before and after `outputs = pool.map(worker, inputs)` worked.

Any idea how to fix this? Setting `use_queues` to `True` didn't help.

Sample code:

```python
import watchtower
import logging
from multiprocessing import Lock, Process, Queue, current_process, Manager, Pool

def worker(var):
    logger.debug("Incoming variable: %s" % var)
    logger.debug("Outgoing variable: %s" % (var+1))
    return var+1

def main():
    inputs = []
    for i in xrange(1000):
        inputs.append(i)

    logger.debug("Starting run now!")
    pool = Pool(processes=3)
    outputs = pool.map(worker, inputs)
    pool.close()
    pool.join()
    logger.debug("Just finished run")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    logger = logging.getLogger("multi")
    logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

    fh = logging.FileHandler("test.log")
    fh.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
    logger.addHandler(fh)

    wt_project_handler = watchtower.CloudWatchLogHandler(stream_name="test",
                                                         use_queues=True)
    wt_project_handler.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
    logger.addHandler(wt_project_handler)

    main()
```

issue_title: change datetime.date return type to string?
issue_body:
Please first make sure you have looked at:

* Documentation: https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki
* Other issues

### Environment

To diagnose, we usually need to know the following, including version numbers.  On Windows, be
sure to specify 32-bit Python or 64-bit:

- Python: 2.7.13 (Windows 10 64-bit), 2.7.14 (Linux)
- pyodbc: 4.0.21 (Windows 10 64-bit), 3.0.7-0ubuntu2 (Linux)
- DB: SQL Server 12.0.5000
- driver: MS ODBC 13

### Issue

I have moved the codebase for a large existing python 2 project from pymssql to pyodbc. The move was easy, everything builds and runs fine, except that pyodbc returns SQL Server columns of type `time` as `datetime.date`, where pymssql (as well as the postgres native drivers) return strings.

Meaning, I receive `datetime.time(11, 54, 13, 161000)` instead of `11:54:13.161000`.  Is there any way I can change this behavior globally without resorting to a stored procedure of some sort? Handling a returned object instead of a returned string will involve several days worth of refactoring and testing.

Suggestions?

Thanks,

John

import pyscreenshot

If there are multiple displays, only the primary one would be captured.

- Arch Linux x86_64 with two displays: No issue.

issue_title: Invalid mlogfilter pattern should result in an error
issue_body:
Hi,

mlogfilter should warn or error where there is an extra, unmatched,  parameters in command line. 

I was recently doing a Mongo University course, M202, and one of the tasks required usage of mlogfilter with --pattern.

The command I used looked like that : 

`mlogfilter example.log --namespace example --pattern "{"_id": 1, "l": {"$not": 1}}" --operation update`

Now, it was pointed out rightfully that the problem with that command is the usage of double quotes as pattern delimiter, while the pattern string itself contain double quotes.

I would however expect mlogfilter to at least warn that there is a parameter in command line which it can't use, or match to any parameter allowed. 

I find this silent ignoring of `_id": 1, "l": {"$not": 1}}"` a bug that can result in wrong information being found through mlogfilter.

Regards,
Piotr

issue_title: Use official builds from cefbuilds.com for all platforms
issue_body:
Below are listed custom patches that we currently apply to Chromium and CEF. The goal is to get rid of these so that we can use official builds from cefbuilds.com . Without requirement to build Chromium from sources we can speed up the process of building cefpython from several hours to just a few minutes - this will facilitate development of the project.

**1. [windows] [linux] Patch to fix HTTPS cache problems on sites with certificate errors**

This patch is so that caching works with self-signed SSL certificates on private networks. See Issue #125. We have to drop this feature or convince Marshall to add this patch in CEF - it modifies behavior only when "ignore_certificate_errors" option is set to True (which is not default), so I don't see why it would be a problem to apply this in CEF as well. We could even add a separate command line switch for this feature, so that it doesn't collide with current "ignore_certificate_errors" behavior. See the related [CEF topic](http://www.magpcss.org/ceforum/viewtopic.php?f=6&t=11609).

**2. [linux] Disabling tcmalloc memory allocator global hook**

Marshall is currently testing Linux builds with tcmalloc disabled and if there are no issues tcmalloc will be disabled by default for all builds on cefbuilds.com - See [upstream CEF issue 1827](https://bitbucket.org/chromiumembedded/cef/issues/1827/tcmalloc-should-be-disabled-in-linux-osx). Related cefpython issues: #73 and #155 .

**3. [linux] Patch to fix the CEF GTK implementation**

This patch needs to be reinvestigated. See [details here](https://github.com/cztomczak/cefpython/wiki/BuildOnLinux#fix-the-cef-gtk-implementation). Recently with Aura UI changes in Chromium there were many implementation changes in CEF in regards to GTK/X11. Needs to be checked whether the "hidden window" and "GtkVBox" issues were resolved. Regarding the GtkPizza element issue in wxPython - maybe this can be fixed by exposing a helper function in cefpython that will remove the GtkPizza element for the control CEF is being embedded in. The removal of GtkPizza element is causing auto-focus issues so while at it reinvestigate to see if anything can be done with that.

issue_title: Argcomplete doesn't work when script name is relative to ~
issue_body:
Argcomplete doesn't complete if you use a path relative to ~. This applies both to the global completion support and when activated for a single script only.

Example:
```
$ ./test-complete.py <TAB>
-h      --help  -t      --test
$ ~/test-complete.py <TAB>
Display all 110 possibilities? (y or n)
<Normal built in bash file completion result here, and no flags from the script>
$ /home/user_name/test-complete.py <TAB>
-h      --help  -t      --test
```
I used argcomplete 0.8.5 with Python 3.4 for the above example, in case that is relevant. I have *not* tested if the bug also happens with Python 2.x.

The python script in the example is a trivial test script (that I wrote to verify it wasn't my much more complicated program causing the issue), though I can attach it if you want to.

As a side note, this also appears to happen when relative an environment variable (i.e. `$HOME/test-complete.py <TAB>`), though that is not something I normally use (unlike ~-relative paths)

issue_title: image_to_data returns different results than image_to_string
issue_body:

I don't think that this is intended behavior.

issue_title: Maximum dimensions allowed
issue_body:
Hi, it seems the current implementation of sobol_seq allows generating sequences of coordinates for up to 40 dimensions.

I.e. executing the function for 100 dimensions returns the message:
```

    1 <= DIM_NUM <= 40

On the [linked page](http://people.sc.fsu.edu/~jburkardt/py_src/sobol/sobol.html) towards the original Sobol library, it states that there are improved implementations allowing up to 1111 dimensions. **Are there any intentions of adopting those improvements here?**

Thanks in any case for this sobol_seq implementation, very convenient!

speedtest-cli appears to report bytes as bits
issue_body:
When I run speedtest-cli on my wired ubuntu server, I get internet download speeds of ~3.5Mbps.  When I download a 100MB file from the internet, I get internet download speeds of ~3.5MBps (not bytes not bits). issue_title: given's interaction with mock.patch decorator is broken in almost every way
issue_body:
@waynew is trying to use unitest.mock's patch decorator together with Hypothesis's given and py.test's fixtures. He's not having a very good time of it, which is unsurprising because this combination almost entirely doesn't work.

The first way it goes wrong:

You can mostly use mock.patch with given if you don't care about py.test fixtures. For example, the following will work:

```python
import thing
import pytest
import hypothesis.strategies as st
from hypothesis import given
from unittest import mock

@mock.patch('thing.fnord')
@given(thing=st.text())
def test_a_thing(fnord, thing):
    pass
```

But if you try to add an extra named argument then everything will go wrong: Hypothesis will expose something with just *args and **kwargs because that's what mock.patch gives it, so py.test won't be able to find the fixture.

It's unclear that there is a correct thing Hypothesis could do at this point. mock.patch *is* using functools.wrap, so in Python 3 we could propagate the __wrapped__ attribute, but AFAIK there's no equivalent in Python 2. Additionally, note that the signature of the wrapped function is not correct, because the first arguments are filled in manually. Historically, propagating wrapped has broken py.test (https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/677), but this may be affected differently now?

Anyway: Can of worms, unclear there's a correct thing to do here.

The second way it goes wrong is more amusing and is *probably* fixable, or at least can be mitigated:

 import thing
import pytest
import hypothesis.strategies as st
from hypothesis import given
from unittest import mock

@given(thing=st.text())
@mock.patch('thing.fnord')
def test_a_thing(fnord, thing):
    assert False
```

This test passes.

Why does this test pass? It passes because Hypothesis can't currently tell the difference between being called as a bound method and just having an argument explicitly passed to it as the first positional argument. This means that it thinks that the mock is self and begins the shenanigans for [the executor API](http://hypothesis.readthedocs.org/en/latest/details.html#custom-function-execution). It looks for a method called execute_example, finds it, and then calls it with the test function.

It then of course immediately returns, never executing the test.

Possible ways to work around:

* Have given return a descriptor, so that we can actually detect if it's been bound as a method.
* Explicitly check for being passed a mock instance as self (I don't want to do this one, but it would work)
* Tell people not to do this, or to use autospec when using patch.

issue_title: abspath seems to be used on all paths, maybe normalize in argparse instead
issue_body:
Seems like all paths are normalized via os.path.abspath, if this is the case, it would be better to normalize them to absolute in argparse instead of tainting the main code with this. It could possibly also speed up the execution if you happen to have an insanely huge setup, but the biggest win would be less cruft in the code. One could also imagine os.path.exists checks if the user entered something bogus to be able to fail early via generic checks in combination with a meaningful error message.

Since doing this depends on the 'root' parameter, you would have to do this via a custom action, as follows from the Python documentation:

    >>> class FooAction(argparse.Action):
    ...     def __init__(self, option_strings, dest, nargs=None, **kwargs):
    ...         if nargs is not None:
    ...             raise ValueError("nargs not allowed")
    ...         super(FooAction, self).__init__(option_strings, dest, **kwargs)
    ...     def __call__(self, parser, namespace, values, option_string=None):
    ...         print '%r %r %r' % (namespace, values, option_string)
    ...         setattr(namespace, self.dest, values)

A single action could be crafted to support both single and multi-occur parameters.

Hi,

I am using XlsxWriter to do SOMETHING but it appears to do SOMETHING ELSE.

I am using Python version 2.7.11 and XlsxWriter 0.9.6 and Excel version 14.0.7177.5000 (32-bit).

Here is some code that demonstrates the problem:

```python

import xlsxwriter
import datetime
workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('hello.xlsx')
fmt = workbook.add_format()
fmt.set_num_format('mm/dd/yyyy')
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()
worksheet.write_datetime(0,0,datetime.date(1900,1,1),fmt)
workbook.close()

```

I'm pretty sure the problem is in this section of utility.py:

    # Special case for datetime where time only has been specified and
    # the default date of 1900-01-01 is used.
    if (not isinstance(dt_obj, datetime.timedelta)
            and dt_obj.isocalendar() == (1900, 1, 1)):
        excel_time -= 1

I think it should instead read
    if (not isinstance(dt_obj, datetime.timedelta)
         and not isinstance(dt_obj, datetime.date)
            and dt_obj.isocalendar() == (1900, 1, 1)):
        excel_time -= 1

issue_title: Backward incompatibility: error path not available anymore
issue_body:
The code below raises a voluptuous error because extra data are forbidden by and empty schema.

```
from voluptuous import Schema

try:
    Schema({})({"extra": "invalid", "extra2": "invalid"})
except Exception as exc:
    print '\n'.join([', '.join(error.path) for error in exc.errors])
```

With voluptuous 0.9.3, the extra keys are displayed ("extra2", "extra").
With voluptuous >=0.10, the error `path` is not longer available and an empty string is displayed.

Is it an expected change, or is it a bug that needs to be fixed?

issue_title: Schema contraction
issue_body:
There is a useful function in the Schema class that allows extension of a schema by adding keys to it. This saves a lot code when you have multiple schema with only minor variations. Something that might also be useful is the ability to contract a schema, taking keys away. I'm not sure if this would be too specific a feature for this library, and I'm also not sure of a clean way to do it, but right now we write our own helper functions to do just this.

Is it worth pursuing?

issue_title: Sikuli submodule imports and image path
issue_body:
This is just something I noticed while converting my sikuli scripts.

Sikuli has its own import mechanism to mimic Python package imports, allowing you to import a `submodule.sikuli` folder with a `submodule.py` and any related images inside, and adds them to the image path.

So if you want to structure your scripts, e.g. by functionality like below, you can use ``import submodule`` in your main `module.sikuli`:
```
├── module.sikuli/
│   ├── module.py
│   ├── image.png
│   ├── submodule.sikuli/
│   │   ├── submodule.py
│   │   ├── image2.png
```
For lackey I had to change this back to a normal Python subpackage structure - remove `.sikuli` from folder names and rename `submodule.py` to  `__init__.py` inside:
```
├── module/
│   ├── module.py
│   ├── image.png
│   ├── submodule/
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── image2.png
```
This is all good and makes more sense in pure Python to me, but the subfolders are not added to the image path automatically on import, like in sikuli (so it can't find those images). This hack works for me (`__init__.py` in each of the subpackages):
```
from lackey import *
addImagePath(os.path.join(getBundlePath(), __name__))
```
Not sure yet if/how this could be changed but just wanted to note this. Maybe if lackey adds any subpackages found in the main module's directory to the image path on import?

issue_title: XML with multiple objects vs one object yields different results.
issue_body:
One of my queries to a web service returns multiple `Things` in XML.

	<ArrayOfVehicleMakes xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" xmlns="http://ws.somedomain.com/">
		<VehicleMakes>
			<VehicleMakeID>28</VehicleMakeID>
			<MakeValue>Ford</MakeValue>
		</VehicleMakes>
		<VehicleMakes>
			<VehicleMakeID>60</VehicleMakeID>
			<MakeValue>Nissan</MakeValue>
		</VehicleMakes>
	</ArrayOfVehicleMakes>


`xmltodict.parse(multiple_VehicleMakes_xml)` yields...

	OrderedDict([
		(u'ArrayOfVehicleMakes', OrderedDict([
			(u'@xmlns:xsi', u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance'),
			(u'@xmlns:xsd', u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema'),
			(u'@xmlns', u'http://ws.somedomain.com/'),
			(u'VehicleMakes', [
				OrderedDict([
					(u'VehicleMakeID', u'28'),
					(u'MakeValue', u'Ford')
				]),
				OrderedDict([
					(u'VehicleMakeID', u'60'),
					(u'MakeValue', u'Nissan')
				])
			])
		]))
	])

Note that `VehicleMakes` is a list containing two OrderedDicts.


Another query returns only one `VehicleMakes` in XML.

	<ArrayOfVehicleMakes xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" xmlns="http://ws.somedomain.com/">
		<VehicleMakes>
			<VehicleMakeID>23</VehicleMakeID>
			<MakeValue>Dodge</MakeValue>
		</VehicleMakes>
	</ArrayOfVehicleMakes>

`xmltodict.parse(one_VehicleMakes_xml)` yields...

	OrderedDict([
		(u'ArrayOfVehicleMakes', OrderedDict([
			(u'@xmlns:xsi', u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance'),
			(u'@xmlns:xsd', u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema'),
			(u'@xmlns', u'http://ws.somedomain.com/'),
			(u'VehicleMakes', OrderedDict([
				(u'VehicleMakeID', u'23'),
				(u'MakeValue', u'Dodge')
			]))
		]))
	])

Note that `VehicleMakes` here is an individual OrderedDict.

I suppose I can check to see what the value of `VehicleMakes` is (list or ordered dict) and act accordingly but I've never run into this before where one way it goes list and one way it goes ordered dict.

Does it have to do with the name `VehicleMakes`? Because it is plural?

I don't have control over the name of `VehicleMakes`, a VehicleMakes represents a single object. I know, it's annoying.

Is this by design and something I need to be working around in my code, or is it not intended?

issue_title: relative import under python2
issue_body:
As python2 defaults to relative import, an ``include`` module importing a new module attempts to load global modules as ``include`` modules. This causes an attempt to load the module name as a URI, which usually fails in ``load_module``. In turn, this causes the import to fail.

For example, if ``include.mount.files.docs&#SEPexamples&#SEPfzk_alice_xrootd&#DOTpy`` imports ``chainlet``, an import for ``include.mount.files.chainlet`` is attempted. This is interpreted as loading the *file* ``chainlet``.

As a workaround, starting module content with ``from __future__ import absolute_import`` will prevent the issue. Ideally, ``include`` will perform only explicit relative imports.

issue_title: Lack of proper support for Facebook Canvas Apps?
issue_body:
The codebase is having some canvas app support (apparently inherited from django-social-auth), but it doesnt seem to be usable anymore. Or at least I am misunderstanding how it should be done :)

With regular oauth login buttons, button is redirecting to `/login/<backend>`, which usually redirects to oauth provider page, then return to `/complete/<backend>` and then redirects to `/` or whatever is configured.

With FB Canvas apps it looks like PSA is requiring `/login/facebook-app` and `/complete/facebook-app` to be called in proper moments, but FB dashboard is allowing to set only single canvas URL. So, if I understand correctly, I have following options:

1. Set canvas url to `/login/facebook-app`, and provide html template like the one in docs, with some redirect URI. But:
 1. if the redirect URI is my canvas URL, I'm getting infinite login loop
 1. I'm not sure if I can redirect to some URL other than my canvas URL (not checked yet), and trick FB to load that in its canvas iframe (after all I'm overriding window.top). Maybe yes.
 1. Regardless, even if the above will work, I will have extra 2 redirects for users who are already authenticated and just want to re-visit my app. Not cool.
1. Set canvas_url to `/complete/facebook-app`:
 1. this works well if user already allowed to share his info with the app, so `complete` might create user record and use it
 1. but of course will work only once, and of course there is no place where user can share profile data with the app in the first place, since in this scenario nobody displays permission dialog
 1. I can try to write my own backend (basically copied from FacebookAppOAuth2) and inside having some smarter `complete` which optionally tries to redirect to login, if necessary. Yet, after short code inspection it seems that it will be extremely hacky at best.
1. Provide my own `/login/fb` kind of view, which can check whether we need to ask for permission or not, and then call login or complete from social auth. Yet, that is extremely tricky, since we have POST data which basically means that we cannot redirect, or we lose the data. So, apparently importing the view methods from PSA django app and calling them directly would be the way...
1. Something else?

FB Canvas app and its associated POST is done in such way that if user is already authenticated, it allows app to continue with zero redirects. Just the response to initial POST will contain HTML for the specific user. I hope that with PSA it is possible to reach the same situation, with no redirects. If user had not shared personal info with the app yet, single redirect to permission dialog and back is unavoidable. In this case, however, PSA requires third redirect after `/complete/..`, it would be great to be able to avoid it as well.

Now, am I missing something obvious? Is there a way to get current code cooperate properly with canvas app? Or I'm first user in years who is trying to do so, only to discover that the code is badly outdated? Any hints appreciated ;)

issue_title: Returned state token not url-encoded
issue_body:
Current implementation reads state from request query parameter with request.get_param(), which handles url-decoding automatically. When state is written back to a redirect url it is not url-encoded. This causes state mismatch in client. Only applies to clients that use state strings that yield are url-decodable.

Reproducable with /examples/authorization_code_grant.py, modifying ClientApplication.

Generate a state token in client:
```
def _request_auth_token(self):
        print("Requesting authorization token...")
        auth_endpoint = self.api_server_url + "/authorize"
        self.state = "123123%25"
        query = urllib.urlencode({"client_id": "abc",
                                  "redirect_uri": self.callback_url,
                                  "response_type": "code",
                                  "state":self.state,
                                  })

        location = "%s?%s" % (auth_endpoint, query)
```

And check state when receiving token:
```
def _read_auth_token(self, env):
    print("Receiving authorization token...")

    query_params = urlparse.parse_qs(env["QUERY_STRING"])
    self.auth_token = query_params["code"][0]
    
    if self.state and self.state != query_params["state"][0]:
        raise Exception("Received auth token with invalid state. If this was a browser, CSRF")
```   

Fix by url-encoding the state when applying it to redirect urls.
/oauth2/grant.py
```
469      query += "&state=" + quote(self.state)

708      uri_with_fragment += "&state=" + quote(self.state)

issue_title: Unsupported encodings?
issue_body:
I wanted to parse a UTF-16 CSV file, so I did something like this:

    r = unicodecsv.reader(f, encoding='UTF-16')

Unfortunately, this just raises an exception when I try to read from it.  I looked at the unicodecsv source code, and I don't think the unicodecsv approach can ever work for this case.  It tries loading the input stream as 8-bit characters, and then decodes each cell value.  Python's 'csv' module can't handle NUL bytes, which are common in UTF-16, so this fails.

I think the answer to this is that the 'unicodecsv' library only works for encodings like UTF-8 or Latin-1 which are supersets of ASCII, and don't use 0x00 bytes.  Is this true?  We should put it in the documentation.

(Also, I think this means I should really upgrade to Python 3!)

issue_title: 新浪多个股票自动encode的问题 可以这么解决
issue_body:
看了下aiohttp的文档. 发现url可以是一个yarl.URL的变量. 

```
import asyncio

import aiohttp
import yarl


# t = easyquotation.use('sina')
# quote = t.all
# print(quote)
async def main():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as c:
        url = yarl.URL('http://hq.sinajs.cn/list=sh601006,sh600000', encoded=True)
        a = await c.get(url)
        print(await a.text())


loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

issue_title: Some remarks on the behavior of some of the widgets
issue_body:
Following are notes of my first impressions when running the examples provided; this is not saying that the behavior of the widgets should be changed; it is more to open a discussion, because the object behavior differed from my intuitive expectation.


**EntryGrid & LabelGrid:**

Should probably be able to choose which row to delete.
adding/deleting a row would be better if it did not move the buttons --> maybe place them on top & set the width?


**Graph:**

Should probably be resizable with the window, by default


**Led:**

Maybe a click on the LED should toggle the on/off?
...or maybe not?
In the examples: `led.to_grey` does not accept an argument; this is inconsistent with the other colors.  Maybe it should take an arg, but ignore it?


**RotaryScale:**

In the examples, the increments go over 100 while neither the display nor the gauge do.
That is, after clicking over 100, reversing of the dial, and values, are lagging when pressing decrement.
The same is true at the other end of the scale.
It seemed a bit odd.

Cheers.
Fred

issue_title: USBErrorBusy with devices in hot-plug events notifications
issue_body:
Hello guys,

I've been playing and implementing some backend with `usb1` and `libusb1` for the past week,
trying to use the event to detect some devices and manage them once registered.

I've stumbled across a weird behaviour for me and would like to know if this is supposed to be a bug (in libusb or python-usb1) or this is expected behaviour that i'm supposed to handle.

The issues appears during hot plug events, when a device is plug-in while listening, trying to get the `device.getSerialNumber()` raise `USBErrorBusy`.

I've tried a multiple of solution including `detachKernelDriver`, `claimInterface`, `releaseInterface` and `resetDevice` but nothing seems to affect the issue.

Strangely if the devices are already plugging in before registering for hot-plugs event, the serialNumber can be retrieve and no `USBErrorBusy` is raised.

I've found an ugly work-around which is to fetch the device and serialNumber through a second `USBContext` instance instanciated on the fly, which make me feel like it's a bug somewhere.

I'm joining this issue with an sample script that reproduce the issue, you can easily test it with plugin a android phone in and out, and also having the phone already plug-in before launching the script.



Here the Work-around in details and next is a sample text output:
```python
try:
    print "Device SerialNumber: %s" % device.getSerialNumber()
except Exception as e:
    with usb1.USBContext() as context:
        device_ = context.getByVendorIDAndProductID(device.getVendorID(), device.getProductID())
        serialNumber_ = device_.getSerialNumber()
        print "Fetching device with new usbContext fix usb_busy error: %s" % serialNumber_
```

```
Registering hotplug callback...
Device arrived: Bus 020 Device 024: ID 18d1:4ee2 - 1
Device SerialNumber: ENU7N15B14005190
Device arrived: Bus 026 Device 003: ID 05ac:8510 - 1
Device SerialNumber: CC2D820AKCDN9KB0
Device arrived: Bus 029 Device 007: ID 05ac:8286 - 1
Device SerialNumber: None
Device arrived: Bus 029 Device 004: ID 05ac:0262 - 1
Device SerialNumber: None
Device arrived: Bus 029 Device 003: ID 0a5c:4500 - 1
Device SerialNumber: None
Device arrived: Bus 029 Device 002: ID 0424:2512 - 1
Device SerialNumber: None
Device arrived: Bus 026 Device 001: ID 8087:0024 - 1
Device SerialNumber: None
Device arrived: Bus 029 Device 001: ID 8087:0024 - 1
Device SerialNumber: None
Callback registered. Monitoring events, ^C to exit

Device left: Bus 020 Device 024: ID 18d1:4ee2 - 1

Device arrived: Bus 020 Device 025: ID 18d1:4ee2 - 1
device.getSerialNumber failed with: LIBUSB_ERROR_BUSY [-6] (USBErrorBusy)
Fetching device with new usbContext fix usb_busy error: ENU7N15B14005190

Device left: Bus 020 Device 025: ID 18d1:4ee2 - 1

Device arrived: Bus 020 Device 023: ID 18d1:4ee2 - 1
device.getSerialNumber failed with: LIBUSB_ERROR_BUSY [-6] (USBErrorBusy)
Fetching device with new usbContext fix usb_busy error: ENU7N15B14005190
```

Could i fix the issue in the first place without needing my work-around ?
Should this be reported to `libusb1` or handle directly in `python-usb1` ?

Is there any device release business logic that should happen during hot-plug disconnect or connect event to make the usb "un-busy"

Thanks for the help guys. 

`hotplug.py`
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# Copyright (C) 2013-2016  Vincent Pelletier <plr.vincent@gmail.com>
#
# This library is free software; you can redistribute it and/or
# modify it under the terms of the GNU Lesser General Public
# License as published by the Free Software Foundation; either
# version 2.1 of the License, or (at your option) any later version.
#
# This library is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
# but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
# MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the GNU
# Lesser General Public License for more details.
#
# You should have received a copy of the GNU Lesser General Public
# License along with this library; if not, write to the Free Software
# Foundation, Inc., 51 Franklin Street, Fifth Floor, Boston, MA  02110-1301  USA
import usb1

eventType = {   usb1.HOTPLUG_EVENT_DEVICE_ARRIVED: 'arrived',
                usb1.HOTPLUG_EVENT_DEVICE_LEFT: 'left', }

def hotplug_callback(context, device, event):
    print "Device %s: %s - %s" % (eventType[event], device, device.getNumConfigurations())

    try:
        print "Device SerialNumber: %s" % device.getSerialNumber()
    except Exception as e:
        if type(e) is usb1.USBErrorNoDevice:
            return 

        print "device.getSerialNumber failed with: %s (%s)" % (e, type(e).__name__)        
        with usb1.USBContext() as context:
            device_ = context.getByVendorIDAndProductID(device.getVendorID(), device.getProductID())
            serialNumber_ = device_.getSerialNumber()
            print "Fetching device with new usbContext fix usb_busy error: %s" % serialNumber_
    pass

def main():
    with usb1.USBContext() as context:
        if not context.hasCapability(usb1.CAP_HAS_HOTPLUG):
            print 'Hotplug support is missing. Please update your libusb version.'
            return
        print 'Registering hotplug callback...'
        opaque = context.hotplugRegisterCallback(hotplug_callback)
        print 'Callback registered. Monitoring events, ^C to exit'
        try:
            while True:
                context.handleEvents()
        except (KeyboardInterrupt, SystemExit):
            print 'Exiting'

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

issue_title: potential error in calculating CLV
issue_body:
I found an issue about the calculation of CLV. 

In customer_lifetime_value(), the discounted cash flow for each period is calculated as:

```
df['expected_revenues_period_'+str(i)] = df.apply(
    lambda r: (m*transaction_prediction_model.predict(i, r['frequency'], r['recency'], r['T'])/(1+d)**(i/30)),
    axis=1
    )
```

in which,
_i_ is th looping counter (in steps of 30), 
_m_ is self.conditional_expected_average_profit(),
_d_ is discount rate.
_transaction_prediction_model.predict()_ returns "conditional_expected_number_of_purchases_up_to_time"

It seems to me that _m_ should be the expected profit for individual customer instead of all the training records, and the discounted cash flow for a single period is `m * (predict(t) - predict(t-1)) / (1+d)**(i/30)`

May I propose to make such change:

```
df['monetary_value'] = monetary_value

for i in range(30, (time*30)+1, 30):
    df['expected_revenues_period_'+str(i)] = df.apply(
          lambda r: (r['monetary_value']*
              (transaction_prediction_model.predict(i, r['frequency'], r['recency'], r['T'])
               - transaction_prediction_model.predict(i - 30, r['frequency'], r['recency'], r['T'])) /(1+d)**(i/30)),
              axis=1
     )
```

issue_title: Unicode filename gets crippled when drag-and-dropped
issue_body: When a Python script is run by drag-and-dropping another file, its path gets crippled in `sys.argv` if it contains Unicode characters, and our `unicode_argv` module doesn't resolve this. Note that this is an issue even in Python 3. Since even calling `GetCommandLineW` returns a crippled path, this may be issue with Windows itself. I have started an issue in Python bug tracker: http://bugs.python.org/issue27469.

issue_title: Does not include both text/plain and text/html versions.
issue_body:
After upgrading from [sendgrid-django](https://github.com/elbuo8/sendgrid-django/), our emails used to be `multipart/alternative` with both `text/plain` and `text/html` content included, but are now simply `text/html` -- the plain text version is no longer included.

In sendgrid-django, an email that inherited from `EmailMultiAlternatives` would trigger both `mail.add_content(Content("text/plain", email.body))` and `mail.add_content(Content(alt[1], alt[0]))`. It appears that in django-sendgrid-v5, the same email only triggers `mail.add_content(Content(alt[1], alt[0]))` without adding the `text/plain` content.

The relevant diff seems to be https://github.com/elbuo8/sendgrid-django/blob/master/sgbackend/mail.py#L87-L91 vs https://github.com/sklarsa/django-sendgrid-v5/blob/master/sendgrid_backend/mail.py#L136-L144.

Is this an intentional change? My understanding of [Django mail](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/email/#django.core.mail.send_mail) implies that a message with both `message` and `html_message` args should include both content types.

issue_title: Request: Optionally validate Username uniqueness with iexact
issue_body:
Having read [this issue](https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/2273) raised at the Django repository, the Django team aren't willing to fix this, citing some strange reasons.

Currently, StringsonFire, STRINGSonfire and stringsONfire would all be valid usernames in the same database, even though, when case is ignored, they're the same. Given usernames are a simple and visually friendly way to identify users within a webapp, I've found an additional test to consider the above three as violating the unique constraint to be useful.

I've written this validator, which uses both UserModel() and UsernameField() to align with the rest of django-registration:

    from django.core.exceptions import ValidationError
    from django.utils.translation import ugettext_lazy as _

    from registration.users import UserModel, UsernameField

    User = UserModel()

    def username_case_insensitive_unique_validator(value):
        kwargs = {'{0}__{1}'.format(UsernameField(), 'iexact'): value}
        if User.objects.filter(**kwargs):
            raise ValidationError(_('A user with that username already exists.'), code='invalid')

I wonder if this could be included, perhaps with a setting `USERNAME_CASE_INSENSITIVE_VALIDATION = True`, or, perhaps like `RegistrationFormUniqueEmail`, where this is already implemented for emails, this could sit in another form?
