issue_title: clipperScale
issue_body:
It's currently set to 100000.  This is not ideal.  It has more rounding error that it has to.  For example, using this scale, 18 / 3 will NOT result in 6:
```
>>> s = 100000
>>> ((18 * s) // 3 * (1./s))
6.000000000000001
>>> ((18 * s) // 3 * (1./s)) == 6
False
```

Whereas if you use a power of two, rounding will be minimized.  I suggest 1<<16, to be close to your existing number.

```
>>> s = 1<<16
>>> ((18 * s) // 3 * (1./s))
6.0
>>> ((18 * s) // 3 * (1./s)) == 6
True
```

issue_title: Inherit management commands from BaseCommand (instead of NoArgsCommand)
issue_body:
Zinnia has two management commands that inherit from `NoArgsCommand`:

1. [spam_cleanup](https://github.com/Fantomas42/django-blog-zinnia/blob/develop/zinnia/management/commands/spam_cleanup.py#L10)
1. [count_discussions](https://github.com/Fantomas42/django-blog-zinnia/blob/develop/zinnia/management/commands/count_discussions.py#L10)

`NoArgsCommand` is deprecated since Django 1.8, and management commands should now inherit from `BaseCommand` instead according to [Django's documentation](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/howto/custom-management-commands/#django.core.management.NoArgsCommand).

issue_title: Version 0.4.2 is incompatible with django-storages
issue_body:
We deployed this on our live site today and it crashed our whole admin site, due to this change https://github.com/fabiocaccamo/django-admin-interface/commit/a09e13fb0070abcb9edac89060446e3aefdfa353#diff-e561f32039c364b52846f6a4931fd449R110

It crashing because we use S3 as media storage (from `django-storage`), which does not implement the `Storage.path` method, as per the Django base class docstring:
> Returns a local filesystem path where the file can be retrieved using Python's built-in open() function. Storage systems that can't be accessed using open() should *not* implement this method.

Reverting to the previous version of `django-admin-interface` (0.4.1) fixed the problem.

issue_title: Any chance of wrapping Jinja?
issue_body:
I was pretty surprised at how slow the Django templates could get. I popped in some Jinga templates using django-jinga and saw 2300ms drop to 290ms. However I couldn't see it yet in the panel.

It was pretty easy to wrap the basic Jinga rendering but I'm not sure about wrapping blocks.

The import:
```
try:
    from jinja2.environment import Environment
    jinja2 = True
except ImportError:
    jinja2 = False
```
The new name getter (pass in the `args` and `kwargs` from `timing_hook`).
```
    def name(self, args, kwargs):
        if hasattr(self, 'name'):
            name = self.name
        elif jinja2:
            if len(args) > 1:
                name = args[1]
            else:
                name = kwargs.get('name')
        return name or ''
```
The wrapper:
```
        if jinja2:
            Environment.compile = _template_render_wrapper(
                Environment.compile, "templates",
                lambda n: not any([re.match(pattern, n) for pattern in TEMPLATE_TIMINGS_SETTINGS["IGNORED_TEMPLATES"]])
            )
```

Also I was surprised at how plug and play django-jinga is and the immediate results. It uses a middleware to redirect all templates ending with e.g "j.html" to Jinga and so you don't have to redo all your templates at once.

issue_title: Clean up append_response_error_content function
issue_body:
The` _common_util.append_response_error_content` function currently has an unused `**kwargs` argument as part of the function signature and a `#pylint: disable=unused-argument` comment to force pylint to ignore this.  In this case, pylint is correct in flagging `**kwargs` as unused and it should be removed along with the pylint "disable" comment.  Also the function could stand to be cleaned up and rewritten.  Suggest this:

```python
def append_response_error_content(response):
    resp_dict = response.json()
    if response.status_code >= 400 and resp_dict is not None:
        reason = resp_dict.get('reason', '')
        error = resp_dict.get('error', '')
        response.reason = '{0} {1}'.format(error, reason)
    return response
```

    def __init__(self, factor, target_size, **kwargs):
        super(Inter, self).__init__(**kwargs)

        self._affine = mx.nd.reshape(mx.nd.array([[1. * factor, 0, 0],
                                 [0, 1. * factor, 0]]), shape=(1, 6))

        self.target_size = target_size



    def forward(self, x):
        out = mx.nd.SpatialTransformer(x,
                                   self.affine_matrix,
                                   self.target_size,
                                   'affine',
                                   'bilinear'
                                   )
```

[1]    4057 segmentation fault  python3 train-test.py
```

## Minimum reproducible example
this is the script described above.
```py
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import time
from multiprocessing import cpu_count

import mxnet as mx
from mxnet.gluon.data import dataset

CPU_COUNT = cpu_count()


class MyDataset(dataset.Dataset):
    def __init__(self):
        """Init."""

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        """Get item."""
        img = mx.nd.random.uniform(shape=(24, 24, 3))
        return img, 0

    def __len__(self):
        """Dataset length."""
        return 10000


train_dataset = MyDataset()

batch_size = 32
train_data_loader = mx.gluon.data.DataLoader(
    train_dataset, batch_size, shuffle=True, num_workers=CPU_COUNT)

ctx = mx.cpu()
ctx = mx.gpu() if mx.test_utils.list_gpus() else mx.cpu()

epochs = 50
for epoch in range(epochs):
    print('####### epoch {} ########'.format(epoch))
    # training loop (with autograd and trainer steps, etc.)
    start = time.time()
    training_samples = 0
    for batch_idx, (data, label) in enumerate(train_data_loader):
        
        # comment out those 2 lines, this script will finish without error.
        data = data.as_in_context(ctx)
        label = label.as_in_context(ctx)

        training_samples += data.shape[0]
        if training_samples > 0 and training_samples % 100 == 0:
            elapse = time.time() - start
            print('{}:[{}] samples/s'.format(batch_idx,
                                             training_samples/elapse))

```


Also, I tried another script:
```py
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import mxnet as mx

from multiprocessing import Pool

def run(par):
    ctx = mx.cpu()
    print('####### run {} ########'.format(par))
    for x in range(5000):
        data = mx.nd.random.uniform(shape=(32, 320, 320, 3))
        data = data.as_in_context(ctx)

p = Pool()
p.map(run, range(50))
p.close()
p.join()
```
this script will finish without any error, with or without mkl.

issue_title: Intel MKL version
issue_body:
This commit (https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/pull/5000) requires Intel MKL 2018 beta, which some features are not available in Intel MKL 2017.

For example: 
File: src\operator\mkl\mkl_pooling-inl.h
```
line 183:
    case pool_enum::kAvgPooling:
      algorithm = (param_.pooling_convention == pool_enum::kValid) ?
          dnnAlgorithmPoolingAvgIncludePadding : dnnAlgorithmPoolingAvg;
```
dnnAlgorithmPoolingAvgIncludePadding is only defined in Intel MKL 2018 beta: https://software.intel.com/en-us/mkl-developer-reference-c-2018-beta-enumerated-types

And currently it was not defined: https://software.intel.com/en-us/node/684760 (Intel MKL 2017 update 2)

issue_title: AutoSlugField doesn't set generated slug back to model instance.
issue_body:
I think this line [setattr(model_instance, self.attname, slug)](https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/blob/master/django_extensions/db/fields/__init__.py#L171) should be moved into `AutoSlugField.pre_save` method, so anything `AutoSlugField.pre_save` produce is set back to model instance. The same way how [django DateField does](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/fields/__init__.py#L1260).
The main reason is to be consistent. After you `save()` your model instance, the slug value off your model instance may be different to what is saved in db currently.

issue_title: Off by one error in _int_size
issue_body:
I noticed this when I was in the area from #179, but the \_int_size calculations have an off by one error: The smallest representable twos-complement integer in n bytes is -2^(n - 1), but this function treats it as if it were  - 2^(n - 1) - 1 (i.e. the negation of the largest reprsentable number).

This means that some numbers require one more byte to represent than is necessary (not a major deal) but also that it errors erroneously about -2^63.

issue_title: Look into how venusian does configuration
plone.protect's CSRF protection override doesn't propagate to parent request
issue_body:
If I mark the request with IDisableCSRFProtection (from plone.protect) during a subrequest, this interface is applied only to the subrequest itself, and not the parent. This makes it impossible to disable CSRF protection inside tile code.

This is causing me specific issues using plone.app.imaging scales inside a tile, as the scales are generated when the tile is rendered, and then CSRF auto-protect blocks the main page request.

I've raised the same issue in plone.protect here: https://github.com/plone/plone.protect/issues/10, as the problem is really with plone.protect's auto-csrf protection but the only place I can see that this could be resolved is somewhere in plone.subrequest.

issue_title: Not possible to use/integrate schemaeditor
issue_labels: [{'id': 241106863, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/plone/plone.schemaeditor/labels/01%20type:%20bug', 'name': '01 type: bug', 'color': 'fa0000', 'default': False}, {'id': 274229966, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/plone/plone.schemaeditor/labels/02%20type:%20regression', 'name': '02 type: regression', 'color': 'ff9900', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Since 2.0.16 (and this [commit](https://github.com/plone/plone.schemaeditor/commit/896a5b8e45bf426dfd224e2827e91a1a16562dc0)), there is a regression in the ability to use the schema editor outside of autotoc tabs (on pages where autotoc pattern is unused).

For my purposes, this is a regression.  The only way (for me) to work around this is to call `require('pat-registry').patterns.autotoc.init `on some dummy (or hidden) element to trigger that patterns init event.  Obviously this is not a problem for core use inside Dexterity types control panel, but this might affect add-ons beside my own.

@thet what was the "wait until autotoc is ready" use case?  Was there an order issue affecting the Dexterity Types CP? 

I wonder if there also a reasonable way to expose the ability to initialize the schema editor imperatively, but the zero-namespace IIFE the code is inside now preempts that?  Exporting a namespace from a return value of the IIFE seems a reasonable compromise?

>    It applies to every Media Segment that appears after it in the
>    Playlist until the next EXT-X-MAP tag or until the end of the
>    playlist.

>    Its format is:

issue_title: Default value of Union is not respected
issue_body:
Ran into this today: it seems the specified default value for ``traitlets.Union`` is not respected.

```python
import traitlets as T

class Foo(T.HasTraits):
    unicode = T.Unicode(allow_none=True, default_value=None)
    unicode_or_list = T.Union([T.Unicode(), T.List(T.Unicode())],
                              allow_none=True, default_value=None)
    
f = Foo()
print(f.unicode)  # This is None as expected
print(f.unicode_or_list)  # This is an empty string, should be None?
```

The workaround is to specify the default value within the first entry of the Union's list, but I found this behavior surprising. Is it a bug?

issue_title: Composition of parameters
issue_body:
Consider this small example:

```python
class A(Task):
    log = Parameter(default="/var/log.txt")

class B(Task):
    logPath = Parameter(default="~/")

class C(A, B):
    logFile = Parameter(default="log.txt")
```

Now, I want to tell task ``C`` to overwrite its inherited ``log`` parameter, which should be ``os.path.join(logPath, logFile)``. To be consistent with the instance caching, I cannot use the
``__init__`` method. However, I could extend the default ``get_param_values`` method, but
that does not seem clean enough to me. In cases like this I would propose something like a
hook to ensure parameter flexibility when e.g. working in such inheritance scenarios. If ``A.log``
had not default (which might be the case if you want to define ``A`` as some kind of interface),
the extension of ``get_param_values`` wouldn't work anyway.

Edit: solved in PR #1254.

issue_title: Material shininess value scale is dependant on input format
issue_body:
Depending on the imported format, shininess value read from material might be either scaled or remain unchanged. When a scale is applied, it also depends on imported format.

For instance, shininess ranges between following values for formats
- FBX -> scaled [0,100]
- OBJ -> scaled [0, 4000]
- DAE -> untouched (Blender range is [0, 511])

Ideally, the retrieved shininess should always be scaled by Assimp based on format to provide a **unique  scale** (for instance between [0.0, 1.0]).

Samples allowing to find those values were exported with Blender 2.77 and can be found [here](https://drive.google.com/file/d/0B-A32fKlVI01OUVIOTg2WFoyS2s/view?usp=sharing.)

Related to #841 and #123.

issue_title: We need an API returning the size of the outputs
issue_body: We could store the size in MB in the output table of the database.

issue_title: Find a replacement for icebox
issue_body:
Icebox does not work anymore, since the new calculators are not using postgres. In particular the loss map and loss curve visualization is already broken. We should replace it with some other technology.
Alternatively, we could replace the queries in `openquake.server.tasks.DBINTERFACE` with an equivalent procedure reading from the datastore and writing in the icebox database. However, that would not solve the shortcomings of the current icebox. It would be better to replace icebox altogether and to move the visualization part in the engine server so that we can have a graphical user interface working on the user machines for demo computations.

issue_title: Put openquake.cfg in a proper place when installed via pip
issue_body:
Currently `oq` searches for the `openquake.cfg` in `/etc/openquake` and in `site-packages`. This does not play well with installations from pip because:

1. It is not part of the python package 
2. It cannot be installed in `/etc`
3. `site-packages` folder is the wrong place

To work around this issue in various installers we are setting the `OQ_SITE_CFG_PATH` env var (see https://github.com/gem/oq-installers/blob/master/unix/env/install.sh#L75 and https://github.com/gem/oq-installers/blob/master/windows/nsis-installer/oq-console.bat#L6)

issue_title: Remove hdf5.vstr arrays from the datastore
issue_body: Variable-length strings are buggy in h5py version 2.6: if the array is short, everything works fine, but if the array is long it may fail in mysterious ways, including corrupting the datastore, saving the wrong arrays, etc etc. All that silently. We should at least remove vstr from long arrays.

issue_title: security group rule definition unclear
issue_body:
I'm trying to find documentation on the exact definition of a Security Group Rule. The AWS Service limits for an EC2-Classic SG say that the limit is 100 rules in the VPC User Guide:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonVPC/latest/UserGuide/VPC_SecurityGroups.html#VPC_Security_Group_Differences

I have a rule that has '115 Permission Entries' under the **InBound Rules Count** column in the console. 

A majority of the rules as seen in the console are single public ip addresses to ports 80, 443, and 22. 
```
type protocol port-range        source
ssh    tcp           22          x.x.x.x/32
http   tcp           80          x.x.x.x/32
https  tcp          443          x.x.x.x/32
ssh    tcp           22          y.y.y.y/32
http   tcp           80          y.y.y.y/32
https  tcp          443          y.y.y.y/32
```
The Boto3 EC2 security group resource has an attribute `ip_permissions` which is  
> a list of one or more inbound rules associated with the security group.
 
http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/ec2.html#EC2.SecurityGroup.ip_permissions

However, the number of inbound rules reports as being 3 rather than 6. 
```python
>>> import boto3
 
>>>ec2 = boto3.resource('ec2')
>>>security_group = ec2.SecurityGroup('sg-123456')
>>>len(security_group.ip_permissions)
3
```

the output of `security_group.ip_permissions` groups the rules by the `FromPort` key and aggregates the list of source ips in the `IpRanges` key

```python
>>>security_group.ip_permissions
[
  {
     ...
     u'FromPort': 22,
     u'IpRanges': [{u'CidrIp': 'x.x.x.x/32'}, {u'CidrIp': 'y.y.y.y/32'}]
     ...
  },
  {
     ...
     u'FromPort': 80,
     u'IpRanges': [{u'CidrIp': 'x.x.x.x/32'}, {u'CidrIp': 'y.y.y.y/32'}]
     ...
  },
  {
     ...
     u'FromPort': 443,
     u'IpRanges': [{u'CidrIp': 'x.x.x.x/32'}, {u'CidrIp': 'y.y.y.y/32'}]
     ...
  }
]
```


I'm assuming that the service limit must be evaluating the rules the same way the boto3 client reports and not the console since the security group still allows adding additional "rules" in the console, but I haven't been able to find any documentation that explicitly says that.

This seems to be contradictory to the security group definition found in the [Amazon EC2 Security Groups for Linux Instances](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-network-security.html#security-group-rules) doc:

> For each rule, you specify the following:
>* Protocol: The protocol to allow. The most common protocols are 6 (TCP) 17 (UDP), and 1 (ICMP).
>* Port range : For TCP, UDP, or a custom protocol, the range of ports to allow. You can specify a single 
>* port number (for example, 22), or range of port numbers (for example, 7000-8000).
>* ICMP type and code: For ICMP, the ICMP type and code.
>* Source or destination: The source (inbound rules) or destination (outbound rules) for the traffi

Are distinct rules only calculated based on the Protocol, Port Range, and ICMP Type? 


It seems that the boto3 client and the AWS console report different values.

issue_title: readable() is inconsistent across python versions
issue_body:
I was looking to copy what `readable()` does in order to add a similar `writable()` function, but it has inconsistent behavior across python versions.  `readable()` will tell you if the file was opened readable (or rather if `read()` will actually succeed), whereas the code path in python2 will simply tell you if the fileobj has a read method, without any comment on whether or not read will fail.  Consider:

```
Python 2.7.11 (default, Jan 22 2016, 08:29:18)
>>> from s3transfer.compat import readable
>>> readable(open('/tmp/asdfafgafdsfd', 'w'))
True

Python 3.5.1 (default, Jan 22 2016, 08:54:32)
>>> from s3transfer.compat import readable
>>> readable(open('/tmp/adfasfasdfd', 'w'))
False
```

I would expect us to try, within reason, to have consistent behavior across python versions.

```

pyspotify:

```
commit 68cdfb5fbd405c71c9ffcad5ed2647fde4d9d92e
Author: Stein Magnus Jodal <stein.magnus@jodal.no>
Date:   Sun May 4 23:22:09 2014 +0200

    docs: Release v2.0.0b3

And the problem goes away (e.g. items add near instantly as when using AUR version).

Had a few questions:

- Is it worth me tracking down why this is happening/have you seen this before?

Background info on my setup:
- No local music library or other plugins used besides mopidy-spotify.
- I removed everything except for my config file in ~/.config/mopidy to make sure I didn't have cache problems.
- I'm on Arch.  Packages are fully upgraded.  Was previously running mopidy-spotify from AUR.
- My ncmpcpp version is 0.6.2.  Have not tried any other clients yet.
- I did setup via sudo python2 setup.py develop for all three (mopidy, pyspotify, mopidy-spotify).  Pulled pyspotify because it looks like mopidy-spotify depends on a version not available to pip.

issue_title: Command line errors change the colour of the terminal
issue_body:
**Steps**
1. Run any command that reliably produces a failure.

**Observation**
- The prompt is now black.
- The typed text is now black.
- I am unable to read my text when using a black background.

**Expectation**
- When the command fails, I expect that my console colours do not change.

**Workaround**
- Running the `tput init` command resets the colours of the console.

issue_title: Unspecified options raise AttributeError instead of being `None`
issue_body:
When I run `paver test` on this `pavement.py` file:
```python
# pavement.py
from paver.easy import *


@task
@cmdopts([('verbose', 'v', 'Turn on debugging info')])
def test():
    print(options.verbose)
```

I get the output
```
---> pavement.test


Captured Task Output:
---------------------

---> pavement.test
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/local/scratch/winni/proj/EchiDNA/mandela/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/paver/tasks.py", line 196, in _run_task
    return do_task()
  File "/local/scratch/winni/proj/EchiDNA/mandela/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/paver/tasks.py", line 193, in do_task
    return func(**kw)
  File "pavement.py", line 8, in test
    print(options.verbose)
  File "/local/scratch/winni/proj/EchiDNA/mandela/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/paver/easy.py", line 42, in __getattr__
    return getattr(self.__get_object(), attr)
  File "/local/scratch/winni/proj/EchiDNA/mandela/miniconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/paver/options.py", line 23, in __getattr__
    raise AttributeError(name)
AttributeError: verbose
```

As a consequence I find myself writing this code in order to set missing flags to `None`:
```python
if not hasattr(options, 'verbose'):
    options.verbose = None
```

This seems unnecessary.  Is this expected behavior and am I missing a better way of doing this?  

Also, Is there a good way to specify default values for command line arguments?

issue_title: Tutorial and documentation examples in general missing large amount of context
issue_body:
The tutorial pulls code out from the middle of a test case which is great in that it should stay up to date, but bad in that it's missing a huge amount of context.

It starts out using object properties that are created off stage, and are extremely hard to find even when one goes digging:

"The first step is to create a git.Repo object to represent your repository.

        from git import Repo
        join = os.path.join

        # rorepo is a a Repo instance pointing to the git-python repository.
        # For all you know, the first argument to Repo is a path to the repository
        # you want to work with
        repo = Repo(self.rorepo.working_tree_dir)
        assert not repo.bare
"

As you can see, apart from trivial missing imports for `os`, reference to `self.rorepo` is without context, creating rorepo would be a logical step before this in such a tutorial.

issue_title: Fetch binary file via repo.git.show
issue_body:
I want to fetch a binary file via `repo.git.show`. That works until I want to handle it as binary file.

```python
from io import BytesIO
import git
    
# initialize repository
repo = git.Repo('.')
# use git show to get the content of example.jpg in revision 19e91a
u = repo.git.show("4cb2a02:example.jpg")

b = BytesIO(u.encode('utf-8'))
```
I run into

```
UnicodeEncodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't encode character '\udcff' in position 0: surrogates not allowed
```

Can I fetch the file binary in any way?

This is more a feature request than a bug. I want to apologize if this is not the correct way to handle it.

```
from pypika import Query, Table
accounts = Table('accounts')
q = Query.from_(accounts).select(
     (accounts.revenue - accounts.cost).as_('profit')
)
q.get_sql()

issue_title: Clean up keywords related to alerts
issue_body:
The current alert related keywords are a mess. Biggest problems:

1. Selecting should the alert be dismissed by pressing `Ok` or `Cancel` needs to be done using separate `Choose Ok/Cancel On Next Confirmation` keywords. I think there used to be a technical reason to do it this way, but looking at the current code this doesn't seem to be the case. Much better to just pass this information as a normal argument to keywords dismissing alerts.

2. There are several keywords with same/similar functionality. In practice `Confirm Action`, `Dismiss Alert` an `Get Alert Message` all close the alert by default.

3. Strange configuration options and default values. For example, `Dismiss Alert` actually accepts the alert by default and needs to be separately configured to dismiss it.

-------

If I've understood it correctly, there can be three kind of Javascript alerts:

1. An alert with a message and Ok button (`window.alert`).
2. An alert with a message and Ok/Cancel buttons (`window.confirm`)
3. An alert with a message, text input field, and Ok/Cancel buttons (`window.prompt`)

We need to think what kind of a set of keywords are needed to handle these different alerts. The more we can keep backwards compatibility (i.e. keyword names) the better, but the most important goal is making the set of keywords consistent and easy to use.

issue_title: balncedconsumer fails to reown partitions in case of network failurs
issue_body:
Hello, 

We experience from time to time problems with balanced consumer: in case of network failures, some partitions left unconsumed. 
Here is an example, we are running two consumers in a group for a topic with 6 partitions. Normally each takes care of 3 partitions. In this gist: 
https://gist.github.com/tigran-a/c93ca879df25624120d09b15e5019952
you can see, that after connection issues, the consumer "lost" one partition. 
The other consumer "lost" 2 partitions.

If I stop both consumers and start them again, all partitions are used.

issue_title: Stopping BalancedConsumer ends in infinite loop
issue_body:
I noticed today a problem with BalancedConsumer. The steps to reproduce this behavior are:

1. Call consume method (blocking or nonblocking)
2. On another thread get the instance and call `stop` method (For my scenario it was in a signal handler)
3. The consume will never end

I think this happens because of this code:
https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/1.0.3/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L297-L310

`SimpleConsumer` always raises `ConsumerStoppedException` and `BalancedConsumer` always ignores it (`continue` skips the blocking part).

Is this the expected behavior? If not, maybe before calling `continue`, self._running should be check. If you thing it's ok I'd be happy to create a PR for this fix (or think about another one).

Thank you!

issue_title: regionprops hodge-podge of output types - optionally return pd.DataFrame ?
issue_body:
## Description

Currently, `regionprops` provides a list of `skimage.measure._regionprops._RegionProperties`.
These in turn contain the properties,  some of which are tuples of integers, others are `np.ndarrays` and others or scalar types. 

I would argue that a common use case for `regionprops` is to calculate features for subsequent classification of these image regions. For this it would be most useful to have the numerical features in a tabular form, ideally a data frame where the rows correspond to the object labels and the columns correspond to the features. For this, some of the features (tuples, ndarrays) such as for example the moments must be flattened. 
Other features such as `coords` are probably not too useful in this scenario.
  
Currently, I am using some fairly ugly code to loop over the regions, loop over all features, checking each one against a list of which features I want to keep and flattening them according to their data type. 

I assume returning a data frame directly from `regionprops` is not an option as it would introduce a dependency on pandas and would break backward compatibility. But given that pandas seems to be here to stay such a dependency could be acceptable. 
I would argue that a helper function as part of `skimage` that takes the output of regionprops and turns it into a `pd.DataFrame` would be highly useful. This would also adress the issue raised in https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/2755

issue_title: Appveyor doesn't test `--doctest-modules`
issue_body:
## Description
Appveyor doesn't run pytest with `--doctest-modules`. This skips certain tests that we check against for Linux and Mac.

I'm not too sure how to fix this.

My understanding of the appveyor build
1. It builds the wheel.
2. Installs the wheel.
3. Runs the tests outside the source dir.

I think we should get rid of `3.` and just run the these in the source dir since it allows us to reuse the `conftest.py` script we already have for Travis.

That said, on my local machine (and maybe Appveyor will show the same result), the tests are failing due to numpy 1.14

issue_title: package_data is missing for linux wheels
issue_body:
You can see this on our [regression tests](https://travis-ci.org/numenta/nupic.regression/builds/46280804#L606):

    IOError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/home/travis/virtualenv/python2.7.9/lib/python2.7/site-packages/nupic/swarming/jsonschema/jobParamsSchema.json'

The missing json file is declared in `package_data`, but not included in the wheel. Wheels build on OS X seem to have all the `package_data` files. 

The script that creates the wheel and uploads it to S3 is [here](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/ci/travis/before_deploy.sh). The wheel command being used to package is `pip wheel --wheel-dir=dist/wheels .`.

issue_body:
I'm having troubles with the issue https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/2148 
where unittests on my local installation get stuck on some serialization tests. 

Is there a way to: 
- [ ] fail some test after a certain timeout (say 120s)? 
- [ ] continue the tests patrol even on failures and report the summary of failed, succeeded tests? 

This is on the contrary to what we want on Travis (fast failover) but would be useful for local issues. 

eg. `./scripts/run_nupic_tests -u -timeout=120 -continueOnError=1`

issue_title: RFC templates: customizable html templates
issue_body:
Some html templates existing in invenio are still demosite-specific. I can mention here the home page of invenio, and the main frame layout with navigation bar layout at page_base.html.

Such pages pulls always some specific css. On the other hand they are going to be almost always overwritten by an overlay, so the css required by them is going to interfere with overwritten templates. This issue is a topic of the following PR: https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/pull/2028

Html snippets like a search-bar, deposit form, and similar are always welcome to have in invenio, as they have high probability of being reused.

issue_title: views: problem on recognize the content type
issue_body:
Today I found an issue when I was [saving a deposit](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest/blob/master/invenio_records_rest/views.py#L493).
For some reason, firefox modified the `Content-type` from simply `application/json` to `application/json; charset=UTF-8`.
And the server wrongly interpreted it and returned error `415`.
With @slint we checked on `werkzeug` and found that the value inside `request.content_type` is the raw value and the correct one should be [request.mimetype](https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/master/werkzeug/wrappers.py#L1717):

> Like content_type, but without parameters (eg, without charset, type etc.) and always lowercase. For example if the content type is `text/HTML; charset=utf-8` the mimetype would be `text/html`.

See [here](http://werkzeug.pocoo.org/docs/0.11/wrappers/#werkzeug.wrappers.CommonRequestDescriptorsMixin.mimetype).

------------------------------------------------------------------
cc @jirikuncar @lnielsen

issue_title: Problem with evalString
issue_body:
In an element: //document/template/pageTemplate/pageGraphic/drawRightString I have following snippet:

Page <evalString default="XXX"><pageNumber />-<getName id="pageZero" default="0" /></evalString> of <evalString default="XXX"><getName id="lastPageNo" default="999" />-<getName id="pageZero" default="0" /></evalString>

This causes code to error in special.py file at do_eval function. I found out that the value argument passed to the do_eval function contains string: "2-1 of" which does not evaluate. If I remove "of" from XML snippet, everything works correctly and passed value is: "2-1" which evaluates OK.

Correct value passed to the do_eval function in special.py should be the one encapsulated in evalString tag pair, nothing beyond that.

issue_title: Improve support for integer-like types
issue_body:
The type registration system currently only uses `__mro__`, which means it doesn't work with ABCs like `numbers.Integral`. In Python 3, types like `numpy.int64` don't inherit from `int`, so they currently crash if set as sensor values or otherwise used in katcp messages.

One approach is to change the type registration system to work with `isinstance` and to register ABCs instead of concrete classes. That needs some investigation because mypy doesn't work well with the `numbers` ABCs.

An alternative is to modify the sensor class to cast value updates to the sensor's registered type. That would fix the problem of setting a sensor to a numpy integer (but with the side effect that reading back the value gives you a different type of object from what you set), but wouldn't help for other messages.

issue_title: tar: write error during Extract ".../Python.2.7.2.tar.bz2"
issue_body:
I am slated to teach a course (week after next) using kivy, but buildozer (at the moment, and for android) doesn't work.  To test this, I created a new user (so that it wouldn't have any weird thing in his env).  I created the simplest application possible (just displaying a label).  I installed buildozer:
```
pip install --user git+https://github.com/kivy/buildozer.git
```
Then when I do `buildozer android debug`, eventually I can see an issue during `Extract .../Python-2.7.2.tar.bz2` that says `tar: write error`.  The run doesn't stop stop there (it really should), but it eventually fails.  There is no issue with permissions, nor with available space (I can unpack the archive by hand).

Aargh!  I need help :-(  can anyone actually get a git snapshot of buildozer to work?

issue_title: No instructions for utilizing in Arch linux (i686 / x86_64)
issue_body:
**NOTE:** The following commands assume that your user has sudo access. If not, this can be accomplished by "usermod -G wheel {user}" without quotes, as root. It is also necessary to uncomment the following line in /etc/sudoers:
%wheel ALL=(ALL) ALL


**If platform is Arch x86_64 Enable multilib repository**
=========================================
Uncomment the following in /etc/pacman.conf:
[multilib]
Include = /etc/pacman.d/mirrorlist

**Update the package database** 
=======================
sudo pacman -Syu

**If platform is Arch x86_64:**
====================
sudo pacman -S jdk7-openjdk \
python2 \
python2-pip \
python2-kivy \
mesa-libgl \
lib32-mesa-libgl \
lib32-sdl2 \
lib32-sdl2_image \
lib32-sdl2_mixer \
sdl2_ttf \
unzip \
gcc-multilib \
gcc-libs-multilib

If prompted with the following, answer 'y' for both prompts:
::gcc-multilib and gcc are in conflict. Remove gcc?
::gcc-libs-multilib and gcc-libs are in conflict. Remove gcc-libs?

**If platform is Arch x86:**
==================
sudo pacman -S jdk7-openjdk \
python2 \
python2-pip \
python2-kivy \
mesa-libgl \
sdl2 \
sdl2_image \
sdl2_mixer \
sdl2_ttf \
unzip


**Install Cython:**
===========
sudo pip2 install cython


**Install buildozer:**
==============
git clone http://github.com/kivy/buildozer
sudo python2 setup.py install

**DONE!**

From here you should be able to run the buildozer build process.
- buildozer init
- modify the buildozer.conf as necessary
- buildozer android debug serve

issue_title: Occasional segfault on macOS
issue_body:
I'm seeing an intermittent segfault from interpreter.so on macOS, which I suspect may be due to a spurious wakeup from a `pthread_cond_wait` call in `module.cpp`.

Some details (I'm don't have a minimal reproducer yet, but am working on it):

- the segfault occurs approximately one time out of every 20 or so runs of a portion of our application test suite
- the application itself is complex, with many moving parts, including use of PyTables, Qt/PySide, Jupyter and its machinery, etc.
- the crash always comes from one of the `numexpr` worker threads; in all observed cases to date, it coincides with the main thread doing garbage collection (via an explicit `gc.collect()` call)
- the crash occurs in the middle of the test run (i.e., not at process shutdown time, when the Python interpreter is in a dodgy state anyway)
- the code under test, and the test suite, don't actually use `numexpr` at any point beyond importing it. Adding an `import numexpr` to the top of the test suite causes the occasional segfault; removing that import gives us a clean run every time (so far, out of hundreds of runs).
- doing  a `set_num_threads(1)` early on also seems to make the segmentation fault disappear.
- by adding some `printf` calls to the `numexpr` source, it looks as though the segmentation fault coincides with one of the worker threads reaching [this line](https://github.com/pydata/numexpr/blob/8b91a6d0eb4f5ad8a71aceefa17300120272d3c8/numexpr/module.cpp#L68). On successful runs, that line isn't reached by any of the workers (which is what I'd expect, given that `numexpr` isn't actually being used at any point).

I don't know the `numexpr` source at all well, and am only just beginning to grok how it should work, but it's looking to me as though this segfault is the result of a [spurious wakeup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spurious_wakeup) in [this line](https://github.com/pydata/numexpr/blob/8b91a6d0eb4f5ad8a71aceefa17300120272d3c8/numexpr/module.cpp#L59) of code. As far as I can tell, there's no provision for handling spurious wakeups in the module.cpp code.

Can you confirm that the `if (gs.end_threads)` block should never be reached if `numexpr` is imported but unused in a piece of Python code?

I'll append the macOS traceback to this comment shortly.

issue_title: ENH: Add support for EDGAR daily indices.
issue_body:
EDGAR publishes a "full" index, that is an index of all filings in the current quarter, that `pandas-datareader` now supports. Once the quarter ends, its indices are moved to a folder and presented as daily indices. So, this enhancement will support pulling together a historical index for those of us who would like to use historical filings. I'm currently working on building this.

At the moment, I'm not planning on building in document retrieval directly, but I do want to make that reasonably easy. In my mind, a workflow would look like this:

1. Use `pandas-datareader` to pull the EDGAR index for the time period of interest. (This is where `pandas-datareader`'s role ends.)
2. Use a list of CIK identifiers, particular filing types, or another dataset (via merge) to filter down the list of documents that you'd like to retrieve.
3. Use `wget`, `curl`, or whatever to pull the documents.

When I say make it easy, I mean that I'm doing things like making the filename paths consistent in the returned index so that you can just concatenate the server name and the path to have a full link. In older data, the directory paths are missing a directory (presumably because they hadn't named it "EDGAR" yet).

My thinking on keeping document retrieval out is that these should be one-time pulls that we shouldn't cache, and the performance of a dedicated tool for pulling (potentially hundreds of thousands) documents should be far better than our readily-available options. Still, it should be trivial to create a download list from the index we return.

Any thoughts?

issue_title: Non-zero exit codes
issue_body:
I'd like a way to make my CLI exit with a custom, non-zero code when using Python-fire.

One option might be to have a specific value you could return from your Fire function in order to exit with a particular code. This could be accomplished with, say, a simple ExitCode class wrapping an integer.

Possibly a better idea is to have a special exception class, e.g. ExitCodeError, such that raising an instance of a that class (or a subclass thereof), modifies the exit code of the CLI.

issue_title: sentry.contrib.flask is a bit slow
issue_body:
We're switching to Sentry for exception logging and noticed that under the default configuration, `raven.contrib.flask.Sentry` ended up adding A LOT of overhead to basic request handling. 

I've since rewrote a bit of it to do context gathering more lazily, and have noticed some dramatic results. 

Before I pull these changes out into a fork, I'm wondering if anyone would be interested in seeing these upstreamed. 

Here's some graphs:

![discord_api___datadog](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5489149/22719975/0b43e0c0-ed5d-11e6-8359-9500724aa30c.png)

Essentially, we observed a net of 20% CPU increase on our API workers just by using Sentry with the stock config.

issue_title: aggregate operators COUNT and SAMPLE should ignore NULL values
issue_body:
Consider the following query:
```
  SELECT ?x (COUNT(?y) as ?ys) (COUNT(?z) as ?zs) WHERE {
    VALUES (?x ?y ?z) {
      (2 6 UNDEF)
      (2 UNDEF 10)
      (3 UNDEF 15)
      (3 9 UNDEF)
    }
  }
  GROUP BY ?x
```
it should return the following tuples:
```
  2 1 1
  3 1 1
```
as, per [the specification](http://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#defn_aggCount):

    [COUNT] counts the number of times a given expression has a bound, and non-error value

But instead it returns the following tuples:
```
  2 2 2
  3 2 2
```

There is a similar problem with the SAMPLE operator.
I would expect that:
```
  SELECT ?x (SAMPLE(?y) as ?ys) (SAMPLE(?z) as ?zs) WHERE {
    VALUES (?x ?y ?z) {
      (2 6 UNDEF)
      (2 UNDEF 10)
      (3 UNDEF 15)
      (3 9 UNDEF)
    }
  }
  GROUP BY ?x
```
return the following tuples:
```
  2 6 10
  3 9 15
```
but instead I get
```
  2 6 _
  3 _ 15
```
(where ``_`` means NULL).

Here the specification is not as explicit as how to handle NULL values,
but both Virtuoso and Corese give me the expected result, so there seem to be a consensus on the fact that SAMPLE should not return NULL values.

(in fact, when one sampled column contains only NULL values, both Virtuoso and Corese populate it with an artificial 0 value).

issue_title: boost 1.60 shared_ptr registration
issue_body:
I encountered the error 

```
TypeError: No to_python (by-value) converter found for C++ type: boost::shared_ptr<galsim::GSParams>
```

in dozens of unit tests when switching from boost 1.59.5 to boost 1.60.0.  This appears to be related to a change in the way the GSParams shared_ptr gets registered.  Inserting the following line at then end of `PyGSParams::wrap` solves the issue under boost 1.60:

```
            bp::register_ptr_to_python< boost::shared_ptr<GSParams> >();
```

but then I start getting the following warning (at least it's not an error!) back under boost 1.59.5:

```
RuntimeWarning: to-Python converter for boost::shared_ptr<galsim::GSParams> already registered; second conversion method ignored.
```

So probably there's a more elegant solution...

issue_title: replica rewind in a paused state
issue_body:
I've been playing with the cluster that ran out of disk space; during the period before the disk resize replica has managed to create 22 new timelines, failing whenever writing to disk, starting again and repeating the promotion/failure. Somehow it stopped at a certain point; however, this behavior made the replica unrecoverable after we solved the disk space issues, as it fetched the timeline history from the WAL archive and attempted to connect to the recovered original master with the very latest timeline, failing with a message like this:

'highest timeline 2 of the primary is behind recovery timeline 23 on test-cluster.'

Apparently I haven't noticed  initially that Patroni had already tried to run pg_rewind and failed, complaining about a missing WAL segment (on timeline 1) that it couldn't fetch from the original archive. Therefore, I decided to stop the replica to run pg_rewind manually by putting patroni into the pause mode and running pg_ctl -my stop on the replica's data directory. To my surprise, Patroni immediately restarted the replica I stopped.

It looks like there is a bug in the logic that decides whether the replica that is not running while the cluster is in the pause state has to start or not. Patroni starts a replica during the pause mode when it suspects the replica has served as the master right before being stopped as the result of being demoted. The reason Patroni starts it is to apply pg_rewind, and it only does it when pg_rewind can be applied; however, we should not attempt to start the cluster once pg_rewind has already finished (whatever the result it). The penultimate line of the code below (taken from ha.py:1110) looks like a bug to me. I think the condition on rewind_executed should be reversed, namely,  rewind_executed or we don't need it or cannot run it - do not attempt to start the replica.

```
         if not self.state_handler.is_healthy():
                if self.is_paused():
                    if self.has_lock():
                        self.dcs.delete_leader()
                        self.dcs.reset_cluster()
                        return 'removed leader lock because postgres is not running'
                    elif not (self.state_handler.rewind_executed or
                              self.state_handler.need_rewind and self.state_handler.can_rewind):
                        return 'postgres is not running'
```

issue_title: fullname kwarg not honored in jira.add_user
issue_body:
Note: resubmitting this (originally issue https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/issues/70) but it was closed without explanation.

When using the add_user method to create a new user, one of the keyword arguments is 'fullname'. Regardless of what you put here, the "Full Name" in JIRA is always the username. Example:

```
result = jira.add_user(username='your_mama',
                       email='your_mama@mycompany.com',
                       password="mypassword",
                       fullname="Your Mama",
                       sendEmail=False,
                       active=True
)
```
After running this, when you look on the JIRA instance, the "Full Name" is not set to "Your Mama" but rather 'your_mama'.

Seen with v. jira-0.47

issue_title: singleton.py misdesign
issue_body:
Hello

I've just made the same issue to one project (PyBitMessage) that using your idea. The thing I want to talk aboue is file [singleton.py](https://github.com/Bitmessage/PyBitmessage/blob/master/src/singleton.py) with singleinstance class especially.

The design of this class is incorrect because of two things as below:
1. This class has to ensure that lock file is being deleted when app is closed (as far as I understand it). But it won't work. Well, it would, probably, but as it's stated in python doc, it's not guaranteed that `__del__` method will ever called. So it can easily lead to situation when lock file remains in system after app shutdown.
2. There is `try..except` block in `__del__` method which would not work ever because, as stated in python doc, any exception that occurs while running `__del__` method will be ignored and just printed a warning in stderr instead.

Thus, I'd suggest to either make some other method like `release()` that should be called directly or redesign this class\idea.

Thanks,
Constantine

issue_title: Default functions arguments should never be mutable
issue_body:
I found some default arguments being empty dicts, which are mutable. This is wrong as you can't be sure that the dicts will really be empty or not.
More details regarding this issue [here](https://pythonconquerstheuniverse.wordpress.com/2012/02/15/mutable-default-arguments/).
What you should do instead to avoid any bug, is initializing your parameters to None and set them to {} in the function in this case.
For instance, you do it [here](https://github.com/algolia/algoliasearch-client-python/blob/88d30f8f4f943976e5eb282e98200e76194ade64/algoliasearch/index.py#L426-L427) and [there](https://github.com/algolia/algoliasearch-client-python/blob/88d30f8f4f943976e5eb282e98200e76194ade64/algoliasearch/index.py#L303).

issue_title: Glorot initialization not quite right for sigmoids
issue_body:
I think the Glorot initialization is not quite right for sigmoids, and in general for any function that does not satisfy

![codecogseqn 2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/10391785/22168023/f4952236-df37-11e6-9fd9-781b2bf1bca3.gif)

near 0. which is true for the tanh but not for the sigmoid (![codecogseqn 3](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/10391785/22168056/2e838258-df38-11e6-8cf0-9b0bb011cd07.gif))

(See (2) through (7) in [the original paper](http://www.jmlr.org/proceedings/papers/v9/glorot10a/glorot10a.pdf?hc_location=ufi),)

Relu seems to be a special case as well (see [He et al. (2015)](http://www.cv-foundation.org/openaccess/content_iccv_2015/papers/He_Delving_Deep_into_ICCV_2015_paper.pdf)).

One solution would be to mimmick the interface in lasagne (see [here](http://lasagne.readthedocs.io/en/latest/modules/init.html#lasagne.init.Glorot))

issue_title: Datastore query limits are silently truncated by the v1beta3 API
issue_body:
This bug manifests itself in a couple of "fun" ways:

```python
len(list(client.query(kind="Foo").fetch(limit=300)))
```

Will yield `300`, assuming there are `300` or more entities of that kind.

```python
len(list(client.query(kind="Foo").fetch(limit=500)))
```

Assuming there are `500` entities of that kind, will yield `500`. If there are `n` entities where `n > 500`, it will yield `n`.

This all seems to be caused by the `v1beta3` api truncating the limits to `300`:

```python
entities, more, cursor = client.query(kind="Foo").fetch(limit=500).next_page()
```

`len(entities)` will be `300` and _not_ `500` even if there are `500` or more entities of that kind. 

We're currently using the following snippet to work around this issue:

```python

def _all_entities(iterator):
    entities, cursor = [], None
    while iterator._limit > 0:
        xs, more, cursor = iterator.next_page()
        iterator._limit -= len(xs)
        entities.extend(xs)
        if not more:
            break

    return entities, cursor
```

issue_body:
When using the Python google-cloud-pubsub library within the Google Cloud ML Engine v1.7 runtime utilizing Python 3.5, attempting to import the library results in a module not found error. This ticket follows up on an issue opened with Google Cloud Support (Case#15774813) which has been passed along to the ML Engine team as far as I was told.

Notably, not all versions of the library are affected. Known broken versions include 0.32.1, 0.33.1, and 0.34.0. However, version 0.33.0 is known not to be broken. Additionally, it only appears to be this library affected as other third party libraries have no issue. Finally, this issue is not reproducible locally. Using Python version 3.5 in my local environment the below sample works without incident.

Traceback:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/runpy.py", line 184, in _run_module_as_main
    "__main__", mod_spec)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/runpy.py", line 85, in _run_code
    exec(code, run_globals)
  File "/root/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/trainer/task.py", line 1, in <module>
    import google.cloud.pubsub
ImportError: No module named 'google.cloud.pubsub'
```

To demo the problem, the following code can be used. To use, run the Makefile using `make LOCATION=gs://...` configuring the LOCATION variable to a Google Cloud Storage location you wish to upload the package to. You can also use `make clean LOCATION=gs://...` to remove the packaging artifacts and the file from the LOCATION you specify.

./Makefile
```make
.POSIX:
.PHONY: run clean

LOCATION = gs://YOUR_BUCKET_HERE/demo.tar.gz

run: TRAINING_ARGS  = demo_$(shell date +%Y_%m_%d__%H_%M_%S)
run: TRAINING_ARGS += --module-name trainer.task
run: TRAINING_ARGS += --package-path trainer
run: TRAINING_ARGS += --packages $(LOCATION)
run: TRAINING_ARGS += --staging-bucket $(shell dirname $(LOCATION))
run: TRAINING_ARGS += --region us-east1
run: TRAINING_ARGS += --config config.yaml
run: TRAINING_ARGS += --runtime-version 1.7
run: ./dist/demo-0.tar.gz
	gsutil cp ./dist/demo-0.tar.gz $(LOCATION)
	gcloud ml-engine jobs submit training $(TRAINING_ARGS)

clean:
	rm -rf dist/ *.egg-info
	-gsutil rm $(LOCATION) 2>/dev/null
	-gsutil rm -r $(shell dirname $(LOCATION))/demo_\* 2>/dev/null

./dist/demo-0.tar.gz: trainer/* setup.py
	python setup.py sdist
```

./config.yaml
```yaml
trainingInput:
  pythonVersion: "3.5"
```

./setup.py
```python
from setuptools import find_packages
from setuptools import setup

setup(
    name='demo',
    version='0',
    # Known broken versions: 0.34.0, 0.33.1, 0.32.1
    # Known working versions: 0.33.0
    install_requires=['google-cloud-pubsub==0.34.0'],
    packages=find_packages(),
    include_package_data=True,
    description='Demo showing broken import.',
)
```

issue_title: Please follow best-practices for versioning/releases
issue_body:
The [history of this repository](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-gcs-client/commits/master/python) indicates that developers are working from and pushing directly to the master branch.  The only indication of the Python package version is contained within [`setup.py`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-gcs-client/blob/master/python/src/setup.py#L12) and was [last updated in December 2014](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-gcs-client/commit/3a5c7cecbca59567a76be1264d15d45824e69199) to `1.9.15.0`. There have been many commits since there without also bumping the version, which can lead to wildly inconsistent behavior when following[ the instructions to install the package directly from GitHub](https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/python/googlecloudstorageclient/download).

I highly recommend the use of [Git Flow](https://github.com/nvie/gitflow), which will enable proper versioning/releasing through the use of branches, tags, and releases. I also recommend [semantic versioning](http://semver.org/) as a best-practice for versioning software packages.

This library is [documented as being the supported Python client](https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/python/googlecloudstorageclient/) when working with Google Cloud Storage, so following these best-practices will make a significant difference for developers like myself who depend on it.  Thank you!

from troposphere import Output, Ref, Template, Parameter

))

issue_title: Add support for healthCheckGracePeriodSeconds for ECS services.
issue_body:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/service_definition_parameters.html

healthCheckGracePeriodSeconds
The period of time, in seconds, that the Amazon ECS service scheduler should ignore unhealthy Elastic Load Balancing target health checks after a task has first started. This is only valid if your service is configured to use a load balancer. If your service's tasks take a while to start and respond to health checks, you can specify a health check grace period of up to 1,800 seconds during which the ECS service scheduler ignores the health check status. This grace period can prevent the ECS service scheduler from marking tasks as unhealthy and stopping them before they have time to come up.

issue_title: Refactor "config selector" syntax into a target or config merge syntax
issue_body:
Part of exploding for the absence of config rather than the ambiguity of config (#2525) is moving from a model where a `Target` has multiple configs defined (ie, both `ApacheThriftConfig` and `ScroogeConfig`) to a model where it has _no_ configs defined, and they are supplied/selected/merged in by the consumer instead.

One possible implementation would be that the existing case `"src/thrift/codegen/selector:selector@scrooge_scala_config"`,  rather than selecting one of a few configs defined on the `selector` target, would instead indicate that the `src/thrift/codegen/selector` target should be `merge`d with a target named `scrooge_scala_config` (defined at the buildroot).

The syntax would also allow for merging config defined in different locations. `"src/thrift/codegen/selector:selector@configs/thrift/scrooge:scala"` would cause a consume-side `merge` of the `selector` target with a named `scala` config target namespaced somewhere else in the repo.

issue_title: scalac-plugin.xml does not end up at the root of the plugin jar for `scalac_plugin` targets
issue_body:
This is easy to repro on upstream pants, though I'm not sure how to do it directly from a bare `scalac_plugin` target.  First apply this patch:

```
diff --git a/testprojects/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/testproject/scalac/plugin/BUILD b/testprojects/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/testproject/scalac/plugin/BUILD
index 1f0493c..67b4d5d 100644
--- a/testprojects/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/testproject/scalac/plugin/BUILD
+++ b/testprojects/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/testproject/scalac/plugin/BUILD
@@ -9,3 +9,10 @@ scalac_plugin(name='plugin',
     '//:scala-compiler',
   ],
 )
+
+jvm_binary(
+  name = 'plugin-binary',
+  dependencies = [
+  	':plugin',
+  ],
+)
\ No newline at end of file
```

Then run
```
./pants binary testprojects/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/testproject/scalac/plugin:plugin-binary
```

Now note that
```
$ jar -tf dist/plugin-binary.jar | grep scalac-plugin.xml
testprojects.src.scala.org.pantsbuild.testproject.scalac.plugin.plugin/scalac-plugin.xml
```

when scalac and pants both assume that ends up in the root of the jar.

issue_title: Ivy resolves for goals other than compile are inefficient
issue_body: Today the ivy resolve task hacks in a secondary means of resolving and mapping artifacts for use by goals like idea, eclipse, binary and bundle.  Ideally we could hook the resolver in such a way that a normal resolve nets us a full artifact mapping efficiently.

issue_title: Dead Map Jar symlinks don't cause resolve invalidation
issue_body:
When debugging a bootstrap issue, we rm'd files from the ivy cache, but forgot to run `./pants clean-all`. Pants didn't detect that the symlinks into the ivy cache were invalid, and so we got a failure from java for a missing class.

It would be nice if Pants could check that the symlinks point to files before calling out to java.

issue_title: pants test.junit --output-mode=ALL doesn't work
issue_body:
I am going through the Pants documentation at http://pantsbuild.github.io/JVMProjects.html and trying to run the various Pants commands. The test.junit command with --output.mode=ALL option (./pants test.junit --output-mode=ALL examples/tests/java/org/pantsbuild/example/hello::) throws an error:

It looks like we stopped supporting this option at some point. Documentation needs to be updated to reflect this change. 

One more issue: 
Same result with "./pants bundle --archive=zip examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/main" command. It worked fine after I changed bundle to bundle.jvm.

issue_title: Pants requires a git repo with at least one commit
issue_body:
If you follow the instructions from https://www.pantsbuild.org/install.html outside of a git repo, you will first see:
```
fatal: Not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git
```

Then, after running `git init`, you will see:
```
fatal: ambiguous argument 'HEAD': unknown revision or path not in the working tree.
```
...because there are no commits in the repository.

git is not supposed to be a requirement (and wasn't at one point)... not clear when it was re-introduced.

issue_title: Shared futures regularly allocate 3kb
issue_body:
From profiling memory usage on OSX (using Instruments, which has become useful since we switched to the system allocator via `cdylib`), it appears that `Shared` futures very regularly (perhaps all of them) result in a 3kb memory allocation for one of their internal HashMaps. This appears to account for about 1/3 of our allocations.

![3kb-alloc](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46740/41669157-fe93dae2-7465-11e8-8ab2-1b42bde3c72f.png)

I would be happy to include an example of my setup if that would help.

issue_title: postgres10 and wal-e
issue_body:
Hi guys,
Just tried to run wal-e with postgres10 beta3.

It looks like there are some breaking changes in the postgres code:
eg the `pg_xlogfile_name_offset` function has become `pg_walfile_name_offset`

Has anyone done any work to identify all the breaking changes?

EDIT:
I just dug through the code and see someone has already coded this:
```python
            version = cls._dict_transform(psql_csv_run(
                    "SELECT current_setting('server_version_num')"))
            if int(version['current_setting']) >= 100000:
                cls._WAL_NAME = 'wal'
            else:
                cls._WAL_NAME = 'xlog'
```

issue_title: Mechanism for coalescing prometheus metrics from several processes on one machine to a single HTTP server on that machine
issue_body:
Hi,

We run a handful of prometheus-monitored Python processes on our machines. The processes are not workers; they have distinct tasks, distinct metrics, and each is started by systemd.  We would like to export all metrics to a single HTTP server, rather than hosting one server per process (easily achieved by calling start_http_server in each process).

Does a mechanism exist to achieve this, or to get close to achieving it? If not, what is the recommended setup for monitoring multiple processes per machine?

issue_title: Getting the default font for a document
issue_body:
Currently, in most cases if you attempt to determine the default font for a document, that is:

    documents.styles['Normal'].font.name

Very often the returned value will be blank — even though the normal style will definitely have a font specified when the file is opened, and it is located here:

**file.docx/styles.xml**
  ...
`<w:styles ...><w:docDefaults><w:rPrDefault><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Times New Roman" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Times New Roman" w:cs="Times New Roman"/><w:lang w:val="en-US" w:eastAsia="en-US" w:bidi="ar-SA"/></w:rPr></w:rPrDefault></w:docDefaults>

In the same way that there are special attributes for getting and setting the various margins of a Section, it seems that it should be possible to add getters and setters to the Document object for the default font properties.

Not having a very comprehensive understanding of the underworkings of xmlalchemy I am uncertain what steps I would take to enable this feature. I would be happy to write up the functionality if you point me in the right direction.
`

issue_title: Various prison addresses cause usaddress.tag to bail
issue_body:
I work with databases of addresses of prisons, and there are a number of them that trip up ``usaddress.tag``.  Below are the problematic addresses (taken from http://www.dc.state.fl.us/orginfo/facilitydir.html, http://www.tdcj.state.tx.us/unit_directory/, and http://www.bop.gov/locations/list.jsp ).  If there's a more helpful way for me to report/package these failures let me know how.

A few trends:
 - "Unit" is taken as ``OcupancyType`` even when it should sometimes be recipient.
 - Weird Texas road names ("FM", "Spur", "Hwy")
 - Including both PO Box and street address trips things up.
 - Doubly-named streets (such as  700 NE 55th Blvd, State Road 26 East -- "State Road 26 East" is a redundant name of "NE 55th Blvd", but the address as given includes both).

It's an interesting question as to whether the last two (PO Box with street and doubly-named streets) are considered "valid" addresses.  The redundant street info is clearly not needed; but the address as rendered by official sources includes it.  Letters addressed that way would arrive.  So I'd suggest that even though it's weird and *maybe* non-conformant, ``usaddress`` as a tool for handling in-the-wild addresses shouldn't bail out when it encounters them.

issue_title: Minimum browser support
issue_body:
I'd like to concretize the browsers we're willing to support for Kolibri to the following:

- Internet Explorer 10+
- Firefox 35+
- Chrome 40+
- Android browser 4.2+
- iOS 6.0+
- Safari 5+
- ~~Opera~~ (latest ones use the same rendering engine as Chrome)

The versions given above are meant to be negotiated. But we need to commit to supporting certain versions soon as that will determine what external dependencies we'll be bundling in (see [Kolibri Frontend Architecture doc](https://trello.com/c/wlRWKYUe/13-front-end-architecture-high-level-concepts)).

To answer this realistically, we'll need to look at our most important partners and deployments, and see what technologies they're using. Here's a list of people we need to contact:

- FUNSEPA
- Nalanda
- WCH
- UNICEF
- Aflatoun
- ~~World Bank~~ (Premature to contact, according to Liz)
- {{ add more here @jamalex and @jeepurs }}

issue_title: Wrong instantiation of urllib2.HTTPSHandler
issue_body:
Here https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/commit/5820c980f4733606c93149e62e7a6737785eda6d#diff-16683aec6de1b227110863506f6fb267R380

`handlers.append(urllib2.HTTPSHandler(self._ssl_context))` is wrong because HTTPSHandler has `debuglevel` as first parameter

````
 class HTTPSHandler(AbstractHTTPHandler):

        def __init__(self, debuglevel=0, context=None):
            AbstractHTTPHandler.__init__(self, debuglevel)
            self._context = context
````

please correct line 380 to `handlers.append(urllib2.HTTPSHandler(context=self._ssl_context))`

Isn't this a security issue too?

``` 
                values = data[13: 13 + node_length]

issue_title: stream=False is undocumented / Getting build image IDs is undocumented
issue_body: The (different) return value that one gets if passing `stream=False` is undocumented (nor is whether the default `True` or `False`), which is to return a 2-tuple of `image_id, output`. The fact that `stream=False` can get you the image ID of your new image is also undocumented (which perhaps would be useful to have available even if you pass `stream=True`, since until I read the source code for an unrelated reason, I had asked whether it was possible to get the image ID, and since it seemed like the answer was "no", resorted to passing in my own UUID4 to use as the tag and using that).

issue_title: support cache-from flag
Can not pass `fileobj` together with `path` context to `build`
issue_body:
It seems to be impossible to pass ephemeral `Dockerfile` to build command together with build context. Here is command line use case:

    docker build -t . -f-<<EOF
    Lorem ipsum docker file
    EOF

According to the documentation https://docker-py.readthedocs.io/en/stable/images.htmlit should be possible to pass `Dockerfile` as file object, but in current implementation `path` param with context is ignored.

Here is path that fix part of this issue (it don't work when `path.startswith(('http://', 'https://','git://', 'github.com/', 'git@'))`)

    diff --git a/docker/api/build.py b/docker/api/build.py
    index 0486dce..711def2 100644
    --- a/docker/api/build.py
    +++ b/docker/api/build.py
    @@ -133,7 +133,7 @@ class BuildApiMixin(object):
                if not fileobj:
                    raise TypeError("You must specify fileobj with custom_context")
                context = fileobj
    -        elif fileobj is not None:
    +        elif path is None and fileobj is not None:
                context = utils.mkbuildcontext(fileobj)
            elif path.startswith(('http://', 'https://',
                                'git://', 'github.com/', 'git@')):
    @@ -149,7 +149,7 @@ class BuildApiMixin(object):
                            lambda x: x != '' and x[0] != '#',
                            [l.strip() for l in f.read().splitlines()]
                        ))
    -            dockerfile = process_dockerfile(dockerfile, path)
    +            dockerfile = process_dockerfile(dockerfile, path, fileobj)
                context = utils.tar(
                    path, exclude=exclude, dockerfile=dockerfile, gzip=gzip
                )
    @@ -332,7 +332,13 @@ class BuildApiMixin(object):
                log.debug('No auth config found')
    
    
    -def process_dockerfile(dockerfile, path):
    +def process_dockerfile(dockerfile, path, fileobj):
    +    if fileobj is not None:
    +        return (
    +            '.dockerfile.{0:x}'.format(random.getrandbits(160)),
    +            fileobj.read()
    +        )
    +
        if not dockerfile:
            return (None, None)

issue_title: Use of isinstance(event, str) causes errors in python2.7
issue_body:
The use of isinstance(event, str) in In dependencies.py causes an error in python2.7 code using \_\_future\_\_.unicode_literals. This is because unicode is not str in python2.7. 

I'm not sure if this project wants to support python2.7, but the use of the six module indicates that there is at least some interest in doing so. 

If supporting python2.7 and python3 is a development goal then changing all mentions of 

    isinstance(\(.*\), str)

to

    isinstance(\1, six.string_types)

and ensuring 

    from pgmpy.extern import six

is at the top of each relevant file should solve the issue.

issue_title: Versioning
issue_body:
There are two possible ways to deal with versioning:
1. Have different packages for different api version.
2. Allow wrappers to adapt to new versions.

I like option 1 because it keeps resource mapping simpler, but in other hand seems much more work having to create a new wrapper for minor changes. Eg.: facebook's API has 2.3 and 2.4 versions with very few changes.
We should look into how Raml and Swagger deal with it. If they natively support versioning, I'm in favor of also supporting it.

issue_title: Download fails when old style redirects to on demand
issue_body:
When a course page on the old site redirects to the new site, the old_style download attempts to parse it and fails, but not raising the exception that causes the on_demand try. An example is the `crypto` course.

```

Applying this banal patch to force the on-demand download works:

```
diff --git a/coursera/coursera_dl.py b/coursera/coursera_dl.py
index a6672b2..6e359e2 100644
--- a/coursera/coursera_dl.py
+++ b/coursera/coursera_dl.py
@@ -1048,12 +1048,7 @@ def download_class(args, class_name):
 
     Returns True if the class appears completed.
     """
-    try:
-        logging.debug('Downloading old style class %s', class_name)
-        return download_old_style_class(args, class_name)
-    except ClassNotFound:
-        logging.debug('Downloading new style (on demand) class %s', class_name)
-        return download_on_demand_class(args, class_name)
+    return download_on_demand_class(args, class_name)
 
 
 def main():

issue_title: Vocabulary contains all words from train, dev, and test data?
issue_body: Hi, I was looking at the code for `train` command and noticed that the vocabulary is built from all datasets (train, dev, test). Is this intentional? I am asking because I thought the common thing to do is to build vocabulary from train set only so that words in dev/test set that does not occur in train set are converted into `<unk>` and given a single embedding. Building vocabulary from all three sets means that there will be no unknown words in dev/test set (assuming `min_count=1`).

issue_title: Enable mypy on tests
issue_body: After #1232, we've [disabled running mypy](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/mypy.ini) on our tests (and before #1232, we weren't running them on tests, anyway).  It looks like [there aren't than many issues](https://gist.github.com/nelson-liu/f94a96265973a390066f45399718fe5e), and fixing them and re-enabling mypy on the tests is probably worth it.

issue_title: Faster MSD calculations
issue_body:
I found a Python implementation to compute MSD with FFT which seems faster than conventional MSD algorithm because the algorithm is `O(N*log(N))`.

I did a quick and small benchmark here : https://github.com/hadim/public_notebooks/blob/master/quick_msd_calculations/quick_msd_calculations.ipynb

Two things : 

- the returned `DataFrame` is less detailled than in `tp.msd`.

- the msd array is sliced at the end of the function to take into account `max_lagtime` parameter which is really not efficient ! I didn't find a way to integrate it in the algorithm and I don't know wether it's possible.

Would you be interested by a PR to integrate it into trackpy ?

If you do, we also need to discuss how to inegrate it properly with `tp.imsd` (maybe with a `fft=False` default parameter ?).

issue_title: Roadmap?
issue_body:
Is this meant for public use or is this an internal project at the moment? If it's meant for the public, there appear to be a few things hindering adoption of this library. It would be great to have some clarity on the future of this project.

# Test failures
There are many test failures related to pyxb contracts, which I'm not familiar with. There are lots of references to "C:\\Users\\krgupta\\Desktop\\", so I'm assuming this is part of the problem? I submitted #55 to at least correct the README, so the test instructions will actually run the tests, but the tests fail even on master so my changes don't appear to be the culprit (tested under Python 2.7.11).

# Python 3 Support
The README says "We'll be ensuring this SDK is compatible with Python 2.6+, 3.2+ and PyPy". However, this library is not compatible with Python 3.x. I've submitted #54 to fix usages of `print` as a statement (rather than a function) but there appears to be many other significant issues standing in the way of full Python 3 support. #56 is my attempt to start adding support for Python 3.4+ (3.2 is no longer supported as of February 2016 and 3.3 is only supported until September 2017, so it's questionable whether to support that version now).

# JSON API
Are there any plans to introduce support for the JSON API?

          ADMIN_RUNNING=$(docker inspect --format="{{ .State.Running }}" deis-store-admin)

            echo "deis-store-admin container is required for graceful shutdown"
            exit 2
          fi
          set -e -x -o pipefail
          # determine osd id
          CURRENT_STATUS=$(/usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph health | awk '{print $1}')
          OSD_HOSTS=($(/usr/bin/etcdctl ls /deis/store/hosts/| awk -F'/' '{print $5}'))

            PUBLIC_IP=$(fleetctl list-machines -fields="machine,ip" -full -no-legend| grep `cat /etc/machine-id` | awk '{print $2}')

            fi

          # if we own an osd and its healthy, try to gracefully remove it
          if [ ! -z "$OSD_ID" ] && [[ "$CURRENT_STATUS" == *"HEALTH_OK"* ]] && [ ${#OSD_HOSTS[@]} -gt "3" ]; then
            /usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph osd out $OSD_ID
            sleep 30
            TIMEWAITED=0
            until [[ $(/usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph health) == *"HEALTH_OK"* ]]
            do
              if [ $TIMEWAITED -gt "1200" ]
              then
                echo "ceph graceful removal timeout exceeded"
                break
              fi
              echo "waiting" && sleep 5
              TIMEWAITED=$((TIMEWAITED+5))
            done
            /usr/bin/docker stop deis-store-daemon
            /usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph osd crush remove osd.$OSD_ID
            /usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph auth del osd.$OSD_ID
            /usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph osd rm $OSD_ID
            /usr/bin/etcdctl rm /deis/store/osds/$PUBLIC_IP
            etcdctl rm /deis/store/hosts/$PUBLIC_IP && sleep 10
            # remove ceph mon
            /usr/bin/docker stop deis-store-monitor || true
            /usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph mon remove `hostname -f` # fixme
            /usr/bin/docker stop deis-store-metadata || true
          fi

          # TODO: remove the next check once etcdctl is using etcd2
          ETCDCTL=/usr/bin/etcdctl
          if ! $ETCDCTL --version | grep -q "etcdctl version 2.0."; then
            ETCD_VERSION=2.0.13
            curl -sSL https://github.com/coreos/etcd/releases/download/v${ETCD_VERSION}/etcd-v${ETCD_VERSION}-linux-amd64.tar.gz | \
              tar zxv -C /opt/bin etcd-v${ETCD_VERSION}-linux-amd64/etcdctl --strip=1
            ETCDCTL="/opt/bin/etcdctl"

issue_title: Cluster restore does not populate etcd keys
issue_body:
When trying to restore a v1.9.1 cluster (Ceph-less), the instructions in http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/backing_up_data/#finishing-up do not successfully populate etcd keys.  I had to recreate domains manually, for example.

We should consider strongly consider adding test coverage for backup/restore in the event of catastrophic failure.

issue_title: Proposal feat(k8s): custom labels per application
issue_body:
The idea is to allow additional labels in the application besides name and type defined with the variable `K8S_LABELS`. For instance `deis config:set K8S_LABELS="content=json,platform=nodejs"`
This labels are appended to the defaults and the new ones cannot override the defaults

issue_title: Inconsistencies in registry s3 bucket creation with ceph-s3 vs stateless platform
issue_body:
Looking at the `etcd` namespace configuration for s3 stateless platform vs ceph-s3 I started to notice some inconsistencies that should be address

Look at the configurations and namespaces at:
https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/registry/templates/config.yml#L78

And then head over to
https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/registry/templates/create_bucket and it is quickly apparent that we are only creating buckets with the `storage-gateway` namespaced keys but still doing `s3` namespaced bucket creation.

That's a pretty odd behaviour all in all. May be best if the script can do either namespace, or even *both* if we want to support that.

#4641 may fix this problem or help clean it up
