issue_title: Proposal: replace sshd inside deis-builder with progrium/execd
issue_body:
In deis-builder, we have sshd running to handle incoming connections from port 2223 (port 2222 is from the router). However, openssh does a lot of things that we do not require. https://github.com/progrium/execd is a much lighter component that seems right up our alley, so let's have a discussion on potentially replacing one with the other.

Things to consider:

 - will this remove the `remote:` string from the builder output once and for all?
 - can it handle connection timeouts from a proxy correctly?

issue_title: App process died at one point, no longer logs to `deis logs`
issue_body:
I have a Twitter bot I use as a test app on my deis cluster. Recently, I noticed that it hadn't tweeted anything for a while, and when I checked on its processes it was listed as `destroyed`. I scaled back up to 1, and noticed that the process was now listed as running but doesn't produce any log output. The app has been in this state for several days and, though listed as "running", isn't doing anything.

I tried destroying the app and re-creating it with the same configuration and name, but when I started up the new app and checked `deis logs`, I was simply presented with the logs for my _old_ application and no new STDOUT output. When the bot starts up, it prints three lines to state it's online and streaming a timeline from Twitter. Or it prints errors if something's wrong. No output means it's not actually running.

I also tried editing the Procfile to change the name of my process from `cmd` to `bot`, but that still doesn't cause the app to actually come up. If I SSH into my cluster using `deisctl ssh router`, I can run `fleetctl list-units` and I _do_ see the container for my app running (it's the first one):

```
core@deis-1 ~ $ fleetctl list-units
UNIT				            MACHINE				        ACTIVE	SUB
dcelis-ebooks_v4.bot.1.service	4733f964.../10.132.127.105	active	running
deis-builder.service		    c14d7014.../10.132.129.119	active	running
deis-controller.service		    67a57db0.../10.132.130.126	active	running
deis-database.service		    67a57db0.../10.132.130.126	active	running
deis-logger.service		        c14d7014.../10.132.129.119	active	running
deis-logspout.service		    4733f964.../10.132.127.105	active	running
...
```

However, I'm not sure where to go from here with debugging. It appears my app's container is up and running with a process, but it's not doing anything and I don't know why. I've since renamed the process back to `cmd` because I like it better and the change didn't appear to do anything. Pushing up a new version and scaling that process type to 1 gives the same results. A container is running (now named `dcelis-ebooks_v5.cmd.1.service`) but producing no output.

Somewhat related: it's perturbing that I can destroy an application, create a new one with the same name, and see the old application's logs in `deis logs`.

issue_title: Import of datetime columns with nanosecond precision
issue_body:
When importing dateframes into a database using `turbodbc`, I had problems with datetime columns 
with nanosecond precision and I found a fix:

Pandas uses nanosecond precision by default, e.g. if you use 
`df = pd.DataFrame({'A': pd.to_datetime(['2017-07-30', None])})`
In `turbodbc_numpy.insert_numpy`, this is not converted correctly in a dataframe import leading to 
error messages like

`message: [EXASOL][EXASolution driver]Feature not supported: Cannot cast from DECIMAL(20,0) to TIMESTAMP (Session: ...)`

I fixed this by adding a further if clause to `_convert_array` within `insert_numpy.py`:

```
    def _convert_array(array):
        dtype = getattr(array, 'dtype', None)
        if dtype == '<M8[us]':
            return array.astype(datetime.datetime)
        elif dtype == '<M8[ns]':
            return array.astype('<M8[us]').astype(datetime.datetime)
        else:
            return array
```

The double conversion -- first to microsecond precision, then to datetime.datetime -- is necessary
because the direct conversion to datetime.datetime doesn't work. (For details, see the discussion
on https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19502506/convert-numpy-datetime64-to-string-object-in-python?noredirect=1&lq=1  starting from "Interestingly, the numpy developers decided ...").

issue_title: Transaction origin and transaction salt in Account Abstraction
issue_body:
### What is wrong?
In account abstraction, now origin of the transaction(`transaction.origin`) is always the sender - `ENTRY_POINT`(`0xffff…ffff`) and it's meaningless in a contract's context. `transaction.origin` should probably be changed from `transaction.sender` to `transaction.target` or if origin of the transaction no longer holds significant meaning in account abstraction(e.g, it's just a contract that verifies signature, pays for gas and then forwards to another contract. A real account of a user may be composed of many contracts.) it probably can be removed.

Also I'm working on removing gas price from transaction format. As discussed in #326 , one option is to add `salt` to transaction format(perhaps adding `salt` for short term and transitioning to EIP232 transaction format for long term?). 

### How can it be fixed

- transaction origin
    - 1.1 change transaction origin from `transaction.sender` to `transaction.target`
    - 1.2 remove transaction origin
    - 1.3 ?
- salt
    - 2.1 add salt to transaction format
    - 2.2 EIP232 transaction format
    - 2.3 2.1 for short term and 2.2 for long term
    - 2.4 ?

issue_title: Refactor JournalDB
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

The current implementation of `JournalDB` works by keeping track of the  *original* values in the database so that when a *revert* occurs, it can restore all of the original values.

This causes a lot of read overhead since every *set* requires reading the existing value.

### How can it be fixed

Lets invert this model.  Instead of tracking existing values, lets instead, avoid writing to the database until a commit occurs.  This means that the journal will now effectively be a cache as well as allowing batch committing of values (this probably also means removing the `BatchDB` implementation since `JournalDB` will be able to do everything that `BatchDB` can currently do.

- On `set` and `delete` operations, the journal should now store the value that was written.
- When committing a checkpoint, all of it's values should be merged into the previous checkpoint.
- When reverting a checkpoint, we can just throw away all of the changes from that checkpoint and all subsequent checkpoints.
- If the final checkpoint is committed, all changes should be written to the underlying database.

issue_title: [RFC] Specify Specification Options
issue_body:
Affects #549

### Draft Configuration Options

* RI = Reference Implementation
* SPEC = Specifications

Those are configuration options for web3.py, which would allow a developer to choose its the strictness in 3 levels:

| ConfigOption | default |  description |
| ------------------------------- | --- | --- |
|  **--strict** | true | enforces strict yellow-paper (any RFC or number available? ) compliance |
| **--dev** | false| development mode, enforces the **actual** `--dev` option behavior of the RI (geth)  |
| **--custom** | false| custom mode, allows for custom-specs placed in a `custom` folder (e.g. middleware which changes protocol/specs) |

(side-note: for #549, only `strict` and `dev` will be implemented. But care will be taken that the provided constructs can easily extended to provide full customization, if wished so by the project leads. )

Thoughts, comments?

issue_title: Convert `setup.py` to use preferred style for dependencies
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

The `setup.py` file is not setup in the way most of our other projects are these days.


### How can it be fixed?

Move all of the extras required to a single data structure [like this](https://github.com/carver/ethereum-python-project-template/blob/master/setup.py#L8-L29).  Make sure to add a `[dev]` extras require for easy development installation.

Also update any docs to match this new pattern.

issue_title: conda install with multiple (more than one) packages fails to list packages
issue_body:
Observe:

```
>conda install pyqt
Fetching package metadata ...........
Solving package specifications: .

# All requested packages already installed.
# packages in environment at C:\Users\heath.raftery\AppData\Local\Continuum\Anaconda3:
#
pyqt                      5.6.0                    py36_2

>conda install qtpy
Fetching package metadata ...........
Solving package specifications: .

# All requested packages already installed.
# packages in environment at C:\Users\heath.raftery\AppData\Local\Continuum\Anaconda3:
#
qtpy                      1.2.1                    py36_0

>conda install pyqt qtpy
Fetching package metadata ...........
Solving package specifications: .

# All requested packages already installed.
# packages in environment at C:\Users\heath.raftery\AppData\Local\Continuum\Anaconda3:
#
```

When calling `conda install` with one package, the package version is listed. When calling with multiple packages, the list is missing. As well as being confusing, this makes writing instructions for installation difficult, since the output cannot be verified if combining individual `conda install` commands.

issue_title: Warn and don't use expired auth tokens
issue_body:
Does an auth token have its own expiry information built-in?  If so, conda shouldn't attach expired auth tokens to any Anaconda Repository instance: doing so, today, will result in `401 Unauthorized` errors even when trying to access public content.

Instead a warning should be issued and, I believe, conda should proceed without the expired token.

On the server-side when there is a request for a public channel or public asset I would argue the auth hand-shake results should be ignored -- the content is public and the request would succeed with no auth token at all.  But that isn't a conda issue, and I've submitted an enhancement request to the Anaconda Server private repo here: https://github.com/Anaconda-Platform/anaconda-server/issues/4152

It is possible that this is actually an issue to be logged to the https://github.com/Anaconda-Platform/anaconda-client repo, but given that I've only seen the behavior when executing `conda`, and that is also how others have seen it (see #3399 and #5055) I think it makes sense, in the first instance, to log this here.

issue_title: --use-local breaks on missing file:/// repos
issue_body:
I've got a system install in /opt (root owned)  I run conda-build (as a local user, not as root) and so the package is written to /Users/hugo/conda-bld/osx-64/.    Then if I try to install the package using conda install <pgname> --use-local, I get an error because file:///opt/anaconda/conda-bld/osx-64 is not a valid url (presumably because I've never built a package there, and so there is no repo there)

If I run conda-build with sudo, then the package is built into /opt/anaconda/conda-bld, and then the problem goes away

My guess is that the fix is that --use-local should be robust to missing local repos?

issue_title: conda-env may update conda while it's still in use
issue_body:
(cc @levy5674, @patricksnape) Issue reported in https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6606#issuecomment-355149329: `conda-env` can update `conda` (either explicitly or by `auto_update_conda = True`) while it's still using it and thus makes the current process get into an inconsistent state (using parts of the old as well as updated `conda`). `conda-env` loops over the different dependency types in https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.4.6/conda_env/cli/main_update.py#L104. issue_title: conda install fails to install package files for env on different filesystem
issue_body:
Consider `conda` 3.14.1 installed under `/home/pauli/tmp/miniconda`. `/tmp` is on a different filesystem (same result is found for any filesystem provided it's different from where miniconda is installed).

I do
```
$ export PATH=/home/pauli/tmp/myconda/bin:$PATH
$ cd /tmp
$ conda create --yes -p $PWD/myconda python=2.7
$ wget http://pav.iki.fi/tmp/asv_test_repo-0-py27_0.tar.bz2
$ conda install -y -p $PWD/myconda asv_test_repo-0-py27_0.tar.bz2
$ ./myconda/bin/python -c 'import asv_test_repo'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
ImportError: No module named asv_test_repo
$ ls -l myconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/asv_test_repo/
total 0
```
However, if I instead do `cd ~/tmp`:
```
$ cd ~/tmp
...
$ ./myconda/bin/python -c 'import asv_test_repo'
$ ls -l myconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/asv_test_repo/
total 8
-rw-r----- 2 pauli pauli 189 Jul 15 17:47 __init__.py
-rw-r----- 2 pauli pauli 354 Jul 15 17:47 __init__.pyc
```
The package is incorrectly installed if the new environment is on a different filesystem than the main conda installation.

issue_title: Support package variants
issue_body:
Packages, notably ones containing C extension modules, can be optimized for different hardware (including GPUs).
Allow conda to track multiple variants of such packages, such that `conda install` could then fetch and install the appropriate variant, depending on a) user requests, or b) based on the specific hardware available.
As a point in case, The TensorFlow package now emits warnings of the form
```
W tensorflow/core/platform/cpu_feature_guard.cc:45] The TensorFlow library wasn't compiled to use SSE4.1 instructions, but these are available on your machine and could speed up CPU computations.
```
letting users wonder how to obtain a variant that has SSE4.1 support, etc.

issue_title: Optional dependencies
issue_body:
This is a mixture of a `conda` and `conda-build` issue. However, I'm going to start by raising it here. We can latter break it up into smaller issues on the relevant repos. For now, I simply want to get the discussion going.

Periodically one has optional dependencies. The initial thought on this was that one could try to build against all dependencies and ship only the required subset (if that would work). Sometimes this works if a package has a GUI add-on requiring Qt. Due to the need for version pinning, this is often unsatisfactory as there is no way to constrain the version of Qt that does get installed later. However, sometimes it doesn't work without considering version pinning (e.g. building `libxml2` support for something).

While one could consider the use of features to help here, generally the view has become that features are extremely unsavory. This involves how they are handled in the solver. Though it also adds another package to handle a problem that may only affect one package in the first place. It would be much better if there was some way we could specify these optional features in the recipe.

Please share your thoughts

cc @msarahan @pelson @ocefpaf

issue_title: `TypeError` when setting a parser in `TabixFile.fetch`
issue_body:
I have compressed and indexed a gtf file using bgzip and tabix and want to use it with python3 and pysam 0.9.1.4:

    > tabixfile = pysam.TabixFile("annotations/all_annotations.gtf.gz")

Fetching seems to work, except that contrary to the example given in the documentation (<http://pysam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#introduction>), entries are not parsed by default:

    > text_fetcher = tabixfile.fetch("MtDNA")
    > next(text_fetcher).contig
    AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'contig'

So I tried to set the `parser` option of `TabixFile.fetch`:

    > gtf_fetcher = tabixfile.fetch("MtDNA", parser=pysam.asGTF)
    TypeError: descriptor 'set_encoding' requires a 'pysam.ctabix.Parser' object but received a 'str'

Is that a bug ? If not, it would be good to give example parser usage in the documentation.

Bonus: How can I build a custom parser that would define other fields than the `gene_id` and `transcript_id` described here <http://pysam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#pysam.asGTF>?

issue_title: [Unhandled exception] TypeError: start_process() got an unexpected keyword argument 'cwd'

issue_title: Consider using black code formatter
issue_body:
Currently, we try to adhere to pep8 and ensure adherence to pep8 (and some other code style rules) by running flake8 on each PR (via Hound). This has the benefit of enforcing PEP8 via CI, but it can be annoying for the person who pushes code: they push the code, and immediately get a whole bunch of drily formulated messages that point out style errors.

In a relatively recent development, a code formatter ala gofmt is gaining a lot of traction in the Python world: [black](https://github.com/ambv/black). Similar to gofmt, it completely ignores the style of the input. Instead, it parses the Python file and rebuilds it from scratch, in a stable way.

The benefit is that the user can completely forget about style. Just write it however you want, and let black reformat it. The drawback (some might call it a benefit) is that black has complete control over the style.

Needed tasks:

 - [ ] add pre-commit hooks to run black before committing
 - [ ] add documentation on used code style to `CONTRIBUTING.md`
 - [ ] add test runner that ensures that running black on a commit results in an empty diff
 - [ ] make sure flake8 doesn't check any style violations (for the rare case where black and flake8 might disagree)

issue_title: Support rolling back from nightly build to public build in `az component`
issue_body:
Whilst it is **highly recommended** to install nightly builds into a virtual environment, many install the nightly build into their global location and over the top of any other non-nightly installation they already have.

Currently, `az component` does not support rolling back to a previous version.

As suggested by @JasonRShaver, @tjprescott, it would be helpful to have support to rollback to the latest non-nightly/public version of the CLI.

A suggestion has been an `az component rollback` command.

issue_title: [ACS] Deleting an ACS instance only deletes the "logical" ACS resource
issue_body: Creating an ACS instance via `az acs create` generates all of the necessary Azure resources (e.g. load balancer, master/agent VMs) in order to support the cluster, however, when you delete it via `az acs delete`, it only seems to remove the ACS resource, and none of its children that were created as a result of the create command. Maybe this is expected behavior, but it seems non-ideal since ACS provides a higher-level abstraction on top of the underlying infrastructure, kind of like how deleting a k8s `Deployment` obejct will also delete the "underlying" `ReplicaSets`, `Pods`, etc.

issue_title: [Discussion] Provide ls/rm command aliases
issue_body:
This is highly subjective, but I feel like it would be a little more "ergonomic", and potentially more idiomatic for some users, to be able to use `ls` and `rm` commands--within the applicable command groups--as opposed to `list` and `delete`. I find myself naturally attempting to type either `ls` or `rm` many times (e.g. `az resource group ls`), due to the muscle memory I've built-up from other CLIs, so I can imagine that providing this kind of "interopable" interface could help some users more easily integrate AZ into their regular workflow. I wouldn't recommend changing what's already there, but I'm curious whether it wouldn't be valuable to provide aliases for some of the core commands, that have pseudo-established naming conventions for some users.

Apologies if this discussion has already been had. I couldn't find an issue when I did a search, so I figured I would just file a quick issue while I was thinking about it.

issue_title: Service Bus Queues: custom properties string values containing quotes are mutated in the received message
issue_body:
String custom properties containing quotes are mutated in the received message. 'This "should" work fine' as a value for a custom property in the input message becomes '\\"This \\"should\\" work fine\\"' in the received message.

Python code to reproduce
```
from azure.servicebus import ServiceBusService
from azure.servicebus import Message

service_namespace = 'YOUR_SERVICE_NAMESPACE'
shared_access_key_name = 'KEY_NAME'
shared_access_key_value = 'KEY_VALUE'
sbs = ServiceBusService(service_namespace=service_namespace,
                        shared_access_key_name=shared_access_key_name,
                        shared_access_key_value=shared_access_key_value)

def test_message(custom_properties):
    out_message = Message()
    out_message.custom_properties = custom_properties
    sbs.send_queue_message('receipt-classification-input', out_message)

    in_message = sbs.receive_queue_message('receipt-classification-input')

    if out_message.custom_properties == in_message.custom_properties:
        print('Custom properties identical when received')
    else:
        print('Expected: ' + str(out_message.custom_properties) + ' got ' + str(in_message.custom_properties))


test_message({'property': 'This should work fine'})

issue_title: QueueDescription.CountDetails.* always come back as 0
issue_body:
Hi,

Bug from a part of the discussion here: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/168#issuecomment-66495789

Using List queues/topics in ServiceBusManagementService do not fill correctly the "CountDetails" object.

After investigation, the problem comes from the namespace in the result:
```xml 
<CountDetails xmlns:d2p1="http://schemas.microsoft.com/netservices/2011/06/servicebus">
        <d2p1:ActiveMessageCount>0</d2p1:ActiveMessageCount>
        <d2p1:DeadLetterMessageCount>0</d2p1:DeadLetterMessageCount>
        <d2p1:ScheduledMessageCount>0</d2p1:ScheduledMessageCount>
        <d2p1:TransferMessageCount>0</d2p1:TransferMessageCount>
        <d2p1:TransferDeadLetterMessageCount>0</d2p1:TransferDeadLetterMessageCount>
</CountDetails>
````

and `CountDetails` is defined:

```python
class CountDetails(WindowsAzureData):
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.active_message_count = 0
        self.dead_letter_message_count = 0
        self.scheduled_message_count = 0
        self.transfer_message_count = 0
        self.transfer_dead_letter_message_count = 0
```

Then, the parser receives as "tagName" a string likes "ActiveMessageCount" but waits for "d2p1:ActiveMessageCount" here:

```python
def _get_child_nodes(node, tagName):
    return [childNode for childNode in node.getElementsByTagName(tagName)
            if childNode.parentNode == node]

```
And the parser finds no children...

I don't think there is a lot of solution....
1- Cut all the namespaces before navigate into the XML to build the object
2- Modify `_get_child_nodes` to try to ignore NS using `getElementsByTagNameNS` if `getElementsByTagName` do not give nodes. For instance in naive pseudo-code:
```python
def _get_child_nodes(node, tagName):
    result = [childNode for childNode in node.getElementsByTagName(tagName)
            if childNode.parentNode == node]
    if result:
        return result
    for ns in get_all_namespace_declared(node):
        result = [childNode for childNode in node.getElementsByTagNameNS(ns, tagName)
            if childNode.parentNode == node]
        if result:
           return result
    return []
```

What do you think? I can try a pull-request with one of these solutions if you think it's correct.

issue_title: Redesigning as pure Python distribution
issue_body:
I would like to discuss the use of non-pure Python package dependencies in `pre-commit`.
In particular the use of C code adds build difficulty and security risk, which I hope can be carefully weighed against the need for it.

**Background**
You could envision two possible designs of `pre-commit`:
1. a pure Python tool that can be used by dev teams in almost any setting, vs.
2. a CPython-dependent tool that requires custom C code, Python header files and C toolchain to install.

This trade-off is of course not particular to `pre-commit`. 
Sure, in Python-based projects, depending on C libraries and certainly on CPython is very common.
For `pre-commit` however, it has more impact than usual: adding `pre-commit` to a CI setup is more difficult in design 2, and I see that as a major use-case (it is for me, in practice).

**Current state**
As far as I found, `pre-commit` currently
1. optionally depends on [`LibYAML`](http://pyyaml.org/wiki/LibYAML) via [`PyYAML`](http://pyyaml.org/wiki/PyYAML); and
2. depends on the CPython header files via [`pyterminalsize`](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pyterminalsize/).

1. LibYAML has not seen maintainance in the last two years and is still at 0.1.6.
It is not widely available from OS packaging (e.g., not on Alpine Linux), and appears to not be installed automatically following the installation/build instructions for `pre-commit`.
Building without LibYAML succeeds though, so this is less of a problem.
2. `pyterminalsize` is @asottile's own, still young library.

**Discussion**
I am wondering, how important is it to keep `pyterminalsize` with [just that small snippet of C code](https://github.com/asottile/pyterminalsize/blob/ad23efea4aff257365262e07b843b154c7ad79da/_pyterminalsize.c) as a dependency? Is it easily possible to take a different approach, and avoid CI containers having to install a C toolchain?

issue_title: Using CONTROL_MODE_TORQUE with the C API
Number of observable symbols in MultinomialHMM?
issue_body:
Hello,

I've been fiddling about with the MultinomialHMM class, and have a few questions:

It seems that the implementation of the model is unable to handle a set of observable symbols that is bigger than the number of states. I haven't found anywhere that this is a limitation of the mathematical model, so it seems a bit like an arbitrary limitation to me. Have I misunderstood something here? Maybe having more observable symbols than states makes no mathematical sense, and is redundant in some way? I honestly don't know and am curious.

The fit function's parameter X has the shape (n_samples, n_features), according to the code comments 
https://github.com/hmmlearn/hmmlearn/blob/master/hmmlearn/base.py#L411
and the API documentation,
http://hmmlearn.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#hmmlearn.base._BaseHMM.fit
If n_features is the number of possible symbols emitted (as in, the size of the set which the model "picks from" whenever a symbol is to be emitted), should the shape really be (n_samples, n_features)? To me, (n_samples, 1) feels like a more reasonable shape, or if multi dimensional symbols are allowed, (n_samples, dim_features) or something similar. The way it is written now makes me think of some system where each sample can be some weighted combination of each symbol.

If someone could clear up my confusion, I would be very grateful!

issue_title: Python process is not killed after shutdown/exit call
issue_body:
**Expected behavior**
When a shutdown/exit call is made by the client, the Python orphan process should be killed

**Actual behavior**

issue_title: Run tasks (fetch urls) inside separate thread
issue_body:
## Long story short

The idea is to have ability to suspend all running/queued tasks for a time
As I understand it, threads can wait for events as long as needed, so i try to run asyncio loop inside a thread class, and then control its state by events.
According to aiohttp docs its good practice to run all requests in one session per app, and if i have single-threaded app, everything works fine.
But if i run same fetch tasks in separate thread with one session for all of them - i recieve the error about `Timeout context manager`
On the other hand, if i change task like in `fetch_per_request()` - then error goes away

(closed connections after thread wakeup are not a problem)

I think that I poorly understand the interaction between threads and asyncio tasks
Can someone clarify it?

## Expected behaviour

Fetch urls content inside event_loop in separate thread

## Actual behaviour

<GetContentThread(Thread-1, started 140570646071040)> start
Exception in thread Thread-1:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/python/lib/python3.5/threading.py", line 914, in _bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "threads_simplify.py", line 23, in run
    self.loop.run_until_complete(task)
  File "/opt/python/lib/python3.5/asyncio/base_events.py", line 387, in run_until_complete
    return future.result()
  File "/opt/python/lib/python3.5/asyncio/futures.py", line 274, in result
    raise self._exception
  File "/opt/python/lib/python3.5/asyncio/tasks.py", line 239, in _step
    result = coro.send(None)
  File "threads_simplify.py", line 29, in fetch_per_thread
    async with self.session.get('http://python.org') as resp:
  File "/home/user/python3.5_proj/p1/env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/client.py", line 626, in __aenter__
    self._resp = yield from self._coro
  File "/home/user/python3.5_proj/p1/env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/client.py", line 208, in _request
    with timer:
  File "/home/user/python3.5_proj/p1/env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/helpers.py", line 680, in __enter__
    raise RuntimeError('Timeout context manager should be used '
RuntimeError: Timeout context manager should be used inside a task


## Steps to reproduce

```

import asyncio
import aiohttp
import threading


class GetContentThread(threading.Thread):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):

        super(GetContentThread, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        self.loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
        self.conn = aiohttp.TCPConnector(verify_ssl=False, limit=0, limit_per_host=5, force_close=True)
        self.session = aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=self.conn, conn_timeout=30)

    def run(self):
        print('{} start'.format(threading.current_thread()))

        asyncio.set_event_loop(self.loop)
        
        # does not work
        task = self.loop.create_task(self.fetch_per_thread())
        
        # work
        #task = self.loop.create_task(self.fetch_per_request())

        self.loop.run_until_complete(task)

        print('session {}'.format(id(self.session)))

    # session per thread
    async def fetch_per_thread(self):
        async with self.session.get('http://python.org') as resp:
            assert resp.status == 200
            return await resp.text()

    # session per request
    async def fetch_per_request(self):
        async with aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=self.loop) as client:
            async with client.get('http://python.org') as resp:
                assert resp.status == 200
                return await resp.text()


t = GetContentThread()
t.start()

```

## Environment

aiohttp (2.0.7)
asyncio (3.4.3)

issue_title: Understanding prepare() RuntimeError on StreamResponse object
issue_body:
I am attempting to build a really simple server endpoint that streams some byte data (supposed to response to a Range request from a client) using an instance of the `web.StreamResponse` class.  I know in the older versions, `start()` is required before `write()`, but is `prepare()` meant to replace `start()` ? 

```
# simple func to provide byte data
def bytesource():
    videofile = open('bytedump.txt', 'rb')
    byteload = videofile.read()
    return byteload[10:2000] 

# basic view intended to provide byte data
async def bytedata(request):
    stream = web.StreamResponse(status=200, reason='OK')

    stream.headers['Content-Type'] = 'text/html; charset=utf-8'
    stream.headers['Cache-Control'] = 'no-cache'
    stream.headers['Connection'] = 'keep-alive'
    stream.headers['Accept-Ranges'] = 'bytes'

    #stream.start(request)
    #stream._start(request)
    #stream.prepare(request)

    msg= bytesource()
    stream.write(msg)

    stream.write_eof()
    return stream

```
If I uncomment out `stream.prepare(request)`, I get back: 

`RuntimeError: Cannot call write() before start()`

If I try `stream.start(request)` I obviously get: 

`AttributeError: 'StreamResponse' objet has no attribute 'start'
`

Finally however, if I try: `stream._start(request)` everything works, and I get my byte data response sent to the client, and everything is ok. 

But clearly, I am not meant to use a private class method here, right? What am I doing wrong or what am I missing in my understanding of how to implement such a view correctly? 

Thank you for any help you can provide.

issue_title: Implement aiohttp.client_exceptions.CertificateError and aiohttp.client_exceptions.SSLError
issue_body:
## Long story short

My idea is implement 2 new exceptions `aiohttp.client_exceptions.CertificateError` and `aiohttp.client_exceptions.SSLError` which has bases `ssl.CertificateError` and `ssl.SSLError` as well `aiohttp.client_exceptions.ClientOSError`. When users catch `aiohttp.client_exceptions.ClientOSError` there is no way to understand is it ssl error, dns error or networking error or whatever else

There is a list of all exceptions and their bases in ssl module

```
<class 'ssl.CertificateError'> (<class 'ValueError'>,)
<class 'ssl.SSLEOFError'> (<class 'ssl.SSLError'>,)
<class 'ssl.SSLError'> (<class 'OSError'>,)
<class 'ssl.SSLSyscallError'> (<class 'ssl.SSLError'>,)
<class 'ssl.SSLWantReadError'> (<class 'ssl.SSLError'>,)
<class 'ssl.SSLWantWriteError'> (<class 'ssl.SSLError'>,)
<class 'ssl.SSLZeroReturnError'> (<class 'ssl.SSLError'>,)
```

## Expected behaviour

`
aiohttp.client_exceptions.CertificateError: Cannot connect to host expired.badssl.com:443 ssl:True [[SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:748)]
`

will raise 

`
aiohttp.client_exceptions.ClientConnectorError: Cannot connect to host expired.badssl.com:443 ssl:True [[SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:748)]
`

```python
import aiohttp
import asyncio

async def main():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        async with session.get('https://expired.badssl.com/') as response:
            return await response.text()

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main())

```

Python 3.6.2
Mac OS X 10.12.6
latest aiohttp master branch

issue_title: aiohttp.ClientSession reconnect
issue_body:
Sometimes servers will disconnect the client on purpose after each request. 

When using aiohttp.ClientSession to do several calls, it means we need to re-create a session for each request, and that defeats the with pattern..

I would like to propose the addition of a "reconnect" option (False by default)

when used, this option will try to reconnect if the previous call disconnected the socket, so this 
code works even if the server disconnects the client after the first call:

```python

async with aiohttp.ClientSession(headers=headers, reconnect=True) as session:
    async with session.get("http://httpbin.org/headers") as r:
       ..
    async with session.get("http://httpbin.org/headers") as r:
      ...
```  

What do you think? Happy to try to implement it of you think it's  good idea!

issue_title: deep learning
Build command broken on Windows
issue_body:
Running `setup.py build` on Windows breaks on the CSS command. This is due to excessive quoting of the command by the `run` function.

[This change](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/commit/f175b2391ae468de39831e65674012c9543b0ccb) introduced an extra level of quoting to all commands. 

On Windows, the `lessc` command list looks like the following:

```
['lessc', '--source-map', "--include-path='E:\\Development\\sccolbert\\notebook\\notebook\\static'", 'E:\\Development\\sccolbert\\notebook\\notebook\\static\\style\\ipython.less', 'E:\\Development\\sccolbert\\notebook\\notebook\\static\\style\\ipython.min.css']
```

Which would be valid if joined with spaces. But with the extra quoting, the command is invalid on Windows:

```
lessc --source-map '--include-path='"'"'E:\Development\sccolbert\notebook\notebook\static'"'"'' 'E:\Development\sccolbert\notebook\notebook\static\style\ipython.less' 'E:\Development\sccolbert\notebook\notebook\static\style\ipython.min.css'
```

The [docs](https://docs.python.org/2/library/pipes.html#pipes.quote) for `pipes.quote` indicate that the quoting is only compatible with Unix shells.

On my machine, doing a simple space-join is sufficient to fix the breakage. But, I'm not sure if this will cause breakage on other platforms.

pinging @jdfreder @minrk

issue_body:
Comments can neither be queried nor set on A or CNAME records.

    If a A or CNAME is created with a comment, the record is created but the comment is not set.

    view="Internal"
)

# A record with TTL set via web interface
example = objects.ARecord.search(connector=conn, ipv4addr="127.1.1.1")

example.__dict__

{'connector': <infoblox_client.connector.Connector at 0x7fe494811fd0>,
 'ipv4addr': '127.1.1.1',

issue_title: Implement a way to chain plugins
issue_body:
There's currently no easy way to write a plugin which leverages an existing plugin. I can think of a few cases where it would be very helpful. For example:

* A `gh_repo` plugin which accepts a simple field like `buildinspace/peru`, builds the github url and the passes on the information to the git plugin via env vars
* A `gh_release` plugin which queries the github API  to find the latest release and the uses the curl plugin to download it.

I can think of two ways to support this:

* `plugin.yaml` has a `pre exe` (which manipulates the environment) and a `chain plug` or `pass plug` or something which specifies the plugin which runs after the `pre exe`
* We could provide another entry point called something like `peru-plugin`. Plugins can call this command to leverage other plugins. However, this is probably not a good idea because plugins could cause infinite loops.

Let me know if you think this idea would be worth pursuing, I'll try implementing a simple poc.

issue_title: Unable to read product with sync plan assigned
Add `realm` field to Location entity class
issue_body:
The [Location](http://nailgun.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/nailgun.html#nailgun.entities.Location) entity does not have a `realm` field. The API docs list this field, and the field is listed as a comment in the class definition itself. However, the field is currently commented out.

The reason is that, when reading a location, no realm information is returned by the server. See [BZ 1216234](https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1216234). However, it may be possible to work around this issue. For example, method [`UserGroup.read`](http://nailgun.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/nailgun.html#nailgun.entities.UserGroup.read) works around an _extremely_ similar issue. Perhaps the solution used in that method can be used here as well.

I am looking for a way to enforce certain tests, usually the tests within a specific class, to be executed in serial due to that they interfere with each other. I could use `-n0` but that would be an overreach - it would apply to the entire test run, not just for a specific class for instance. Would it be possible to just have a decorator directive on the class to 1. instruct xdist to schedule the tests in the class to the same slave as well as 2. instruct them to be run in serial?

If there are other ways of achieving this, I would be interested in that too.

Thanks for a great product.

issue_title: --looponfail runs tests forever when using --junitxml
issue_body:
Running `pytest --looponfail --junitxml=report.xml tests/` results in the following:

- Runs the tests
- Generates the test report file
- File watcher says `# MODIFIED path/to/report.xml`
- Runs the tests
- Generates a new test report file...

I can invoke it without the report, that's fine, but it's annoying because I have the report argument in my tox.ini, so using `tox -- --looponfail` shows this behavior.

Given that the `junitxml` argument knows the file path, it seems like a potential solution here is to explicitly skip changes to that file.

It's likely there's a similar issue for html reports and coverage.

issue_title: Configuration option for a custom event loop policy and/or custom event loop class?
issue_body:
It would be useful when testing an application that uses a custom implementation of `asyncio.AbstractEventLoop` to be able to provide a custom event loop policy and/or custom event loop via a configuration option.

It is of course already possible to set the event loop policy globally with `asyncio.set_event_loop_policy` (e.g. in `pytest_configure`) or to override the `event_loop` fixture so that it returns a loop of the desired class, so perhaps this would be unnecessary.

issue_title: Provide a similar function as expandSubcollections when calling get_collection()
issue_body:
Provide a similar function as `expandSubcollections=true` from the REST api when calling `get_collection()`. When you have hundreds of pools across a couple of vCMP guests, you get an enormous scaling issue. 

The below code would make 101 API calls if there were 100 pools
```python
from f5.bigip import ManagementRoot
mgmt = ManagementRoot('10.190.7.161', 'admin', 'admin')
pools = mgmt.tm.ltm.pools.get_collection()
for pool in pools:
    print(pool.name)
    for member in pool.members_s.get_collection():
         print(member.name)
```
While code like this, would one do 2 calls if it queried for all pool members at once.
```python
from f5.bigip import ManagementRoot
mgmt = ManagementRoot('10.190.7.161', 'admin', 'admin')
pools = mgmt.tm.ltm.pools.get_collection(expand_subcollections=True)
for pool in pools:
    print(pool.name)
    for member in pool.members:
         print(member.name)
```

You can currently hack your way around it but it defeats all use for the sdk.
```python
pools = mgmt.tm.ltm.pools.get_collection(requests_params={'params': {'expandSubcollections':'true'}})
for pool in pools:
    print(pool.name)
    for member in pool.membersReference['items']:
         print(member['name'])
```

issue_title: Provide a way to run a script for setting up HTTP / DNS for validation
issue_body:
While a lot of sites can get by using the plug-ins shipped by letsencrypt (both existing and future ones), there will always be sites that require custom operations for provisioning the resources required for validation (e.g. files on a web server for Simple HTTP validation, DNS records for DNS validation). The easiest and most flexible way to automate this is running a user-provided script that gets passed the challenge type, identifiers (domains) and some challenge type specific parameters. E.g. for Simple HTTP validation, it could look like this:

`custom-provision-script --type simpleHttp --tls false --path .well-known/acme-challenge/Token1 --content '{ "type": "simpleHttp", "token": "Token1", "tls": false}' --domain example.com --path .well-known/acme-challenge/Token2 --content '{ "type": "simpleHttp", "token": "Token", "tls": false}' --domain example.com`

For DNS validation, it could look like this:

`custom-provision-script --type dns --name _acme-challenge --type txt --content Token1 --domain example.org --name _acme-challenge --type txt --content Token2 --domain example.com`

While those sites could write and use custom Authenticator plug-ins, scripts are easier to deploy. You  can invoke scripts with their full path, whereas letsencrypt plug-ins need to be installed using setuptools.

This has come up before (see e.g. #1015, #1475). While some users could make webroot work for them in combination with FUSE-based file systems (e.g. sshfs) or are satisfied with an interactive solution (using the manual plug-in), this isn't practical for many sites (including mine).

issue_title: TZPATHS missing Solaris path
issue_body: In `dateutil/tz/tz.py`, TZPATHS is set to a list of paths where zoneinfo files might be found.  The path on Solaris is none of them, but `/usr/share/lib/zoneinfo` instead.  For delivery on Solaris, we're patching that path to the front of the list, but perhaps adding that path would be nice for folks getting dateutil to work on Solaris direct from source.

issue_body:
As brought up in the PyLondinium sprint, the README / documentation does not prominently explain how to install `dateutil`. This is particularly important because the PyPI package is actually called `python-dateutil`, which is in violation of the standard convention!

I think we should put "installation" instructions above "Download" in [README.rst](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/README.rst). Something like:

> # Installation
> `dateutil` can be installed from PyPI using `pip` (note that the package name is
> different from the importable name):
> ```bash
> pip install python-dateutil
> ```

issue_title: @override_settings doesn't play nicely with SALESFORCE_DB_ALIAS setting
issue_body:
In attempt to balance both completeness and speed, I have some tests which use a local datastore and some which reach out to Salesforce. I try to keep the Salesforce tests small -- just enough to test all of the required core functionality -- and then rely on the local datastore for testing more complex behavior. (This also helps with API usage limits.)

The natural way to do this is would seem to be with the `@override_settings(SALESFORCE_DB_ALIAS='')` decorator -- use this to set the salesforce db to the remote datastore *only* for connectivity tests, and leave it set to a local datastore the rest of the time.

I was coming up against a strange bug where column Ids weren't being set correctly. After some thinking and sleuthing, I found that at least part of the problem lies with how database alias-checking is done in django-salesforce: there are places where the alias is checked on load, and then never again (ie live changes won't affect it). Thus, any updating of the `settings` isn't going to be reflected (I assume this is copy-by-value).

I'll make up a pull request to demonstrate what I mean by this.

issue_title: Reporting fields errors from whole schema validator
issue_body:
Sometimes validating a field at a time is not enough and you want to validate a combination of fields. The fields could be deeply nested inside your structure. In that case you create a ```validate_schema``` validator and do all checks there. At the end you want to report errors not for the whole schema, but for particular fields. Documentation for ValidationError states that it accepts 

> String, list, or dictionary of error messages. If a dict, the keys will be field names and the values will be lists of messages

We can probably extrapolate last statement that dictionary values could contain not only list of messages, but also dictionary of nested field errors.

But in reality when you raise a ValidationError with a dictionary it is treated as just another error message (which happen to be a dictionary, not a string). Instead this error dictionary should be deeply merged with errors reported by individual fields.

I have an implementation for that BUT there is one test case (tests.test_decorators.TestValidatesSchemaDecorator.test_passing_original_data) which on it's own tests a different thing, but it showcases passing a dictionary to ValidationError that is not a field-to-error mapping, but rather complex error message data structure:

```python
    raise ValidationError({'code': 'invalid_field'})
    ...
    assert '_schema' in errors                                                      
    assert len(errors['_schema']) == 1                                              
    assert errors['_schema'][0] == {'code': 'invalid_field'}
```

I believe, this usage is wrong (since documentation states that error messages should be strings, see quote above), the test should be altered to use simple string as an error message, library should be extended to support getting errors for multiple fields from whole-schema validator.

Here is a sample test of what I'm trying to achieve:
```python
def test_allow_reporting_field_errors_in_schema_validator(self):
    class NestedSchema(Schema):
        baz = fields.Int(required=True)

    class MySchema(Schema):
        foo = fields.Int(required=True)
        bar = fields.Nested(NestedSchema, required=True)
        bam = fields.Int(required=True)

        @validates_schema(skip_on_field_errors=True)
        def consistency_validation(self, data):
            errors = {}
            if data['bar']['baz'] != data['foo']:
                errors['bar'] = {'baz': 'Non-matching value'}

            if data['bam'] > data['foo']:
                errors['bam'] = 'Value should be less than foo'

            if errors:
                raise ValidationError(errors)

    schema = MySchema()
    errors = schema.validate({'foo': 2, 'bar': {'baz': 5}, 'bam': 6})
    assert 'bar' in errors
    assert 'baz' in errors['bar']
    assert errors['bar']['baz'] == 'Non-matching value'
    assert 'bam' in errors
    assert errors['bam'] == 'Value should be less than foo'
```

What do you think ?

PS I understand that deep merging errors have corner cases and I already have an algorithm which IMO does that in a sane way.

issue_title: Ordered deserialization (ordered loading)
issue_body:
I'm attempting so deserialize objects from a JSON data structure to database instances, but I need to rely on one field being serialized first.

For example, consider the `product` definition:
```
{
  "name": "t-shirt",
  "options": [
    { "name": "size", "values": ["small", "medium", "large"] },
    { "name": "color", "values": ["red", "yellow", "blue"] },
  ],
  "variants": [
    { "sku": "red_sm", options: [ { "name": "size", "value": "small" }, { "name": "color", "value": "red" } ],
  ]
```

In my database, this data is reflected using the entity-attribute-value pattern. Serialization into this form is fairly straightforward – with the help of `post_load`. However, when re-ingesting I need to have `options` processed before `variants`, so that my `variants` can reference their `options` instead of attempting to duplicate them.

Is there a way to deterministically invoke deserialization in order?

issue_title: Python 3.5/3.6 Functions with Type Hints are lost
issue_body:
Let's say I have a simple function:

```
import cloudpickle
import typing, inspect


def add(x: int=1,y: int=2 ) -> int:
    return x+y
```

It runs as expected:

```
>>> add()
3
>>> print(typing.get_type_hints(add))
{'x': <class 'int'>, 'return': <class 'int'>, 'y': <class 'int'>}
```

Now if I pickle it, then unpickle the function, I lose the annotation for the typing.

```
>>> f = cloudpickle.loads(cloudpickle.dumps(add))
>>> print(typing.get_type_hints(f))
{}
>>> f()
3
```

So when a method is pickled/unpickled the typing is dropped.  Is there anyway to include typing in the pickling process? It helps with introspection of a user's method when you send it someplace else.

issue_title: Contract db
issue_body:
There are two types of contracts in blockchain.
1. Contracts created by normal addresses
2. Contracts created by other contracts

The first are easy to get from node. Just get every transaction in every block and check if it creates a contract. This takes aproximatellly a day and a half

The second is complicated
You can take every transaction in every block. Check its destination address and check it code. If code is not empty, then this is contract. So you will get all contracts, created by other contracts.
But you will not get any contract, that is interacted with by other contracts only.

The complete solution is to trace every transaction in blockchain and see if it results in contract creation.

Another solution is to directly read LevelDb. Don't know if it is possible.

What way does Mythril use?

issue_title: DISC algorithm modifies input zenith series
issue_body:
Here's the offending [lines](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/master/pvlib/clearsky.py#L360):

```python
    Ztemp=SunZen
    Ztemp[SunZen > 87]=87
``` 

``Ztemp`` references the same object as the input ``SunZen``, so the ``87`` assignment rudely modifies the input object.

This can be fixed by using ``Ztemp=SunZen.copy()``.

Can be closed in the same PR as #6.

issue_title: Create an example being used with mypy/python3.6 enhancements for ClassVar
issue_body:
Per the mypy documentation:
http://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/python36.html#syntax-for-variable-annotations-pep-526

I think it has to be done like this:

```python
import dependency_injector.containers as containers
import dependency_injector.providers as providers
from my_services import MyService
from my_drivers import MyDriver


class Services(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
    my_service = providers.Singleton(MyService, driver=MyDriver) # type: ClassVar[MyService]
```

Though I **think** this might also work:


```
class Services(containers.DeclarativeContainer):
    my_service: ClassVar[MyService] = providers.Singleton(MyService, driver=MyDriver)
```

I don't get completions in Visual Studio Code for the latter, so I don't know if that's actually the way to do it. The mypy docs don't show assignments here: http://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/python36.html#syntax-for-variable-annotations-pep-526 unless it's done with the `# type: ` syntax.

issue_title: Precision of threshold/value in generated c files
issue_body:
Currently, the values in the model are outputted with default precision (i.e. 6 significant digits in general). 
For example if in the saved model the threshold is 
_0.51548230441328713_, then the generated c file will have _data[1].fvalue <= 0.515482_. 
This can have a very significant impact on the model accuracy, where predictions in original model and predictions from generated c code vary drastically.

Generated files should have max available (or parameterized) precision, sth like 
oss << std::setprecision(std::numeric_limits<double>::digits10 + 2);

issue_title: z3 report model unavailable wrongly
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm passing the following constraint to z3py, which is making use of z3. z3 outputs that the model is unavailable. I'm assuming it is UNSAT in z3's view.

However, I am able to solve this constraint using the below set of values. 

Constraint: (i5 + i6) < ((i5 * i8) - (i6 * i8)) , 0 != (i5 * (i13 - i8)) , 1 != (i5 * (i13 - i8)) , 2 != (i5 * (i13 - i8)) , 3 != (i5 * (i13 - i8)) , 4 != (i5 * (i13 - i8)) , 5 != (i5 * (i13 - i8)) , 6 != (i5 * (i13 - i8)) , 7 != (i5 * (i13 - i8)) , ((i0 * i6) - (i7 - i0)) < ((i6 % 963) + (441 * i7))

z3 says that the model is unavailable.

Values solvable: i5 = -1, i6 = 1, i8 = -1, i13 =1, i0 = -1, i7 = 0

Substituting the values into the constraint, we get

(-1 + 1) < ((-1 * -1) - (1 * -1)) , 0 != (-1 * (1 - -1)) , 1 != (-1 * (1 - -1)) , 2 != (-1 * (1 - -1)) , 3 != (-1 * (1 - -1)) , 4 != (-1 * (1 - -1)) , 5 != (-1 * (1 - -1)) , 6 != (-1 * (1 - -1)) , 7 != (-1 * (1 - -1)) , ((0 * 1) - (0 - 0)) < ((1 % 963) + (441 * 0))

=> (further simplifying, we get)
 
(0) < (2) , 0 != (-2) , 1 != (-2) , 2 != (-2)) , 3 != (-2) , 4 != (-2) , 5 != (-2) , 6 != (-2) , 7 != (-2) , (0) < (1)

As you can see, this is indeed satisfiable.

I also try to incrementally test each subconstraint one at a time. The issue seems to be at the last subconstraint ((0 * 1) - (0 - 0)) < ((1 % 963) + (441 * 0))

If I change the % operator to a +, then it works.

This seems like a bug to me.

issue_title: Quantifiers and non-linear arithmetic (followup problem)
issue_body:
Thanks for quickly fixing a bug concerning quantifiers and non-linear arithmetic which I reported!
I downloaded the new version of Z3 today (20 June 2018), compiled it and found that unfortunately it still behaves in a strange way on the following input (similar to my original bug report):
```
(assert (forall ((x Real))
	    	(=> (< x 1.0)
		    (exists ((y Real))
			    (and (>= y 0.0) (<= y 0.1) (<= x (* y y)))))))
```
This formula says that for every real number x < 1 there exists a real number y between 0 and 0.1 (this latter value can be made arbitrarily small) such that x is smaller or equal than the square of y. However, the square of y is at most 0.01 and hence can not always be larger or equal than the given x (which might be close to 1).

If I run Z3 on this input it says "sat", which it should not.
If I use (set-option :produce-proofs true) instead it says "unsat".

issue_title: Respect image metadata
issue_body:
For image media types, they can (optionally) include width and height as part of their metadata. As stated in [the jupyter client docs for `display_data`](http://jupyter-client.readthedocs.io/en/latest/messaging.html#display-data)

> The only metadata keys currently defined in IPython are the width and height of images:

```js
metadata = {
  'image/png' : {
    'width': 640,
    'height': 480
  }
}
```

For us this means respecting the image metadata within the ImageDisplay in [`transformime-react`](https://github.com/nteract/transformime-react). The transform does receive a metadata prop:

```jsx
<ImageDisplay metadata={metadata} />
```

issue_title: Vehicle.commands.goto always treats alt as relative
issue_body:
Vehicle.commands.goto takes a LocationGlobal or a LocationGlobalRelative but either way it assumes the alt value is relative. This means that if you pass it a LocationGlobal and say "goto" it will fly to that position ABOVE the home position (rather than mean sea level). 

Internally we need to detect we're dealing with a global and pass in the associated relative alt.

issue_title: DKPY2: Remove RC override examples
issue_body:
I think it's appropriate to remove the RC override examples in DKPY2.

My primary motivation is not that this is unsafe user behavior (it can be but that's not relevant), but rather that this does not work on Solo, which has its own pipeline for RC controls. As a result, we don't have a generic way of this working even in the case of Python code, so we may want to approach this differently in the future.

For example, the `dronekit.sitl` package can include its own RC functions; `dronekit.solo` could do likewise. But Dronekit core would not include these.

issue_title: Subtypes of string passed as the webtest user are not handled in check_environ
issue_body:
This came up when a section of test code resulted in a Django safe string being passed in as the username.
Webtest check_environ checks explicitly for an exact type match not a subtype match.
The relevant webtest lines are:

METADATA_TYPE = PY3 and (str, binary_type) or (str,)

assert type(environ[key]) in METADATA_TYPE, (
            "Environmental variable %s is not a string: %r (value: %r)"
            % (key, type(environ[key]), environ[key]))

Is there a reason why this isn't assert isinstance(type(eviron[key]), six.string_types) ?

Also this only came up on our Py3 build, digging into that led me to compat.py in django-webtest which Py2 effectively casts the safe string to a regular string:

    def to_string(s):
        return str(s)

But on Py3 leaves it as a safe string:

    def to_string(s):
        if isinstance(s, str):
            return s
        return str(s, 'latin1')

In general I'm not at all sure what that compat file is doing or why.

When submitting a GaudiRun application (and quite possibly others), large files are copied from the shared area into the job specific inputdir. This is a waste of both file space and time. issue_title: remoteSubDir feature for IGangaFile
issue_body:
I would like to see a ```remoteSubDir``` attribute (or alternative) added to ```IGangaFile```, for ```LocalFile```, ```MassStorageFile``` and ```DiracFile```.

If defined this should be used to move files into a relative path on the worker node at the start of jobs.
In addition it should be used as part of the pattern matching for files when the job has finished.

This has been requested a few times (and may even be an open issue on github that I can't find) and should be less than 50 of lines of code in the correct few places.

Adding this feature removes one major sticking point that prevents some power-users from migrating from 6.0.44, hence why I would very much like to see this in 6.1.19.

issue_title: Shared Area persistency rebuild
issue_body:
There was a recent catastrophic loss of shared area persistence for prepared jobs.

Is it worth writing a short script to correctly rebuild the dictionary of shared folders for all jobs in the repo?
(And offer to clean up after obviously lost shared dirs in the past?)

Any user who simply loaded the effected version of Ganga will cause all shared areas to become unaccounted and unlinked simply by loading and closing an effected version of Ganga as this destroyed the metadata dictionary on disk.
(This isn't the same as when we bled empty folders in the shared space but users will potentially lose a _lot_ of storage space due to the likes of `GaudiExec` jobs.)

This is probably best done in a script which is exposed to the GPI and the old `rebuild` methods for the `ShareRef` dropped imo as they feel overly complicated from having gone through them multiple times.
This will not be as painful as it was during 5.x and 6.0 as we now have correct lazy loading of subjobs and a few other optimizations that mean that loading an individual job from disk shouldn't take too long. (Obviously pending #917).

issue_title: Missing a splitter for Vanilla DIRAC users
issue_body:
After trying to help some LSST users in Edinburgh I think we're missing a Splitter which is capable of splitting files on Vanilla DIRAC based upon the location of files at different sites.

It should be possible to make a direct port of `SplitByFiles` from LHCb as it uses sthe `OfflineDiracSplitter` which _should_ be using vanilla DIRAC methods.

I'll try and get around to rebasing this at the end of the month as it should be a relatively quick thing to migrate some functionality to Vanilla DIRAC.

If there is a working splitter for Vanilla DIRAC that I've overlooked feel free to close this.

```python
import pytraj as pt
```

```python
pt.distance(traj, '@C* @N*')
```

* by a list/array/tuple ... of masks
```python
pt.distance(traj, ['@C* @N*', '@H @CA'])
```

* by integer index
```python
pt.distance(traj, [[3,4],[6,7]])
```

Advantage of using distance by mask: use can calculate COM distance.

issue_title: odd and surprising things discovered while writing DNS records
issue_body:
I was adding dns records to my crawler and ran across a few odd things:

```
from io import BytesIO
from warcio.warcwriter import WARCWriter

payload = '''\                                                                                                                                                 
20170509000739                                                                                                                                                 
google.com.10INA172.217.6.78                                                                                                                                   
google.com.10INA172.217.6.78                                                                                                                                   
google.com.10INA172.217.6.78                                                                                                                                   
'''

payload = payload.encode('utf-8')

with open('test_dns.warc', 'wb') as f:
    writer = WARCWriter(f, gzip=False)

    # oddness #1 -- programming error leads to negative Content-Length                                                                                         
    # (error is that dns things are 'resource' not 'response' according to WARC 1.0 standard)                                                                           
    # recommend: raising an exception for negative Content-Length                                                                                                      
    record = writer.create_warc_record('dns:www.google.com', 'response', payload=BytesIO(payload),
                                       warc_content_type='text/dns')
    writer.write_record(record)

    # surprise #2 -- if I don't specify length=, I get a length of 0.                                                                                          
    # recommend: this should just work                                                                                                                         
    record = writer.create_warc_record('dns:www.google.com', 'resource', payload=BytesIO(payload),
                                       warc_content_type='text/dns')
    writer.write_record(record)

    # specify length, this one looks OK                                                                                                                        
    record = writer.create_warc_record('dns:www.google.com', 'resource', payload=BytesIO(payload),
                                       warc_content_type='text/dns', length=len(payload))
    writer.write_record(record)

```

Do you think either is worth patching? Or am I using it wrong?

issue_title: provide a place for testing and development scripts
issue_body:
@jorgeboucas 

In my understanding, any folder that is *not conflicting with the build process* (e.g. `build` or `*egg`) and *not mentioned as a module* in `setup.py` can exist without problems.

The testing example in https://python-packaging.readthedocs.org/en/latest/index.html  is only one of many possibilities. I don't like it (yet) for this project, since there test units are a) defined within module subfolders and b) depend on an additional package `nose` and c) do not comprise build/develop scripts.

Django uses BASE/tests/ and other folders, too, for example. See https://github.com/django/django.

Please provide a folder for such scripts. Thanks.

issue_title: Test Docker Manifest Support
issue_body:
Pulp 2.14 adds support for Docker manifest lists. This change is significant, and it really needs to be tested before 2.14 is released. Some of the testing for this new feature can be automated, but given the lack of Docker clients on our automated systems, some of the testing will need to be manual.

A docker manifest list is a list of docker manifests, and a docker manifest describes the layers of a docker image. A typical docker manifest list will list out one docker manifest per supported architecture, where supported architectures are values like amd64-linux or arm32v7. In addition, there's two versions of docker manifests: schema v2 and schema v1.

New docker clients can natively understand docker manifest lists. When a new docker client contacts the docker hub (or a clone like Pulp + Crane), it will express its support for this new format by sending certain HTTP headers saying so. When an older docker client contacts the docker hub (or a clone), the hub must be capable of downgrading its response on-the-fly. Pulp + Crane do this by pre-emptively contacting the docker hub and fetching all the content that each generation of docker clients might want.

We need to create automated tests that do the following:

* Create a Docker repository, and let it sync content from a repository that has a manifest list with entries for more than one architecture.
* Create a Docker repository, and let it sync content from a repository that has a manifest list with no entry for the amd64-linux architecture.

For each repository, we need the following tests:

* Pretend to be a modern Docker client, and get (or at least list) the contents of the repository.
* Pretend to be an older Docker client, and get (or at least list) the contents of the repository.
* Pretend to be an even older Docker client, and get (or at least list) the contents of the repository.

We also need to manually test this new feature by installing and configuring the official Docker client and ensuring that it can `docker pull` from each repository.

To fully understand the testing that needs to be done, please read https://docs.docker.com/registry/spec/manifest-v2-2/#backward-compatibility

The following Docker repositories may be useful for testing:

* https://hub.docker.com/r/dmage/manifest-list-test/tags/
* https://hub.docker.com/r/dmage/busybox/tags/

This is how my function looks
```
def get_image_embeddings(image_files_list):
    with tf.Graph().as_default():
        config = tf.ConfigProto()
        config.gpu_options.allow_growth=True
        with tf.Session(config=config) as sess:
            NASNET_MOBILE='https://tfhub.dev/google/imagenet/nasnet_mobile/feature_vector/1'
            module = hub.Module(NASNET_MOBILE)

            sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())
            sess.run(tf.tables_initializer())
            
            image_files = tf.convert_to_tensor(image_files_list, dtype=tf.string)
            image_files_dataset = tf.data.Dataset.from_tensor_slices(image_files)
            expected_width, expected_height= hub.get_expected_image_size(module)
            images=image_files_dataset.map(lambda image: preprocess_image(image, expected_width, expected_height))
            image_batches=images.batch(100)
            embs = image_batches.map(module)
            embs = sess.run(embs)
            sess.close()
            return embs
```

issue_title: Certification: missing feature of PARENT_PARTITION_CHANGE
issue_body: For Router 5.5.3, when the original Leader comes back and attaches to the new partition formed by original router, Harness would verify that the statusTLV in address solicit message from original Leader would have value 4 (PARENT_PARTITION_CHANGE) which currently not implemented in OT yet.

issue_title: OpenThread deinitialization
issue_body: When OpenThread is initialized it takes some peripherals. It is not possible to deinitialize the OpenThread stack and use taken peripherals by other modules. It seems to be a good idea to provide termination function that releases OpenThread resources if an application don't use Thread connectivity continuously.

issue_title: Create an otPlat API to handle vendor / platform specific Spinel properties.
issue_body:
Occasions exist where a host needs to send commands that are specific to its platform. Such commands should not be implemented by Openthread but should be handled by code that is technically separate from Openthread but still part of the NCP image.  Because Spinel and ncp_base get first opportunity to parse incoming frames from the host, it becomes necessary for ncp_base to have a way to route vendor specific requests to an appropriate handler.

For this reason it is proposed to create a new otPlat API that ncp_base will call for any Spinel property frames that it cannot handle internally.  Calls to this API shall be configured at build time via a core config variable that shall be disabled by default.

issue_title: Certification: Add HashMAC address get cli
issue_body:
Test Harness requires a cli to get HashMac address which is used as extended mac address during commissioning process.

We can't use `extaddr` instead since Harness gets HashMac address before `joiner start`, so we need to add a new cli `hashmacaddr`.

issue_title: Possible vulnerability to replay attack in LinkAccept handler
issue_body:
LinkAccept handler compares received Response TLV with both mChallenge (generated for multicast LinkRequest) and neighbor->mPending.mChallenge (generated for unicast LinkRequest).  If at least one of challenges matches Response TLV, LinkAccept message is accepted.

IMHO Response TLV should be compared with only one (appropriate) Challenge value and multicast mChallenge value should be invalidated after timeout to make sure replay attack is not possible.

**Contexto**

`pydatajson` es uno de los proyectos de código abierto que como _librería funcional_ consideramos un producto prolijo y acabado.

El código en este repositorio tiene que estar bien documentado, bien testeado y seguir estándares de estilo.

Vamos a formalizar el abordaje de estos aspectos de a poco, de manera que incorporemos al build de Travis algunas de estas reglas.

issue_title: Consider adding optional persistent history
issue_body:
It would be nice if there was an easy way to add persistent history to ``cmd2`` applications.

We would need to decide if this is done for readline history, ``cmd2`` **history** command history, or both.  Perhaps there would be two separate options for making each of these persistent?

We could probably add an optional argument or two (with default values) to the cmd2.Cmd.__init__() initializer.

[This](https://kushaldas.in/posts/developing-command-line-interpreters-using-python-cmd2.html) blog post demonstrates the basics of how to make the readline history persistent:

```Python
import os
import atexit
import readline

history_file = os.path.expanduser('~/.mycli_history')
if not os.path.exists(history_file):
    with open(history_file, "w") as fobj:
        fobj.write("")
readline.read_history_file(history_file)
atexit.register(readline.write_history_file, history_file)
```

For ``cmd2`` **history** command serialization, we could probably just serialize/deserialize the contents of self.history using the ``pickle`` module.

issue_title: Explore eliminating POSIX_SHLEX  and STRIP_QUOTES_FOR_NON_POSIX
issue_body:
cmd2 currently defaults to non-POSIX parsing with shlex and strips quotes before passing args to functions.

The basic difference between POSIX and non-POSIX parsing rules in shlex deals with how quotes are processed. See [https://docs.python.org/3/library/shlex.html#parsing-rules for more details.](url)

Previously it was decided to default to non-POSIX since the quoting rules are more intuitive.
From a maintenance perspective, it would be nice to have 1 parsing behavior for our shell and not try to emulate multiple shells. This is especially true when trying to parse tokens for tab-completion.

Are you OK if I remove these flags and preserve the default behavior?

Thoughts? @tleonhardt @kotfu

issue_title: No transpose option for Eigen arguments/return values
issue_body:
Background:
To store a block of vertices, it is usual to use a Nx3 matrix in row major in numpy and a 3xN matrix in column major in C++ (Eigen). These 2 matrices are the same thing in memory, but pybind11 will automatically transpose in memory my Nx3 row major matrix in numpy to a Nx3 column matrix. I would like to keep my vertices in column major in C++

One way to implement this no transpose option for return value is to add additional enum value to `return_value_policy`. However, since `return_value_policy` values are not flags, if I am not mistaken, a `_notranspose` version for each enum is need.

For arguments, I can just add a notranspose option to `arg`

My questions are
* Am I missing something? Is there an easy way to do this?
* If no, would such a contribution be welcomed? I need this for my project, so I think I will implement this anyways.

issue_title: Multiple inheritance with static factories and pimpl breaks return type
issue_body:
I encountered the following unexpected behaviour. When having the following inheritance relation `Base <- Derived <- DerivedImpl` and a static factory `std::shared_ptr<Derived> Derived::make() { return make_shared<DerivedImpl>(); };` (`Base` has a similar `make`). When `Base` and `Derived` are wrapped (and `DerivedImpl`) is not all works well if `Derived` is not marked to inherit from `Base`. But if the inheritance relation is added then `Derived.make()` returns `Base` instead of the expected `Derived`.

See the attached patch file (of `test_inheritance`) for an example (remove the inheritance relation to get the expected correct behaviour).

[pimpl_error.txt](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/files/601562/pimpl_error.txt)

issue_title: Nested hierarchy test items is hardcoded to standard pytest python/unittest
issue_body:
For a customer, I wrote 2 custom pytest plugins. Each of these plugins contains custom Python classes of `pytest.File`, `pytest.Collector` and `pytest.Item` to create a nested tree of test suites and tests.

When using `pytest-reportportal`, this nested structure is flattened in ReportPortal. When looking at the code, I see explicit references to `pytest.Class`, `pytest.Function` and alike. This limits the nesting only to pytests using the standard `python` or `unittest` plugins.

Please make `pytest-reportportal` agnostic to which plugin(s) generate test items: you should process the tree as returned by any possible pytest plugin.

Note: IDEs like `PyCharm` show the nested hierarcy returned by my pytest plugins correctly.

issue_title: Deliver one-year certificates for HPKP/HSTS/DANE/TLSA compatibility
issue_body:
Hi,

For [HSTS preloading](https://hstspreload.appspot.com/), we need 18 weeks (126 days) and [at least 180 days to be considered A+ on SSL Labs](https://www.ssllabs.com/).
To be useful, the lifetime of a TLS certificate and the private key should be at least the double of the validity period of HPKP, so [at least 120 days](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7469#section-4.1) (2 x 60 days), so the system admin engineers could have the possibility to overcome a serious problem. 
The comfort zone is one year if we keep in mind the necessary time and required testing for the replacement of the private key.
With DANE/TLSA and DNSSEC enabled, each renewal of the TLS certificate by `letsencrypt` requires that every Let's Encrypt user has the time to regularly modify (= each 90 days) its DNS zone to add the new TLSA DNS resource record. The renewal of domain names are - for the most part - made every year, it would be smarter to be synchronized with that.

It's better to deliver one-year certificates to empower users to use the whole TLS stack and not the bare minimum. If you have some really great research papers to keep the 90 days validity by default, you should at least offer the option to generate one-year certificates, because in the Let's Encrypt CA offering, it seems that we are advised to renew - more manually than automatically in the facts - every 90 days the TLS certificate and the private key, and also avoid additional configuration with HPKP, DANE/TLSA and HSTS. Let's Encrypt should be secure by default.

Thanks in advance,
HLFH
