issue_title: Jupyterhub with Active Directory & Shared Folder
issue_body:
Having read through issues https://github.com/jupyterhub/ldapauthenticator/pull/32 and https://github.com/jupyterhub/ldapauthenticator/pull/36, I set out to try hook the LDAPAuthenticator into [realmd](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/realmd/) and [SSSD](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-US/Red_Hat_Enterprise_Linux/6/html/Deployment_Guide/SSSD-Introduction.html) in order to allow Active Directory users to authenticate on Jupyterhub on a CentOS 7 machine, automatically adding missing users; so far this seems to work! As a bonus, I configured a shared directory between the users, to allow them to share notebooks.

@minrk helped me enormously, most of the ideas here came from him. 

Perhaps some of this should be included in the README or elsewhere in the documentation?

## Jupyterhub Config

The `jupyterhub_config.py` should include the following:

```
import os, sys, subprocess                                            
config_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)) 

# Extend the LDAPAuthenticator with a custom add_user method
# add_user.sh should be in the same directory as jupyterhub_config.py
# (mostly by @minrk)

from ldapauthenticator import LDAPAuthenticator 
class MyAuthenticator(LDAPAuthenticator):      
    def add_user(self, user):             
	super().add_user(user)
        script_path = os.path.join(config_dir, "add_user.sh")      
        subprocess.check_call(['bash', script_path, user.name])
                                  
# Use the custom authenticator; no quotes!
c.JupyterHub.authenticator_class = MyAuthenticator            

# see issue #32
# you can also use c.LDAPAuthenticator for all of these (matter of style)

c.MyAuthenticator.server_address = 'myadserver'                   
c.MyAuthenticator.bind_dn_template = '{username}'         
c.MyAuthenticator.lookup_dn = True          
c.MyAuthenticator.use_ssl = False                
c.MyAuthenticator.lookup_dn_search_filter = '({login_attr}={login})'
c.MyAuthenticator.lookup_dn_search_user = 'ldapsearchuser'          
c.MyAuthenticator.lookup_dn_search_password = 'ldapsearchpassword'  
c.MyAuthenticator.user_attribute = 'sAMAccountName'             
c.MyAuthenticator.user_search_base = 'ou=Org users,dc=domainorg,dc=local' 
c.MyAuthenticator.lookup_dn_user_dn_attribute = 'cn'
c.MyAuthenticator.admin_users = {'adminuser'}          
          
c.Spawner.notebook_dir = '~/notebooks'  
```

## Script

In my case, `add_user.sh` looks like this:

```
#!/usr/bin/env bash

user=$1  
userhome="/home/ad-domain/${user}"

# add_user should be idempotent, so we test if the user home directory is there
if [ ! -d "${userhome}" ]; then    

     # Use realmd to add the user
     realm permit ${user}                   

     # Create home directory       
     mkdir "${userhome}"              
     chown "${user}:domain users" "${userhome}"   

     # Add user to the sharedfolder group
     usermod -aG sharedfolder ${user}

     # Create notebook directory and symlink the datalab folder into it
     mkdir ${userhome}/notebooks  
     chown ${user}:domain\ users ${userhome}/notebooks -R  

     # Symlink a shared folder into the user notebook directory
     ln -s /home/sharedfolder ${userhome}/notebooks/sharedfolder
fi     
```

## Realmd and SSSD

For this to work you need to set up realmd, a frontend for SSSD. I follow [the Red Hat guide for doing this](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/7/html/windows_integration_guide/ch-configuring_authentication). The only modification I manually made to `/etc/sssd/sssd.conf`  are to set:

```
use_fully_qualified_names = False
fallback_homedir = /home/ad-domain/%u
```

The former due to a problem with SSSD accepting the AD username format and the latter because I preferred putting all my AD users in one folder (if you don't want this edit `${userhome}` in the script above accordingly).

## Sharedfolder

To make the shared folder work as expected, you need to set permissions correctly, set group ownership of the folder to the sharedfolder group, set the setguid bit of the folder on, and make sure new files will have the right permissions. I did this as follows:

```
# Set ownership to the administrator and sharedfolder group
sudo chown -R folderadmin:sharedfolder /home/sharedfolder

# Set the setguid bit
sudo chmod g+s /home/sharedfolder

# Apply the correct group permissions to the folder
sudo chmod -R g+rwX /home/sharedfolder

# Make sure all new files added to the folder get the right permissions
sudo setfacl -d -m g:sharedfolder:rwX /home/sharedfolder
```

(I am a little fuzzy about this last bit, so if there are errors here please correct me!)

issue_title: ContractDiscovery initialization failed.
issue_body:
## Only For Bugs
``` python
     blockchain_service = BlockChainService(
         privatekey,
         decode_hex(registry_contract_address),
         host=rpc_host,
         port=rpc_port,
     )

     discovery = ContractDiscovery(
         **blockchain_service**,
         decode_hex(discovery_contract_address)  # FIXME: double encoding

```
ContractDiscovery used  blockchain_server as parameter to init. howerver, ContractDiscovery's parementer is node_address as follows:

class ContractDiscovery(Discovery):
    """ Raiden node discovery.

    Allows registering and looking up by endpoint (host, port) for node_address.
    """

    def __init__(self, node_address, discovery_proxy):
        super(ContractDiscovery, self).__init__()

        self.node_address = node_address
        self.discovery_proxy = discovery_proxy

    def register(self, node_address, host, port):
        if node_address != self.node_address:
            raise ValueError('You can only register your own endpoint.')

        endpoint = host_port_to_endpoint(host, port)
        self.discovery_proxy.register_endpoint(node_address, endpoint)

issue_title: tps benchmarking
Units in DDD files
issue_body:
DDD files found in TRiP98 package have header with [g/cm**2] units of FWHM.

Currently we are saving DDD files with data in [cm] (or [g/cm**2] as only water is supported) units, with (wrong) header which says:
```#   z[g/cm**2] dE/dz[MeV/(g/cm**2)] FWHM1[mm] factor FWHM2[mm]```

Header should be consisent with data units.

In the file `ddd.py` from SH12A source there was a line related to units change, from [cm] to [mm].
See https://github.com/DataMedSci/pymchelper/pull/116/commits/c5bc9f1edff195f96c3b4fc72c394886f15b2951#diff-11b22823f3d8993745a6d6d0e130d049R318 

```
# Scale from cm to mm according to Gheorghe from Marburg
FWHM1 *= 10.0
FWHM2 *= 10.0
```

We should figure out which units TRiP98 is using and use them consistently.

The deal is that we used to use 0.8.1 and had following setup:

issue_title: Use django test database when running test
qcodes overhead
issue_body:
In qcodes there seems to be quite some overhead,  both in the `qc.Loop` and in the different objects living on servers. An example script is included below. The output of this script on my system:

```
## server
DataSet: DataMode.LOCAL, location='2016-05-24/12-18-47'
   chan0: chan0
   amplitude: amplitude
started at 2016-05-24 12:18:50
qc.Loop: dt 2.94 (length 2000)
simple get: dt 1.31
## local
DataSet: DataMode.LOCAL, location='2016-05-24/12-18-51'
   chan0: chan0
   amplitude: amplitude
started at 2016-05-24 12:18:51
qc.Loop: dt 0.19 (length 2000)
simple get: dt 0.02
```

For a small dataset of 2000 points the `qc.Loop` takes about 3 seconds! I set all delays to zero, and the mock model does no calculations at all. The same "measurement" with a simple loop and local objects takes hardly any time.

Can we reduce this overhead or am I missing some options than I should set?

``` python
#%% Packages
import numpy as np
import qcodes as qc
import time
from toymodel import MockGates, MockMeter, MockModel

#%% Make toy model

server_name=None
server_name='test%d' % (1000+np.random.randint(1000) )

class MockModelLocal:
    ''' Same as MockModel, but without a server    '''
    def __init__(self, name='Model-{:.7s}'):
        pass

    def ask(self, *query):
        return self.do_query(*query)            

    def write(self, *query):
        self.do_query(*query)            

    def do_query(self, *query):
        query_args = query[1:]
        query = query[0].split(':')

        instrument = query[0]

        param = query[1]
        if param[-1] == '?' and len(query) == 2:
            getter = getattr(self, instrument + '_get')
            return getter(param[:-1])
        elif len(query) <= 3:
            value = query[2] if len(query) == 3 else None
            setter = getattr(self, instrument + '_set')
            setter(param, value)
        else:
            raise ValueError


class AModel(MockModel):
    def __init__(self):
        self._gates = [0.0, 0.0, 0.0]
        super().__init__()

    def gates_set(self, parameter, value):
        pass

    def gates_get(self, parameter):
        return 0

    def meter_get(self, parameter):
        return np.random.rand()

class AModelLocal(MockModelLocal):
    def __init__(self):
        self._gates = [0.0, 0.0, 0.0]
        super().__init__()

    def gates_set(self, parameter, value):
        pass

    def gates_get(self, parameter):
        return 0

    def meter_get(self, parameter):
        return np.random.rand()


#%% Test speed

gates = MockGates('gates', model=model, server_name=server_name)
meter = MockMeter('meter', model=model, server_name=server_name)
station = qc.Station(gates, meter, server_name=server_name)
station.set_measurement(meter.amplitude)


steps=gates.chan0[0:50:.025]
t0=time.time()

issue_title: social_core.backends.facebook.FacebookOAuth2 should have REDIRECT_STATE set to False
issue_body:
I'm new to Facebook Oauth and actually new to the entire Oauth world, so this bug took me very long time to figure out.

Since I'm new I'm forced to use Facebook API v2.10.

When `REDIRECT_STATE` set to `True`, PSA will compose authentication url like following:

`https://www.facebook.com/v2.10/dialog/oauth?scope=email&redirect_uri=http%3A%2F%2Fexample.com%2Fcomplete%2Ffacebook%2F%3Fredirect_state%3D1TIlS0FPkTctrPCQhgf2rdO3bawWvwdB&client_id=[client_id]&return_scopes=true&state=1TIlS0FPkTctrPCQhgf2rdO3bawWvwdB
`

And Facebook will reject such kind of url, gives error message like following:

> Can't Load URL: The domain of this URL isn't included in the app's domains. To be able to load this URL, add all domains and subdomains of your app to the App Domains field in your app settings.

This the callback url configured on redirect URL doesn't allow `redirect_state` inside the URL, renders the authentication url invalid.

I'm not sure if older version of Facebook API allows this, but this is the case I'm encountering, and when I override the setting `REDIRECT_STATE` with a custom backend, Facebook allows me in.

Following is the entire code I'm using now with Facebook API v2.10:

```python
from social_core.backends.facebook import FacebookOAuth2

class FacebookOAuth2Override(FacebookOAuth2):
    REDIRECT_STATE = False
```

And settings:
```python
SOCIAL_AUTH_FACEBOOK_KEY = 'key'
SOCIAL_AUTH_FACEBOOK_SECRET = 'secret'
SOCIAL_AUTH_FACEBOOK_SCOPE = ['email', 'public_profile']
SOCIAL_AUTH_FACEBOOK_PROFILE_EXTRA_PARAMS = {
  'locale': 'en',
  'fields': 'id,name'
}
```

Please verify and fix, cheers.

issue_title: Execute resource create and update steps and step chains in parallel
issue_body:
Due to the way that we've designed the language host, a `RegisterResource` can be assumed to be runnable as soon as the engine sees it. This is because each language host requires that all dependencies of a resource be resolved before ever invoking the engine.

Because of this fact, we can use a very simple scheduler (if you can even call it a scheduler) to schedule steps coming out of `RegisterResource` events immediately on worker threads. Some `RegisterResource` events resolve to multiple steps - those steps can all be executed one after another on a worker thread.

issue_title: [tune] A few differences between the Hyperband implementation and the original paper
issue_body:
Comparing to the original [post](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~kjamieson/hyperband.html), I spotted a few differences (not sure if there are others):

- [s_max](https://github.com/richardliaw/ray/blob/8b85e7bc4aa96af8fb56485702e3490c8dfd2a0d/python/ray/tune/hyperband.py#L75) is currently a fixed value (=5). It should be calculated from max_t (with the definition in the original post) and eta (using `int(log(max_iter)/log(eta))`)

- [n_0](https://github.com/richardliaw/ray/blob/8b85e7bc4aa96af8fb56485702e3490c8dfd2a0d/python/ray/tune/hyperband.py#L79) is calculated differently. The original post rounded (s_max+1)/(s+1) whereas the current implementation doesn't, leading to [bracket sizes](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/31fefa20b746e2ae8d9e9af3c72e61112bcb9725/python/ray/tune/test/trial_scheduler_test.py#L193-L197) that are different from the original post (always an exponent of 3).

issue_title: Segfault when calling ray.put on a large numpy array.
issue_body:
The following fails (on my laptop) with a bus error.

```python
import ray
import numpy as np

ray.init()
x = np.zeros(10 ** 9)

ray.put(x)
```

This may be related to Arrow's lack of support for large arrays. However, even if we don't support it right away we need to throw a reasonable exception.

issue_title: Radio fields with empty choice are not checked="checked"
issue_body:
* Package version: 1.6.1 * Django version: 1.10.6 * Python version: 2.7.9 ### Description: When rendering a ModelForm with a RadioSelect widget for a model field with `null=True`, the null option is not checked even if the default value is set to `None`. This comes from the fact that Django normally converts the field value from `None` to `''` in the `render()` method: 
class Select(Widget):
    ...
    def render(self, name, value, attrs=None):
        if value is None:
            value = ''
        ...
This conversion is bypassed by django-crispy-forms. When the field is rendered, the value for the option is `''`, and the value for the field is `'None'`, so `checked="checked"` is not inserted. 
<input type="radio"{% if choice.0|stringformat:"s" == field.value|stringformat:"s" %} checked="checked"{% endif %} ....
Screenshots and other additional info are not included in this issue as a PR is coming.

issue_title: Should we set up a Twitter account to notify people about new releases?
issue_body: The title pretty much says it all, is this something we want to do? Someone is already using twitter.com/streamlink, and I'm not sure if it's worth our time, but it might be nice to have for new releases. Potential downsides I see are people asking questions that we aren't going to answer because no one is monitoring it, but I guess we could have a disclaimer.

issue_title: Make trio.TaskLocal work in non-async scope
issue_body:
As the Trio documentation rightly says about including a request identifier in log messages:

> Now, here’s the problem: how does the logging code know what the request identifier is? One approach would be to explicitly pass it around to every function that might want to emit logs… but that’s basically every function, because you never know when you might need to add a log.debug(...) call to some utility function buried deep in the call stack, and when you’re in the middle of a debugging a nasty problem that last thing you want is to have to stop first and refactor everything to pass through the request identifier! 

And indeed, task local storage provides a nice solution for retrieving the request identifier :

```
task_info = trio.TaskLocal(conn_id=None)
def log_filter(record):
    if task_info.conn_id is None:
        return

    record.msg = '(conn %d) ' + record.msg
    record.args = (task_info.conn_id,) + record.args
```

But there is a problem: the log_filter needs to be added to every log handler - it's not enough to just add it to the local logger because then it won't be applied all the way down to the call chain, and we can't add it to the root logger because it is not inherited. This is a little ugly, but can be worked around by running something like:

```
     # Add connection information
    for handler in root_logger.handlers:
        handler.addFilter(server.log_filter)
```

..after all logging has otherwise been initialized.

But, there is always going to be that annoying bit of code that tries to log something before the event loop has started, giving us a `RuntimeError: must be called from async context` when accessing task_info.

Thus my question: can't access to the task local object outside of an async context just return the default value? Having to catch a `RuntimeError` when accessing an attribute is nasty.

issue_title: More careful validation of cancel scope deadline values
issue_body:
Right now, assigning to the cancel scope `.deadline` attribute does:

```python
self._deadline = float(new_deadline)
```

so that means that we at least know that `scope.deadline = "asdf"` or `scope.deadline = None` will fail. However, surprisingly, it allows code like:

```python
scope.deadline = "1.2e3"  # a string is a float?1?
scope.deadline = float("nan")  # uh not sure what will happen here
```

We should perhaps validate these values more carefully.

issue_title: Wait for a concurrent.futures.Future
issue_body:
An existing asyncio code base of mine needed a new background task, and I thought, hey, let me see what happens if I try to write that in trio. So I basically have two threads going, one running the asyncio loop, another running trio. Now I want to call from one to the other. Say I want to call into trio, and wait in asyncio.

I can't use `BlockingTrioPortal` for this, because it would block my asyncio thread. Going on level further down, I can see there is ``TrioToken.run_sync_soon``, which seems quite handy, it allows me to say:

```python
async def call_inside_trio(trio_token, f, *a, **kw):
    future = concurrent.futures.Future()
    def call_this_interop():    
        future.set_result(f(*a, **kw))
    trio_token.run_sync_soon(call_this_interop)
    await asyncio.wrap_future(future)
```

Because two threads are involved, I should probably use a threading-based ``Future`` class. Good that asyncio has this handy ``asyncio.wrap_future`` helper that converts the ``concurrent.futures.Future`` object to an ``asyncio.Future`` one.

Now what if we want to go the other way around? Calling a function inside the asyncio thread, and blocking within trio for the result?

Before figuring out the asyncio part, I actually noticed that curio supports a trap that can deal with a threading Future (https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/blob/a3e629da67a67e87cef7d026e5d355e9dd5e3179/curio/workers.py#L69), which I think would be a solution to this problem.

And looking at Trio's ``run_sync_in_worker_thread``, I could imagine myself building something by following along how it uses``wait_task_rescheduled`` (but skipping the part where an actual thread is created, instead resuming once the future result is ready - https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/0db92590aa092b8b9988d8120bf9591f95d77f79/trio/_threads.py#L398). It seems a bit involved though.

I suppose I am wondering if I am approaching this right, if I missed some already existing solution, of if anyone with more experience has a better idea. To be clear, I want do to this:

```python
async def foo():
     return 1

async def main():
     await call_in_async_io(foo)

start_asyncio_thread()
trio.run(main)
```

And specifically, I can implement ``call_in_async_io`` by returning a ``concurrent.futures.Future``, so I need trio to wait on that.

This could also cause issues in cwltoil.py, wherever location is accessed as it is. ```
cwltoil --batchSystem gridengine --outdir $PWD --jobStore $PWD/job --retryCount 0 --logDebug touch.cwl touch.json
```

issue_title: Log ASCII Standard (LAS) Support
issue_body:
### Overview
I am interested in creating a set of plugins (possibly a well-logging suite) to read Log ASCII Standard (LAS) files using the [`lasio` python package](https://github.com/kinverarity1/lasio/).

### Help Wanted
Does anyone have: 

- experience working with `lasio`?
- any ideas on unique ways to visualize well log data? (I'm thinking cylinders with warped radii based on numerical values.
- ideas for types of filters/transformations that might be nice to have for well log data?

This set of features should be straightforward to implement and I will try to get a development branch going soon.

issue_title: Object cache inconsistent after fetch
issue_body:
If I create a new remote, call remote.fetch() and then try to obtain one of the newly-fetched commits by its sha, I get a BadObject exception deep inside gitdb:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/foobar/ggit/ggit/repo.py", line 134, in foreach
    results[i] = fn(items[i], m, *args, **kargs)
  File "/home/foobar/ggit/gg", line 185, in mergepull
    sb = repo.commit(headRev)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/GitPython-0.3.2.RC1-py2.7.egg/git/repo/base.py", line 378, in commit
    return self.rev_parse(str(rev)+"^0")
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/GitPython-0.3.2.RC1-py2.7.egg/git/repo/fun.py", line 151, in rev_parse
    obj = name_to_object(repo, rev[:start])
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/GitPython-0.3.2.RC1-py2.7.egg/git/repo/fun.py", line 89, in name_to_object
    return Object.new_from_sha(repo, hex_to_bin(hexsha))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/GitPython-0.3.2.RC1-py2.7.egg/git/objects/base.py", line 64, in new_from_sha
    oinfo = repo.odb.info(sha1)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/gitdb-0.5.4-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg/gitdb/db/base.py", line 256, in info
    return self._db_query(sha).info(sha)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/gitdb-0.5.4-py2.7-linux-x86_64.egg/gitdb/db/base.py", line 243, in _db_query
    raise BadObject(sha)
BadObject: BadObject: 4634b83087e4744ee980a17a63952412374b1b95

I can work around the problem by calling repo.odb.update_cache() after the fetch but before looking up the commit.

It would be nice if Remote.fetch() did this automatically to ensure cache consistency.
